The today glance is the read-only counterpart to planning you do on the laptop. The schedule itself is built and edited there, on the calendar and across your projects, and the panel is simply that plan, delivered to your hand at the bench.
Today. The count of tasks scheduled for the current day, shown in sky blue.
Overdue. The count of tasks whose date has already passed, surfaced in red so nothing quietly slips.
Coming up. The count of tasks on the near horizon, in amber, so you can see what is arriving before it does.

The amber badge and the Today panel button live in the header of the Notebook tab. The Home tab shows an inline Today card instead (a static list inside the scrollable home surface). If you want the full overlay with stat tiles, go to the Notebook tab and tap Today in the header.

The Today panel is on by default because most people want the quick read. If you would rather not see the Today button on the Notebook tab, you can toggle "Show Today" off in the phone's Settings, and the button and its overlay are removed entirely.
The Today panel opens as a full-screen overlay from the Notebook tab header. Three stat tiles at the top, grouped task rows below, drag handle to dismiss.

The TodayPanel overlay on a phone, showing three stat tiles (Today count, Overdue count, Coming up count), then grouped color-ticked task rows for overdue, today, and upcoming items, with a drag handle at the bottom.

entries.title: View a method on your phone
breadcrumbs: View a method on your phone
headings: Log a variation from the bench
View method on phone (Notebook tab path)
Why the projection is sealed and read-only
The method and your reality stay in sync
bodySnippets: The Methods tab is your full protocol library on the phone, searchable and available offline. Tap any method to read it big and scrollable at the bench. When you change something on the fly, log the variation right there and it lands back on the laptop as a timestamped note on...

Following a protocol at the bench off a laptop screen is awkward. The screen is across the room, the text is small, and your hands are busy. The Methods tab puts your entire library on the phone, sized to read at arm's length. And because real protocols drift, you ran the gel ...
Methods reach the phone in two ways, and both end up in the same big-text read mode.

The Methods tab is the method library for the phone. It shows all your methods with search, type filter chips, and a sort toggle (by Type, A to Z, or Recent). Tap any row to open that method in read mode.
On the laptop, open the experiment and go to its Method tab.
Press View method on phone . This button appears when a phone is paired, so it shows up exactly when it can do something.
The laptop publishes a sealed, read-only projection of the method, and the phone renders it large and scrollable for the bench.
The View method on phone button also appears on the Notebook tab, giving you a shortcut to the focused experiment's method without needing to navigate to it first on the laptop.

The phone renders whichever kind of method the experiment uses, so a structured protocol stays structured rather than collapsing into a wall of text. That covers a PCR program with its thermal steps, an LC-MS method, a compound method, and a plain Markdown protocol. The projec...

When you deviate from the written method, tap Add a variation and describe what you actually did. The phone posts that back to the laptop, where it lands as a timestamped variation note on that experiment's method. You capture the real conditions in the moment they happen, ins...

Offline download. The library is cached on the phone so read mode works at the bench with no signal. A status chip at the top of the tab shows the download state: "Download for offline", "Downloading", a count of cached methods when ready, or "Update available" when the laptop...
Favorites. Tap the star icon on any row to save that method as a favorite. Favorites are stored locally on the phone.

Active-experiment recommendations band. When the laptop has an open experiment, a sky-tinted card appears at the top of the library list (above search results) showing the methods attached to that experiment, with a live green dot. Tapping a recommendation opens the focused ex...

The method is sent as a sealed projection, so the relay passes it to your phone without being able to read your protocol. It is read-only because the method of record lives on the laptop, where version history tracks every change. The phone is the bench-side reading surface, a...

The written protocol is the plan; the variation note is what occurred. Keeping both, with the variation timestamped against the method, is what makes the record reproducible. Pair this with a routed photo of the result and the experiment carries both what you intended and what...
The Notebook tab offers a View method on phone button when a laptop is paired. The Methods tab gives you the full offline library.
The Companion Notebook tab on a phone showing the View method on phone button, which opens the focused experiment's method in the big-text read mode.

entries.title: Data Hub
headings: What Data Hub is and why it exists
The data grid and live summary
Referencing Data Hub in notes and results
Connection to the rest of the app
A real publication page behind the figure
bodySnippets: Data Hub is a free, open-source GraphPad Prism alternative built into ResearchOS. It runs your statistics and your publication figures entirely in the browser, so your data never leaves your folder.

Prism and its peers are graphical front ends over math that is already settled and already available as proven open-source code. The value they sell is the workflow, not the statistics. Data Hub reproduces that workflow on top of battle-tested open-source libraries, so you get...

Everything runs on your own machine, in the browser. There is no server doing the computation, so your raw numbers stay in your data folder the same way your notes and sequences do. The reason this matters is simple. Your data is yours, the math is open, and you can read the e...

Data Hub lives at and is organized like the rest of ResearchOS. Documents are scoped to projects (collections) with optional subfolders, and they stand on their own rather than being born attached to a single experiment, so one workbook can pull together data from wherever it ...

The left rail is the navigator. A collection selector at the top scopes the workbook list to one project or opens it to everything at once. Below it, the Data Tables section lists your tables in a foldered tree. Two more sections, Results and Graphs, hold the analyses and figu...
Like Prism, Data Hub shapes the table to the analysis you intend, so the grid and the available tests match how your data is actually laid out. New table offers eight archetypes.

A Column table is the starting point. Each column is a treatment group and each row is a replicate. The footer shows the mean, SD, SEM, and n for every group, recomputed live as you type, with no separate summarize step.

An XY table pairs an X column with one or more Y columns, one observation per row. This is the shape for dose-response curves, time courses, and standard curves, and it unlocks correlation, linear regression, and fitted curves.

A Grouped table records two factors at once. Each row is a level of the row factor, each column group is a level of the column factor, and replicate subcolumns sit under each group. That replication is what lets a two-way ANOVA estimate the interaction between the two factors.

A Survival table records time-to-event data. Each row is a subject with a follow-up time, an event indicator (1 if the event happened, 0 if the subject was censored), and an optional group label so arms can be compared with a Kaplan-Meier curve and the log-rank test.

A Contingency table holds counts in a grid of two categorical factors. It runs the chi-square test of independence, and for a 2x2 table it adds Fisher's exact test with the relative risk and the odds ratio.

A Nested table records technical replicates nested inside biological replicates, for example cells within a mouse or mice within a treatment. It runs the nested t-test for two top-level groups and the nested one-way ANOVA for three or more, so technical replicates are not trea...

A Parts of whole table describes the composition of a single whole, a category label and a value per slice. It draws pie, donut, and 100-percent stacked-bar figures, each slice as its percent of the total.
An Info sheet is a documentation table. It holds notes and constants that describe a dataset so the context travels with the data. It runs no analysis and draws no figure.

The grid is where you enter raw replicates. You only ever enter the numbers once. The summary statistics, every analysis, and every figure all read from the same cells, so an edit to a single replicate flows through to the footer, the results sheet, and any graph immediately. ...

Sometimes the raw numbers are not the numbers you analyze. Data Hub can produce a new table that is computed from an existing one, so the cleaning step is explicit and repeatable rather than a one-off edit you cannot retrace. Five transforms ship: a general transform that appl...

A transform creates a derived table that keeps a live link to its source. When you open the derived table, it recomputes from the source's current content rather than from a saved snapshot, so an edit to the original flows through automatically and the derived table can never ...
New analysis offers only the tests that are valid for the open table, so you never pick a test the data cannot support. The set is broad and grows with the table type.

From a Column table: unpaired and paired t-tests, one-way ANOVA with Tukey comparisons, and their rank-based counterparts (Mann-Whitney U, Wilcoxon signed-rank, Kruskal-Wallis); repeated-measures ANOVA and its mixed-model cousin (a random-intercept linear mixed model) for with...

From an XY table: Pearson and Spearman correlation, linear regression, simple logistic regression, the ROC curve with AUC, dose-response curve fitting (4PL and 5PL) with model comparison by AICc and an extra-sum-of-squares F test, and global (shared-parameter) fitting across s...

From the design-specific tables: two-way ANOVA from a Grouped table; Kaplan-Meier survival with the log-rank and Gehan-Breslow-Wilcoxon tests plus Cox proportional-hazards regression from a Survival table; the chi-square and Fisher exact tests from a Contingency table; and the...

Every result leads with a plain-language verdict, a sentence that states the practical takeaway before the numbers. Does it differ, which way, by how much. The full statistics table follows, laid out the way a methods section reads it.
The same results-sheet pattern carries every analysis, from the everyday comparisons to the pharmacology fits and the survival models.

Every analysis carries a Show the code toggle that reveals the exact open-source Python (scipy, statsmodels, lifelines) that reproduces it, with your real group names and values baked in. Paste it into a notebook and you get the same numbers. This is the answer to the question...

The same proof extends to the picture. Every figure carries its own code export, the matplotlib that redraws it from the same group names and values the on-screen figure used, so the plot is as reproducible as the statistic. Both halves answer the same question for the number ...

Data Hub also drafts the paragraphs a paper needs. From a finished analysis it writes a Methods sentence that names the test and cites the canonical reference for it, a Results sentence that reports the finding with the inline statistics from the engine, and a formatted refere...

Most bench scientists are not statisticians, and the most common analysis mistake is running a t-test or an ANOVA on data that breaks the test's assumptions. The guided wizard exists to prevent that. It asks a few plain questions about what you are comparing, then it does not ...

The assumption Report Card checks normality (Shapiro-Wilk, per group) and equal variance (Brown-Forsythe across groups), reports each as a plain-language pass or fail with a one-line reason, and when an assumption fails it falls back to the matching rank-based test that does n...

New graph generates a figure from the open table. The figure is real SVG, so it stays an infinitely scalable vector for a paper and also exports as a crisp hi-DPI PNG for a slide, or copies straight to the clipboard to paste into a document.

A Column table makes a column scatter (every replicate as a point over the group mean, the default, because individual points show the real spread a reviewer wants to see) or a bar with SD or SEM error bars. An XY table makes a scatter with a fitted curve laid over it (a least...

For a Column table you can also draw an estimation plot, the modern effect-size figure that shows the raw data alongside the bootstrap sampling distribution of the mean difference and its confidence interval, in the Gardner-Altman style for two groups and the Cumming style for...

Error bars come straight from the raw replicates, the same numbers the grid footer shows, so a figure of a table is always consistent with that table. Significance brackets are pulled from a stored ANOVA, so the right stars drop onto the figure with one toggle rather than bein...

Color is its own studio. The Palette Studio replaces a single color dropdown with a browseable palette library filtered by how many series the plot has, a custom per-series mode, a generate-and-lock workflow, import from a coolors.co URL, and your own saved palettes, with the ...

Cleaning a figure is part of the same loop. Right-click a data cell to exclude it: the value stays visible and editable in the grid, but every analysis and every plot treats it as absent, so it drops out of the mean, the error bars, the dots, and the stored test at once. Nothi...

You do not have to retype data you already have. Data Hub imports CSV files, pasted Excel ranges (with a transpose option for the common wrong-orientation case), and binary .xlsx workbooks. It detects the header row and the per-column types and maps the data onto a table. The ...

A table, an analysis, or a figure can be referenced from a note or a result. The Copy reference button writes a link that renders as a live chip wherever you paste it, and clicking the chip opens the table in Data Hub. A figure can also be dropped into a note as an image with ...

Data Hub documents participate in the same project and folder structure as notes, experiments, methods, and sequences, and they go through the same Trash flow with the same recovery window when deleted. The point of building the analysis surface into ResearchOS rather than lea...

A few threads tie it to the rest of the app. The power and sample-size planner answers the design question before any data is collected, how many subjects you need to detect an effect, how much power a planned n gives you, or the smallest effect that n can reliably detect, run...

Every Data Hub document is stored as a cell-level version-controlled document, the same technology behind Version History for notes. The dataset and the analysis choices are both versioned, so a change is never silently lost and two people editing different cells converge clea...

The statistics engine is checked against external reference values (scipy, statsmodels, lifelines, R, and a NIST certified dataset) in an automated test suite, so the numbers Data Hub reports match the tools the field already trusts. The public Transparency of tests page docum...

Every figure sits on a plot artboard, an Illustrator-style page in real units. Drop the figure onto a chosen paper size, position and scale it to exact units on a pan-and-zoom canvas, and export the page as an exact SVG. The same artboard is shared with the phylogenetics Tree ...

Because the footer stats, the analysis, and the figure all read the same cells, you cannot end up with a bar chart that disagrees with the table it came from. Change a replicate and the points move, the error bars resize, and the stored test re-runs.
Data Hub. The navigator rail holds your tables, analyses, and figures; the main panel shows the data grid, the results sheet, or the figure you are working on.
The Data Hub tab with the navigator rail on the left listing data tables, analyses, and graphs, and a publication figure with significance brackets in the main panel.
New table. Pick the archetype that matches your data; the grid and the available analyses follow from it.
The New data table dialog showing the eight table type tiles, Column, XY, Grouped, Survival, Contingency, Nested, Parts of whole, and Info sheet, each with a short description.
The results sheet. The plain-language verdict comes first, then the full statistics table, then the pairwise comparisons.
A results sheet showing a plain-language verdict at the top, an ANOVA table with SS, df, MS, F, and p columns, and a Tukey comparisons table with significance asterisks.
Dose-response. A 4PL or 5PL logistic fit reports the EC50 with an asymmetric confidence interval, the Hill slope, and the plateaus, with model comparison by AICc.
A dose-response results sheet with a fitted sigmoidal curve over the data points and an EC50, Hill slope, Top, and Bottom parameter table.
Survival. Kaplan-Meier curves per arm with the log-rank and Gehan-Breslow-Wilcoxon tests, plus Cox proportional-hazards regression.
A Kaplan-Meier survival results sheet with step curves for two arms and a log-rank test summary.
ROC and AUC. The full curve, the area under it with a confidence interval, and the optimal cut point by Youden's J.
An ROC curve results sheet plotting true positive rate against false positive rate with the area under the curve and the optimal cut point.
Linear regression. Slope and intercept with their standard errors and confidence intervals, R-squared, and the residual standard error.
A linear regression results sheet with the fitted line over the scatter and a slope, intercept, and R-squared table.
Multiple regression. Each predictor with its coefficient, confidence interval, standardized beta, and VIF, plus the overall model fit.
A multiple regression results sheet with one row per predictor showing coefficient, standard error, t, p, standardized beta, and VIF.
Contingency. The chi-square test on the count matrix, and for a 2x2 table Fisher's exact test with relative risk and odds ratio.
A contingency results sheet showing an observed and expected count matrix, the chi-square statistic, and 2x2 relative risk and odds ratio measures.

entries.title: The Workbench
headings: Four tabs, four ways to slice the work
Opening an experiment, the four-tab popup
Attach a method (or a PCR protocol)
Variation Notes and per-run PCR tweaks
Projects and Notes skip the filter strip
Each column carries its own count
When to use Notes vs Lab Notes
Lists holds list tasks, not experiments
Save the run, or grow a new protocol
Pick the format by where the file is going
Exporting more than one at a time
Sub-tasks live on list tasks, not experiments
bodySnippets: The Workbench is where active research lives day to day. One page with four core tabs (Projects, Experiments, Notes, Lists), each with its own way of grouping work, plus a fifth tab that appears only for people in a 1:1. The URL is /workbench, and /experiments still redirects ...

The Workbench packs four different views of your in-flight work into one page. The tab strip across the top names them and gives each a color identity. Projects (indigo), Experiments (blue), Notes (emerald), Lists (violet). The active tab fills with its color, and the others r...

Each tab uses a different organizing principle. The Projects tab is a browsable grid of project cards. The Experiments tab arranges protocols by stage in a status-banded list (the default) or a four-column kanban board, toggled with a List/Board button in the pane header. The ...

A fifth tab appears for some people. Lab heads, and any member who is in a 1:1, also see a role-relative Mentoring or Check-ins tab (rose), the shared advising surface for a lab-head and member pair. It is gated, so a solo user with no 1:1s never sees it.

On the Experiments and Lists tabs, a row of colored pills sits below the tab strip, one per project, plus an All projects pill on the left. Click a pill to narrow the view to that project, and click it again (or pick a different one) to clear. The filter carries across those t...

Some tasks never get filed under a project. A one-off experiment you spin up in the "Miscellaneous" standalone slot stays project-less on purpose. To move a task into or out of a project later, open it and use the project dropdown on the popup's Details tab. Alongside your rea...

The Experiments tab arranges every experiment task in your library by stage of the protocol. It opens in List view by default, a dense status-banded list grouped into sections (In flight, Awaiting write-up, Recent results, Earlier). A List / Board toggle in the pane header swi...

A left rail runs alongside both views. The top of the rail has a Status section (All experiments, In flight, Awaiting write-up, Recent results, Earlier) and a By project section with one entry per project. Clicking any rail item narrows the pane to that scope without leaving t...
The in-flight section of the list and the full board share the same four stage categories.
Experiments scheduled to begin today or recently, with no incomplete upstream task blocking the start. These are the cards you can pick up and run today, so the column reads first on the left.

Experiments that depend on an upstream task that hasn't finished yet. The blocking parent's name renders directly under the card in a small amber strip so you know what you're waiting on. Click the parent name to jump straight to that task and unblock the chain.

Experiments where today falls between the start date and the end date. Each card carries a Day N of M freshness label, and (if the experiment is part of a chain) a gray Next: pointer to the experiment that follows it so you can step through a multi-day protocol without leaving...

Experiments marked complete but with no results body or attached images on disk yet. This column is your reminder pile, the bench work is done but the writeup is still owed. When the column is empty it shows a bordered emerald chip reading "All recent experiments have results ...

Below the board, the Recent results zone holds completed experiments with results landed in the last 30 days, in a wide full-card grid. When you have results across two or more projects, the zone sub-groups them by project (a small colored dot per project header) so a busy mon...

The archive at the bottom, completed experiments older than 30 days. The zone header carries a Flat / By project toggle so you can flip between a single time-ordered grid and one block per project. There's no time cap, so the whole completion history is reachable from one plac...

Each card carries the experiment name, a project pill, the project color along the edge, a hero image (if the writeup has one), a method chip strip, and a freshness label keyed to the column it sits in. When an experiment is part of a dependency chain, the Running card shows a...
The blue button at the top right of the tab opens the New Task modal with the type pre-selected to experiment . It is the same modal you'd get from the Gantt.

The Notes tab holds every note that isn't tied to a specific experiment. Meeting notes from a Tuesday lab meeting, a running log of equipment-room weirdness, half-formed ideas you want to find again next month, they all land here, in one flat list with no section headers. The ...

The header carries a search box on the left and three type filter buttons in the middle ( All , Single , Running Logs ). Single notes are one-page records of a moment (a meeting, an idea). Running logs are timestamped multi-entry pages you append to over time (an equipment jou...

The Lists tab is the queue view of every list task you own. Lists are the non-experiment task type, things like checklists, paperwork, ordering tasks, errands, anything that isn't a protocol. The Lists tab sorts them into five stages by how their dates relate to today, stacked...

Each card carries the task name, a project color dot with the project name, and a date signal phrased relative to today ("3d overdue", "Started yesterday", "Starts in 4d"). Clicking a card expands it inline as an accordion panel. Opening one card collapses the previously-open ...
Clicking a list card expands it in place. A violet border highlights the open card, and a panel slides down below the header row. Inside the panel, you get these controls.

Clicking any experiment card opens a centered modal. The popup always opens on the Details tab, regardless of whether the experiment is ready, running, awaiting writeup, or completed. You land on the same view every time, then pick the tab that fits the task at hand.
The popup carries four tabs across the header.

The header row also holds a green completion checkmark, an Export icon (down arrow, experiments only), a Share button, a fullscreen toggle, and Delete. Drag any file onto the popup and it lands in the last-viewed editor tab's or folder, with a confirmation toast at the drop po...

The two markdown tabs hold different kinds of writing on the same experiment. Lab Notes is the during-the-run record, holding running observations, what reagents you grabbed, what went sideways, the photo you took at the bench five minutes ago. Results is the final-output writ...

Both tabs use the same editor surface, a single live markdown body with an Edit / Preview toggle, an attachment strip (Images / Files tabs) pinned below the editor, and an Add File toolbar button. Saving is explicit, so each Save checkpoint records a revertible version you can...

Drag images straight into the body to place them inline, or drop them on the strip to attach without inlining. Switch the strip to its Files tab for non-image attachments (PDFs especially). Click a file link in the prose to open or download it.

Each attached method has a collapsible amber Variation Notes panel at the top of its tab. Click + Add Note and the editor prepends a fresh heading so each entry is timestamped. Hover any rendered entry to reveal an in-place delete button. The notes save back to the experiment,...

For PCR-typed methods, the gradient editor and recipe table below the variation panel are editable per-experiment. Reset to Method drops your overrides back to the protocol's defaults, and Save Changes writes them to this task only.

Sometimes a run drifts from the protocol in a way worth recording on its own, not just a tweak but a real deviation. When a task carries a deviation note it renders in an amber box on the experiment's Details tab, so the next person to read the experiment sees exactly what was...
The Note Deviations dialog is where you write one. You describe what changed during the run, then choose what to do with it.

Click the down-arrow icon in the popup header (tooltip: Export experiment ) to open the Export dialog. The dialog heading reads Export <experiment name> and lists three format cards, each with a short description of what you get and who it's for. Click a card and the file down...

While the file builds, the dialog shows a Preparing export… spinner and disables every button (Escape and the backdrop are also locked) so you can't kick off a second export by accident. If anything goes wrong, an alert shows the error and the dialog stays open for a retry.
Overdue : list tasks whose end date has passed and that aren't complete yet. The first thing in your queue every morning.
Doing : list tasks where today falls between start and end.

Upcoming : list tasks scheduled to start within the next 14 days. Tasks starting further out are omitted from the main list, and a small gray footnote below the section reads "+ N scheduled later than 14d out" so you know they exist without cluttering the view.
Recently done : completed in the last 30 days.
Earlier : completed more than 30 days ago. Collapsed by default, so click the header to expand.
The name row lets you click the name to rename it inline. Press Enter or click away to save.
The sub-task checklist lets you tick individual items to check them off. Click an item's text to rename it, and the delete icon appears on hover to remove an item.
Add item appends a new item to the checklist when you type in the input and press Enter (or click Add ).
The Mark list complete button in the footer marks the whole list done and cascades all un-checked items to complete. Click again to mark it incomplete.
The Open full view link in the footer opens the legacy full-screen popup for the same task, useful for the Details and date-editing fields not exposed in the inline panel.
Details is the entry tab. It holds the name, dates, duration, project, tags, and the high-level toggles. If the task carries a (legacy) deviation log string, it renders here in an amber box.
Lab Notes is the during-the-run markdown body, with a bottom attachment strip (Images / Files tabs) for photos, PDFs, and other files.
Method shows one tab per attached method, with the Variation Notes panel and (for PCR methods) an editable gradient and recipe.
Results is the second markdown editor, for the final writeup (the gel image you'd put in a paper, the plot for a thesis chapter), with its own attachment strip.
Save to task results only. The deviation is recorded in this task's log and the underlying method stays untouched. Use this for a one-off change that only mattered for this run.

Fork as new method. The deviation becomes the seed of a brand-new method file, created as a child of the original with the deviation baked into its text. Use this when the change is good enough to keep as its own protocol going forward.

PDF report : a professional, printable PDF with a title page, a clickable table of contents, an outline pane (bookmarks) for jumping between sections, and inline images. Lab Notes, Results, every attached method (rendered, with per-run PCR tweaks), Sub-tasks, the Deviation log...

HTML report : a single self-contained page bundled in a with its attachments. Images are base64-inlined so the page renders even offline, and PDF methods and other non-image files sit alongside in , , and . Open it in any browser, or mail the zip to a collaborator who doesn't ...

Raw ResearchOS format : the full experiment as a sharable bundle ( , , the raw and , each method as its own JSON file including PCR protocols, plus every attached image and file in the same folder layout the app uses on disk). This is the format another ResearchOS user imports...
Open the Method tab and click the + button on the tab strip.

The Method Picker opens with Recently used in this project and Recently used pinned at the top, the full library below grouped by folder, and a search box that also takes queries. PCR protocols show up alongside markdown methods (their tab gets a small PCR badge).
Pick a method. It becomes a new tab on the strip. Attach as many as the experiment needs.

The Projects tab hides the pills because the cards on it already are the projects. The Notes tab hides them because notes aren't tied to any single project. To scope the note grid, use the search box at the top of that tab and filter by content instead.

Every board column has an uppercase header with a live count and a small info icon. Hover the icon for a one-line reminder of the rule that puts a card there (for Running, "Today falls between start and end date"). When all four columns are empty the board collapses to a singl...

The Notes tab is for writing that isn't attached to any single experiment. Per-experiment lab notes (running observations, what reagents you grabbed, the photo at the bench five minutes ago) live inside that experiment's popup on its Lab Notes tab. If the writing belongs to a ...

Experiment tasks always render on the Experiments tab, never on Lists, even when their dates would make them overdue or upcoming. The split is by task type, not by status. The Lists tab is for the to-do work that supports your protocols, not the protocols themselves.

entries.title: Feedback
headings: Why a pre-filled URL instead of a POST
bodySnippets: The feedback button is a thin layer over GitHub Issues. Click it, pick a type (Bug, Feature request, General feedback), fill in the modal, and ResearchOS opens a pre-filled GitHub issue URL in a new tab. Nothing submits automatically. You see the body, you edit it, you click S...

Every page in ResearchOS carries a FeedbackButton in the bottom toolbar (or the corresponding floating cluster on dashboards). Click it and the FeedbackModal mounts. The button is always available because every surface in the app is a fair target for feedback.

Pick a type at the top of the modal. The modal remembers your last-used type across sessions, so the choice you make most is the one already selected next time (an error-triggered open is the exception, it locks to Bug so you see the crash context you came to report).

On submit, the modal does not POST to a server. It builds a GitHub issue URL with the title, body, and label pre-filled in the query string, then opens that URL in a new tab. You see the body GitHub is about to create, you can edit any of it, and the issue does not exist until...

Every feedback type lets you attach screenshots, by dropping them on the modal, pasting from the clipboard, or clicking to pick files. The images stay in memory and never leave your machine on their own. A GitHub new-issue URL is text only, so the images cannot ride along with...

The BugStomp scene (a small BeakerBot moment you might hit after a crash recovery) ends with the same feedback flow. Stomping the bug opens the FeedbackModal pre-set to Bug type and prefilled with a hint from the crash context. It's the same modal as the manual entry point, ju...

Bug. Something is broken, like a crash, a wrong number, or a misrendering. The modal auto-attaches the current route, the browser/OS string, and any recent uncaught error details so the report has enough context for a fix.
Feature request. A new capability you wish existed. The modal walks a lighter template (what you want, why) and routes to the enhancement label on GitHub.
General feedback. Anything that does not fit the first two, like a comment on UX, a typo, or a confused-by-naming note. Routes to the feedback label.
Nothing on your machine moves until you intentionally submit.
You can sanitize anything that landed in the auto-attached error details before posting.
The full report shape is determined by in the repo, so the GitHub side renders a templated form rather than a raw markdown body.
If you would rather not open a new tab, a Copy Link button puts the same pre-filled issue URL on your clipboard so you can open it yourself.

The GitHub Issues template at defines the form fields and label routing on the receiving side. The modal sends a type query parameter that GitHub uses to pre-select the matching template. The result is one consistent shape regardless of who clicked Bug, Feature request, or Gen...

We could have POSTed feedback straight to a webhook or a server we run, and the user would not have to see the body before submission. We did not, because the moment a POST endpoint exists, every typed word is a thing that left your machine before you saw the final copy. The p...
The FeedbackModal with Bug selected. The body is editable; nothing leaves your machine until you click through to GitHub.
The FeedbackModal with the Bug radio selected, an editable Title field, an editable description, and an Auto-attached error details section at the bottom.

entries.title: Figure Composer
headings: The page frame and the canvas
Icon library is a flag-gated preview
Your figures stay in your folder
bodySnippets: The Figure Composer lays your real data figures out on a single publication page. Each panel is a live view of a sequence map, a molecule, a phylogenetic tree, or a Data Hub plot, arranged at true size on a real paper sheet and exported as one clean vector SVG. It runs entirel...

A finished figure in a paper is usually several smaller figures arranged on one page with panel labels, a few arrows, and a caption. The Figure Composer is the surface that does that last step inside ResearchOS. It lives at and pulls panels straight from the work you already h...

The page is measured in real inches on a real paper size, so what you see on screen is what the journal gets. Nothing about the figure is a screenshot. Every panel stays a true vector, and the whole page exports as one scalable file.

The composer pulls panels from four parts of your workspace. Each source exposes the same placing, resizing, and labeling interface, and each re-renders from the original data whenever the source changes.

When you add a figure to the page, you are not pasting a flat picture. You are placing a live panel that knows where it came from. All four panel sources re-render from their source when the source changes. This is the part most figure tools cannot do, because they only ever s...

Panels are labeled automatically in reading order, top row first and then left to right, in your choice of , , or style. You drag panels to position them, resize from the corner, and an optional snap-to-grid keeps everything aligned.

Selecting a panel opens its style inspector. Each source exposes the controls that make sense for it. A sequence map panel lets you toggle features and annotation tracks. A chemistry panel has no style options (the 2D structure is self-contained). A phylogenetic tree panel exp...
Style changes apply only to that panel in the figure, not back to the source, so adjusting a palette for the figure does not change the Data Hub table.
A paper preset sets the page to a standard physical size. Seven presets cover the common cases.

Portrait and landscape orientations are available for each, and a custom width and height field covers anything the presets do not. The white sheet is the export region, so anything on it is in the figure and anything off it is just working space. The canvas pans and zooms the...

Every placement, resize, drag, style change, and panel deletion is undoable. The toolbar carries an Undo button that steps back through the session history one action at a time. There is no redo; undo covers the whole history of the current editing session.

A small set of annotation tools covers what figures usually need on top of the panels: text labels, arrows, and brackets, placed directly on the page and exported as part of the vector. Beyond annotations, an optional icon library lets you drop scientific icons onto the figure...

The whole page exports as a single vector SVG at publication resolution, so the lines stay crisp at any size and the file drops straight into a manuscript or a slide. Because the panels are true vectors rather than rasterized images, the export is the figure itself, not a phot...
See the Data Hub and Phylogenetics pages for the data figures those panels come from.
Sequences. A panel from the Sequences library renders as a linear or circular sequence map, with features, annotations, and primer sites exactly as the full sequence viewer shows them.
Chemistry. A panel from the Chemistry library renders the molecule as a 2D chemical structure diagram.
Phylogenetics. A panel from the Tree Studio renders the saved tree with all its layers (annotation tracks, color strips, metadata heatmaps) using the same native renderer.

Data Hub. A panel from a Data Hub table renders one of its saved plots (bar chart, scatter, histogram, and so on). The figure math stays in the Data Hub engine; the composer only places the rendered output.
Letter (8.5 x 11 in)
Legal (8.5 x 14 in)
Journal single column (3.5 in wide)
Journal double column (7.2 in wide)
Square (6 x 6 in)

The scientific icon library is gated behind the flag, which is off by default. When the flag is on, the library draws from vetted open-license sources (CC0, CC-BY, and similar), and the Figure Composer auto-generates a credits block listing the source, license, and creator for...

The Figure Composer runs entirely in the browser. There is no server doing the layout or the export, so your figures and the data behind their panels stay in your own folder the same way your notes, sequences, and trees do.
The Figure Composer with three panels on one publication page. Each panel is a live view from its source, not a screenshot.

The Figure Composer at /figures showing a multi-panel publication page with a sequence map panel, a phylogenetic tree panel, and a Data Hub plot panel arranged on a white page sheet, with the panel picker sidebar open on the left.
Selecting a panel opens its style inspector. Controls vary by source type; a tree panel exposes scale bar and branch color, a Data Hub panel exposes its plot's palette.
The Figure Composer with a phylogenetic tree panel selected, showing the style inspector sidebar with controls for scale bar, tip labels, branch color, and palette exposed by that panel's source.
The artboard preset picker. Pick a standard page size or journal column width; the white sheet is the export region at true physical dimensions.

The Figure Composer showing the artboard preset picker open with options Letter, A4, Legal, Journal single column, Journal double column, Slide 16:9, and Square, with a white page sheet rendered at true inches on the pan-zoom canvas.

entries.title: Gantt Chart
headings: Chain two tasks with a dependency
Filter with the multi-select project dropdown
Attach an image to a task
See a whole chain at once with hover-highlight
Standalone tasks have no project to filter by
bodySnippets: The Gantt timeline holds every active task across every project you can see. You scan packed rows of colored bars and your eye picks out the busy weeks, the dependencies that cross projects, and the gaps where nothing is planned yet. The chart is a working surface, not a read-...
Every visual element on the Gantt encodes one specific thing. Once you know the vocabulary, a scroll across the timeline tells you most of what you need at a glance.

The toolbar at the top of the page is where you scope and zoom the chart. The 2026-05 redesign collapsed the old per-project pill row into a single multi-select dropdown so a lab with many projects can keep the chrome compact.

Grab any bar and drag it to a different day. On drop, the task's start date moves to that day and its duration stays the same. Any downstream tasks linked through a dependency recompute their dates to keep their constraints valid. An FS (start-after) child of a moved parent sl...

Most cascades apply silently. The orange "This change will affect N task(s)" modal only opens when an SF (finish-before) chain would shift a task to start in the past. The modal lists every affected task with its old and new dates and a per-task warning line for the past-date ...

A dependency captures the idea that one piece of bench work cannot sensibly begin (or end) until another one reaches a certain point. On the Gantt you express that by dragging one bar and dropping it on top of another. A popup then asks how the two tasks should be linked.

Once you pick a relationship, the dragged task's start date snaps to whatever the dependency requires. Tasks in the same chain share the same colored top stripe, so you can trace a chain across bars. Drag a bar in the chain and downstream tasks shift with it.

Because bars pack into rows by date rather than by chain, the members of one dependency chain can end up scattered across the timeline. Two cues help you read them as a group. The first is always on. Each bar in a chain carries small chain-colored dots in its corner, where the...

The second cue is the hover treatment. Move your cursor over any bar that belongs to a multi-member chain and the whole chain lights up. Every bar in that chain stays at full strength and picks up a ring in the chain's color, while every bar outside the chain dims back so the ...
The redesigned toolbar replaced the long pill row with a single dropdown. Here is how it works.

Not every task belongs to a project. A quick experiment you spin up without filing it under a research question lives in the "Miscellaneous" standalone slot, with no project attached. Those orphan tasks used to vanish the moment you scoped the chart to a specific project, sinc...

The bottom of the Projects dropdown now carries a Standalone row (set off by a dashed divider and marked with a hollow dashed swatch and a small "no project" hint) whose tooltip reads "Toggle visibility of experiments with no project." Tick it to fold your standalone tasks bac...

To move a task into or out of that bucket, open the task and use the project dropdown in its detail popup. Alongside your real projects it lists a Standalone (no project) option. Picking it drops the task off every project, and picking a real project files an orphan task back ...

PIs land on the same Gantt UI as every member, but the timeline pulls from a wider pool. The project dropdown on the left of the toolbar now spans every member's projects across the lab, not just the PI's own. One scroll of the chart shows the whole lab's work side by side.

That changes what the page is for. For a member, the Gantt is a personal schedule. For a PI, it becomes a coordination surface. Dependency chains can stretch across users, the project dropdown doubles as a per-member filter (untick a student's projects to focus on a co-PI's ti...

To file a gel image or a microscope snap, click the bar to open the task, then drag the image file from Finder onto the open popup card. The drop works anywhere on the card, so you do not have to aim at a specific field. The image saves to the task's Images folder and shows up...

Dropping a file does not splice a reference into the notes body. That stays your call. When you want the image to appear inline in your notes, drag it out of the strip and into the notes editor, and ResearchOS places the reference where you drop it.

Right-click any day cell in the date grid to open a context menu with a Mark as PTO / Unmark PTO option. Marked days render with sky-blue diagonal stripes and a small dot in the day header so they stand out at a glance. When a cascade computes new dates for chained tasks, PTO ...
PTO marks are per-user (your time off is private), so a labmate looking at the same chart will not see your stripes unless they are viewing through a PI dashboard that aggregates across members.

Goals appear in two places. Above each week's day headers, every active goal that overlaps the week renders as a thin colored bar (longer goals sit lower). Hover one for a tooltip showing the goal name and days remaining (or overdue). Click a bar to open the goal editor.

The Goal sidebar on the right lists active goals as cards. Each card surfaces SMART subgoals as checkboxes so you can tick them off without leaving the chart. Use the + Goal button in the toolbar to add a new high-level goal.

Click a bar to open the task detail popup, where you can edit name, dates, tags, attached methods, notes, results, and subtasks. The popup is the same one used by the Workbench and the calendar, so changes here sync everywhere.

One colored bar per task. Bar color is the project's color. Bar start and length are the task's start date and duration. Tasks pack into rows automatically (the chart is not grouped by project), so non-overlapping tasks share a row to keep the chart dense.

Dependency stripes. A short top stripe in a contrasting color marks tasks in a dependency chain. Every bar in the same chain shares the same stripe color, so you can trace a chain visually across rows. Standalone tasks show a dimmer per-experiment stripe.
Type icons. A horizontal-lines icon on the right plus a thicker white left edge marks a list task. A dollar-sign icon and a diagonal stripe mark a purchase task.
High-level tasks. Render in muted amber and sit at the top of the packed rows so they read as overlays on the regular schedule.
Completion state. Completed tasks desaturate and go partially transparent with a checkmark on the right edge. A partial fill on the left of a bar shows sub-task completion progress.
Shared badges. A small bracketed initial like on a bar means this task is shared. The bar still uses the project's color, not the owner's.
Edge fade. Bars that start before or end after the visible window fade to black on that edge to signal that they continue offscreen.
PTO stripes. Days you have marked as PTO render with sky-blue diagonal stripes plus a small dot in the day header. Cascades skip these days when computing dependent task dates.

Weekend hash. Saturday and Sunday columns read as light grey background. Tasks belonging to a 5-day project overlay weekend portions with a diagonal hash so you know work does not actually happen on those days. A 7-day project draws solid across weekends.

Projects dropdown (multi-select). Click it to open the project list. Every project starts checked, so the default chart shows everything. Untick a project to hide its bars. Two quick actions sit at the top. Select all restores the everyone-checked default, and Unselect all cle...
Tag chips. When any task or project has tags, a row of tag chips appears next to the project dropdown. Toggle a chip to narrow the chart to tasks matching that tag.
Shared toggle. A small share-icon switch that hides or shows tasks others have shared with you.

Zoom segmented control. Eight buttons: , , , , , , , . Shorter zooms keep day-level detail. Longer ones span many weeks. All currently shows an 8-week window starting from the current Monday, not a full-history view.
Week navigator on the right. Step the visible window back and forward one week at a time, with a date picker for jumping to a specific Monday and a Today link that snaps back to this week.
+ Task and + Goal buttons at the far right of the toolbar to add a task or a high-level goal without leaving the chart.
Start at same time (SS). Both tasks begin on the same day. On the chart they line up at the same left edge.
Start after (FS). The dragged task starts the day after the target finishes, so the two sit back to back with no overlap.

Finish before (SF). The dragged task finishes the day before the target starts. This is a strict gap, where the predecessor clears the calendar entirely before the successor begins, so the two never share a day even when the predecessor is a single-day task.
Click Projects to expand the list.
The default is all checked . The chart shows every project until you uncheck something.
Select all at the top of the dropdown restores the everyone-checked default (useful after a long Unselect-and-pick session).
Unselect all clears every checkmark in one click, so you can quickly re-tick only the one or two projects you want to focus on.
The dropdown stays open while you click, so you can stage the right filter set without dropdown-bounce.
Dropping on a Saturday or Sunday in a project that has weekends disabled bumps the start date to the next Monday. Toggle the weekend setting from each project's detail popup on the Home page.

Only experiments can be chained. Lists and purchases are deliberately kept out of dependency chains, since linking a shopping order or a checklist into a scheduling chain tends to create constraints nobody wanted. On the Gantt this shows up as a non-interactive signal. Drag a ...
The Gantt at a glance, with packed rows of bars colored by project, a multi-select project dropdown in the toolbar, weekend hash, and PTO stripes.
The Gantt timeline with packed rows of colored bars, a multi-select project dropdown in the toolbar, PTO stripes on a Saturday cell, and a dependency stripe linking two bars across rows.
Zoom segmented control and week navigator. The eight zoom levels set how many days each column represents. The navigator steps or jumps to any Monday.
The Gantt toolbar showing the zoom segmented control with eight buttons (1W, 2W, 3W, 1M, 3M, 6M, 1Y, All) and the week navigator with back/forward arrows, a date picker, and a Today link.
Lab head view, where the project dropdown spans every member's projects, so one timeline shows the whole lab.
The Gantt as a PI, with the project dropdown open showing projects from every member in the lab.
Clicking a bar opens the full task editor over the Gantt. Same popup as the Workbench and the calendar.
A task detail popup opened over the Gantt chart, with Details, Lab Notes, Method, and Results tabs.

entries.title: Where you land
headings: The root URL is a router, not a page
Members land on the Projects grid
bodySnippets: Opening ResearchOS drops you straight into the surface that fits your role. There's no separate home dashboard to build or maintain. A lab member or solo researcher lands on the Workbench, opened to a grid of their projects. A lab head lands on the curated Lab Overview. The ro...

ResearchOS used to open on a customizable widget dashboard at , a canvas of tiles you pinned, dragged, and rearranged. That dashboard is gone. The root URL now renders nothing of its own. It signs you in, handles any deep link you arrived with (e.g. a task or project link some...

For a member or solo researcher, the landing surface is the Workbench with its Projects tab selected. The grid shows a card per project with the project's color dot, a percent-complete progress bar, and small count chips (experiments, list tasks, and sequences once you have an...
The New Project button lives on the Projects grid. It opens the same create modal everywhere a project can be started.

When a labmate shares a project with you, it surfaces in your Projects grid alongside your own. The card reads the same as any other, with its color, progress, and count chips. Whether your edits save back depends on the permission the owner granted. Edit permission writes to ...
A lab member or solo researcher lands on the Workbench , opened to its Projects grid.
A lab head (PI) lands on the Lab Overview , the fixed curated page that rolls up the whole lab.
Open the Projects tab on the Workbench (it's where you already are when you land).
Click New Project , pick a color from the swatch, and type a name (e.g. CRISPR Gene Editing Study ).

Press Create . The modal closes and leaves you right where you were, with the new card already in the grid (creating a project doesn't jump you into it). The project's color then carries through everywhere it appears, like the bar on the Gantt.

There's no add-widget palette, no drag-to-arrange canvas, and no per-user dashboard layout to keep in sync anymore. Everyone gets one designed landing surface for their role, and the same{" "} Daily Tasks sidebar runs alongside it.

The color you pick follows the project across the whole app, the bar on the Gantt , the dot on the project card, and the overlay on the calendar. Pick distinct colors early so a busy Gantt stays readable as your project count grows.

Sharing a single project is the right tool when one labmate wants to follow along on one specific project. For a single surface that rolls up every member's activity, today's events, and pending approvals at once, a PI uses the curated Lab Overview instead.
Members and solo researchers land here: the Workbench Projects grid.
The Workbench Projects grid showing several project cards, each with a color dot, project name, percent-complete progress bar, and count chips for experiments and tasks.

entries.title: Image annotation
headings: The raw image is never modified
bodySnippets: Circle a gel band, arrow a feature on a micrograph, label the lanes. The image annotation editor lets you mark up a photo right where it lives in your notes, and it doesn't touch the original file to do it. Your markup is a separate, re-editable layer you can change or remove ...

Lab images carry meaning that the pixels alone do not. A reviewer needs to know which band is the product, which colony you picked, which cell in the field is the one you counted. ResearchOS gives you a drawing surface for exactly that, and it treats your raw image as somethin...

This is the part worth understanding before anything else. When you annotate an image, ResearchOS doesn't flatten your shapes into the photo. The original file stays byte-for-byte identical on disk. Your markup is written to a small companion file next to it, called a sidecar,...

Because the markup lives in its own file, two things follow that matter for trustworthy records. Your annotations stay re-editable, since every shape comes back as a live object you can move, recolor, or delete. And to revert, you delete the sidecar. That returns the image to ...
There are two ways into the annotation surface, both starting from a thumbnail of the image you want to mark up.
Click a thumbnail to open its image metadata popup (the same popup covered in the editor guide ), then press the Annotate button.
Or, on an image strip thumbnail, click the small pencil control to jump straight into the editor.
Either path opens a full-screen editor with the image fit to your viewport and a toolbar across the top. If the image already has annotations, they load back in ready to edit.
The toolbar holds a focused set of marking tools, each suited to a common lab gesture.

Each shape carries its own color and stroke width (text labels use a font size instead), so you can use red for the result and a thinner gray for context. Pick a tool, draw on the image, and the new shape is selected so you can nudge it into place immediately. Double-click a t...

Nothing you place is final. Switch to the select tool to drag any shape, and grab the handles on a box or line to resize it. The color and size controls in the toolbar re-edit whichever shape is selected, so you can recolor an existing arrow without redrawing it. A full undo a...

When you zoom past the fit point, a small navigator appears in the corner with a preview of the whole image and a green box marking the region you are looking at. Click or drag inside it to jump the view to another part of the image without zooming back out.
When the markup looks right, press Save and the editor writes your shapes to the sidecar and closes. Cancel discards everything since you opened the editor. Saving never writes the raw image.

Because the markup is a layer, ResearchOS can paint it on top of the photo wherever the photo appears, and it does. Your circled band and labeled lanes show up in the note's markdown preview, in the image strips, and in the results gallery. The overlay even renders down to thu...

The shapes are stored in the image's natural pixel space, so a single saved annotation scales correctly from a full-width figure all the way down to a 64-pixel thumbnail. Annotate it once and it reads right at every size.
Arrow and Line : point at a feature or connect two things. The arrow draws a head, and the line is a plain segment.
Rectangle and Ellipse : box a region of interest or circle a band, a colony, a cell.
Freehand pen : trace an irregular outline, like the edge of a tissue section.
Polygon : click vertex by vertex to bound a region with straight edges, then close it by pressing Enter or clicking back on the first point (Escape cancels a polygon in progress).
Text label : drop a caption directly on the image, for example to name lanes on a gel or stages on a time-course.

Annotations are stored as vector shapes (an ellipse, an arrow, a line of text) recorded in the image's own pixel coordinates, not as a new bitmap painted over your photo. That keeps the sidecar tiny, keeps the markup sharp at any zoom, and is what makes a one-file delete a cle...

You run a gel, snap a photo at the bench, and it lands in your note (see the editor guide for how images get there). You open the annotation editor, circle the product band with an ellipse, arrow a non-specific band you want a labmate to ignore, and add three small text labels...
The editor over a gel photo. An ellipse circles the product band, an arrow flags a feature, and text labels mark the lanes.

The full-screen annotation editor open over a gel photo. A red ellipse circles one band, a blue arrow points to a feature, and three small text labels read 'Lane 1', 'Lane 2', and 'Lane 3' across the top of the gel.
The same annotation rendered in a note's preview. The overlay follows the image into every surface, down to thumbnails.
A note in preview mode showing the annotated gel inline, with the same ellipse, arrow, and lane labels rendered over the image at full figure width.

entries.title: Import from LabArchives
bodySnippets: The 7-step wizard that turns a LabArchives Offline Notebook ZIP into native ResearchOS tasks, plus the bulk-sort screen for cleaning up after.

The mental model for the importer is one rule. Each LabArchives page becomes one ResearchOS task . Every entry on that page (text, headings, attachments, embedded images) is collapsed into the task's Lab Notes body. The page's newest entry timestamp becomes the task's start da...
Notebook folders become projects. By default each folder lands as a new project named , but the wizard lets you point folders at projects you already have or leave them unassigned.
You start from Settings → LabArchives → Open import… , which opens the wizard as a modal. If you haven't produced an Offline Notebook ZIP yet, hop over to Exporting from LabArchives first.
The wizard runs a fixed sequence of steps. You can back out at any point before the apply phase starts. The step header in the modal shows where you are.

Every notebook folder that contains pages you're importing gets a row in the Map projects step. Pages still inside a folder share one row per folder. A page that sits right at the export root with one folder name above it gets its own row defaulting to create new project named...
Here is how the three decisions behave.

The Start import button stays disabled while any row has a validation error (empty new-project name, no existing project picked). Once the mapping is valid, clicking Start import kicks off the apply phase.

Right after the wizard finishes, the Done step shows an Open bulk-sort button. Clicking it replaces the wizard with a full-screen list of every task the import just created. Tasks are grouped by their assigned project (or under (no project) if unassigned), and each row exposes...

Tick the checkbox on multiple rows and the top of the screen switches to a bulk action bar with Move to a project, Change type to , and Delete N tasks . Edits write through to disk one row at a time, so you can leave the screen partway through and the work already done stays.
Create new project : the wizard creates a new project named and attaches every page under that folder to it. You can edit the name in the row before applying.
Use existing : pick one of your live (non-archived) projects from the dropdown. Pages under that folder are attached to the project you picked.
No project : the pages land as tasks with no project assignment. You can move them to projects later from the bulk-sort screen or by opening each task individually.
A project dropdown to move that task to a different project (or to no project ).
A task type dropdown to flip the task from (the default) to or .
A Delete button for tasks that don't belong anywhere, useful when an old notebook had stray meeting-notes pages mixed in with experiments.

Form-B inline images, by default. Images that LabArchives stores as cloud URLs aren't in the ZIP. The Fetch images step pulls them in when reachable, otherwise the markdown gets a placeholder you can rehydrate later from the per-task banner.
Live LabArchives links between pages. If a LabArchives page links to another page by its LabArchives URL, the link comes across as plain text. ResearchOS doesn't rewrite those into local task links.
Need to produce the ZIP first? See Exporting from LabArchives .
Curious about how the inline-image rehydration paths work? The LabArchives integration page breaks down Form-A vs Form-B and the DevTools-script path.
Imported tasks look and behave the same as native ResearchOS tasks. The Experiments & Notes page covers the task popup, the Lab Notes editor, and how attachments work once they're on disk.
Choose format. The wizard supports the LabArchives Offline Notebook ZIP. PDF and Chrome-print formats are on the roadmap and show as Coming soon cards on this step. Pick the ZIP option and continue.

Upload ZIP. Drag the file you downloaded from your LabArchives confirmation email onto the drop zone, or click to pick it from disk. The wizard validates that the file is a and warns if it's larger than 500 MB (the parser holds the archive in browser memory, so multi-gigabyte ...

Preview notebook. The wizard parses the ZIP and shows what it found, including folder count, page count, entry count, attachment count, plus a collapsible tree of the notebook structure. If LabArchives left some inline images as URLs rather than bundling them, an amber banner ...

Map projects. One row per top-level notebook folder. Each row offers three decisions. Create new project (the default, with a suggested name you can edit), Use existing (pick from your live project list), or No project (the pages on that branch land unassigned). Project mappin...

Fetch images (optional). If the notebook has inline images that LabArchives stores as URLs (and you're not in demo mode), the wizard offers a step to bring those images in. Two paths are available, both credential-free. One is a generated DevTools script you paste into a brows...

Importing. A progress bar walks through the two write phases, creating new projects first, then writing one task directory per page. Leave the tab open. Each page becomes with a , a folder for attachments, a folder for inline images, and a sidecar (inside the subdirectory, not...

Done. The summary lists how many tasks and projects landed, how many pages were skipped as duplicates of earlier imports, how many online-only images were rehydrated, and any per-page warnings. From here you can close the wizard, or jump straight to Open bulk-sort to re-classi...

ResearchOS reads the ZIP and stops there. It never calls back into LabArchives to write changes, and it doesn't poll LabArchives for new content. The import is a one-way snapshot, and you can re-run it whenever you have a fresher ZIP.

Several wizard-step screenshots are pending capture. Steps 2 (Upload) and 5 (Fetch images) require an actual notebook ZIP and a signed-in LabArchives session, neither of which the wiki-capture fixture seeds. Steps 3 (Preview notebook), 4 (Map projects), and the bulk-sort scree...
Step 1, only the Offline Notebook ZIP path is live right now.
The import wizard's Choose format step, with three cards: LabArchives Offline Notebook ZIP selected in blue, and two grayed-out Coming soon cards for the PDF and Chrome print paths.

entries.title: Inventory
headings: Why most lab inventories become fiction
The big idea, count containers not volumes
The Supplies hub, where ordering becomes inventory
Barcode scanning from the desktop webcam
Spreadsheet import for an existing inventory
Precise consumption is opt-in, never the default
Type expiry once, then forget it
Never start from a blank database
History, trash, search, and sharing come for free
bodySnippets: The inventory you will actually keep. Most lab inventories die because they ask for precision the bench cannot sustain. Inventory in ResearchOS asks for almost nothing (a coarse count and the occasional one-tap status) and computes the signals that actually matter (what is exp...

Almost every electronic lab notebook ships an inventory, and almost every academic lab abandons the one it has. The reason is always the same. The system stores a precise running amount (for example "47.3 mL remaining") and expects you to decrement it every time you pipette. N...

So Inventory is built around a different question. The question is not what we can track. It is what a busy lab will realistically keep current with near-zero effort, and how to make those few things produce real value. The answer is to count containers instead of volumes, fli...

The primary number on a stock is a count of physical containers . Three vials, two bottles, one plate, a box of tips. You change that count only when a whole container is finished (you throw out the empty tube, three goes to two) or a new one arrives . That is an event that ha...

There is an optional per-container amount label (for example "1 mL" or "100 ug") for people who want to record how big each container is, but it is never required and it is never decremented for you. It is a label, not a ledger. A lab that wants more detail can add it, and a l...

Alongside the count, every stock carries a coarse status (in stock, low, empty). You flip it with a one-tap control the moment you notice at the bench, because your eyeball is the sensor and no threshold math is needed for that to be useful. If you also set a low-at-count thre...

The payoff of an inventory is not the stock list you scroll. It is three signals, and all three are derived from data you enter once at receive, or not at all, and then maintained by the calendar rather than by you. These are the center of the feature, not an afterthought.

None of these needs an accurate running total of anything. Expiry and staleness need zero ongoing input at all. That is the point. The value lives in signals that the passage of time computes for free.

Inventory does not live alone. It shares a home with Purchases under a single Supplies hub, presented as two tabs. The reason they sit together is that one feeds the other. Purchases answers "what did we buy." Inventory answers "what do we have, where is it, and is it still go...

When you mark a purchase order received , which is something the buyer already does, Inventory offers to add it for you. It pre-fills the name, vendor, catalog number, and link from the line item you bought, defaults the container count to the quantity ordered, stamps the rece...

The flow runs the other way too. When finishing a container drops an item below its low threshold, that item lands in the Purchases reorder queue , so "we are nearly out" turns into "this is on the list to buy" without anyone retyping the reagent. The loop closes (you buy it, ...

Finishing a container is a roughly monthly one-tap event, and a barcode scan is simply that tap made faster. ResearchOS scans through your desktop webcam (the app is Chrome and Edge only, so the browser's built-in barcode reader is available), no separate hardware required.

There are two things you can scan. A container code is a lab-applied label on one specific tube or box. Scanning it counts that container down by one, re-derives the status (to empty at zero, to low when it crosses your threshold), and drops the item into the reorder queue whe...

Lab reagents from suppliers like NEB, Sigma-Aldrich, and Thermo Fisher mostly do not carry the retail barcodes that consumer databases know, so the high-value path is the lab-applied container code you control, not a manufacturer lookup. An optional product lookup can pre-fill...

When you want to know not just that you own something but exactly where it sits, the storage map gives you a location tree (freezer, then rack, then box, nested as deep as your storage really goes) with a box grid at the bottom. The grid is a rows-by-columns layout with A1-sty...

Click an empty cell to place a stock there, or a filled cell to see what it holds (item name, lot, expiry, count) and move or remove it. A breadcrumb above the grid shows the full path of the box you are looking at. The map is optional. You can run a perfectly useful inventory...

If your lab already keeps a list somewhere (and most do, usually a spreadsheet), you do not have to re-enter it. The import path lets you paste straight from Excel or Google Sheets to bulk-load what you already have, so an existing inventory comes across in one action instead ...
Every inventory item belongs to exactly one category. The category you choose drives which extra typed fields appear in the detail editor. The full set of categories is listed below.

All categories share the same stock list, storage positions, expiry, staleness signals, low-count threshold, history, trash, search, and sharing. The category only controls which extra fields the detail editor renders.

The volume-ledger model that sinks most inventories (deduct a few microliters per experiment) is the right tool for a narrow set of cases, like an expensive monoclonal antibody, a controlled substance, or a shared aliquot people argue over. For those, a precise per-use record ...

So that behavior is an explicit per-item toggle, off by default. When off, which is the vast majority, the item lives entirely in the count, status, and expiry world above, and you never see a volume field you are obligated to maintain. When on, that one item exposes the deduc...

Expiring soon and expired. Driven by the expiration date you typed once. The clock does the rest forever. This is what keeps you from running a failed experiment on dead reagent, or tossing money on an antibody that lapsed months ago.

Stale and untouched. Driven by the received date (stamped automatically) plus the last time the record was touched (also automatic). Pure time math, zero ongoing input. This surfaces the six forgotten tubes in the back of the freezer, and the inverse, a critical reagent nobody...

Low or empty. Driven by the container count and an optional low-at-count threshold, or by a single manual low tap. This is what keeps you from re-buying what you already have, and from discovering you are out on experiment day.
Reagent (the default). Generic chemical or consumable with no typed extension fields.

Antibody . Carries target, host species, clonality and clone, conjugate, isotype, reactivity, applications (for example WB, IF, IHC, FACS), recommended dilution, and an RRID for reproducibility. Antibodies are also the textbook case for opt-in precise consumption tracking, sin...

Plasmid . Carries backbone, insert, resistance, bacterial host, size in base pairs, source and Addgene number, and an attached sequence file. Lines up with the cloning and sequencing work elsewhere in the app.
Enzyme , Primer , Cell line , Strain . Plain categories with no extra typed fields today. A future version will add typed fields to each the same way antibodies and plasmids were extended.
Kit . A multi-component commercial kit treated as a single inventory record. Track kit lots and expiry dates without splitting the kit into individual components.

Equipment . A single piece of shared equipment. Equipment items have no container-count semantics since there is normally one instance, but they carry storage location, expiry (for calibration deadlines), and the same staleness signals as any other item.
Other . For anything that does not fit the categories above.

Expiry is the one date worth typing, and you type it exactly once, when the tube arrives or straight off the vendor label. After that the calendar does the work forever. You never revisit it, and the expiring-soon signal stays correct on its own.

The bar to start an inventory is meant to be near zero. Received purchases populate it for you, hand-added items autocomplete their name, vendor, and catalog number from your past purchases, and you can skip location entirely with a free-text note like "-80 door, left." Nobody...

The Companion app on iOS and Android has a dedicated Inventory scanning tab that uses the phone camera as a barcode scanner, so you can scan container codes while standing at the freezer without needing a laptop webcam. See Companion: inventory scanning for the full mobile flow.

Inventory rides the same rails as the rest of ResearchOS, so every item and stock inherits version history , trash with a recovery window , search , and sharing and permissions with no inventory-specific machinery. In a shared lab folder, new inventory records default to whole...
The Inventory list with signal-driven grouping. Expiring, low, and empty items surface at the top so the most urgent reorders are visible without scrolling.
The Inventory list view showing stock items grouped by signal, with colored status chips for expiring, low, and empty items and a search bar at the top.
The storage map. Navigate the location tree on the left and click any cell to see what sits there or to move a stock in or out.
The storage map panel showing a freezer tree on the left (freezer, rack, box hierarchy) and a rows-by-columns box grid on the right with colored cells indicating stock status.

entries.title: Lab calculators
headings: The math is validated, not approximated
bodySnippets: A floating beaker button opens a set of everyday bench calculators. Every one computes live in your browser, and nothing you type is saved.

Bench work is full of small calculations. How much powder to weigh out for a stock, how much to add for a dilution, what the melting temperature of a primer is, how many picomoles are in a microgram of DNA. The lab calculators gather the common ones into a single floating moda...

The calculators live behind a small floating beaker button that sits in the corner of the app. Clicking it opens the calculators modal over whatever you are working on; closing it returns you exactly where you were. The modal is a scratchpad, not a record. It does not write to...

The modal carries one tab per calculator. Each tab is self-contained, so you fill in the fields it needs and the result appears below, updating on every keystroke. Most tabs accept values with a unit selector (nM, uM, mM, M for concentration; uL, mL, L for volume; ng, ug, mg, ...

A general-purpose scientific calculator for the arithmetic that does not fit the other tabs. Type an expression directly or use the on-screen keypad. It handles the usual operators plus sine, cosine, tangent and their inverses, the natural log and log base 10, square root, pow...

The weigh-out calculator. Enter a molecular weight, a target concentration, and a volume, and it gives the mass to weigh out, using n = m / MW and C = n / V. Enter a mass instead and it goes the other way, giving the moles and the resulting concentration. This is the everyday ...

The C1 V1 = C2 V2 calculator. Enter your stock concentration, the final concentration you want, and the final volume, and it solves for how much stock to add and how much diluent to top up with. It flags the case where the final concentration is higher than the stock, which us...

Builds a serial-dilution table. Give a starting concentration, a fold factor per step, a number of steps, and a per-tube final volume, and it lays out each tube with the concentration at that step, the volume of sample to carry over from the previous tube, and the volume of di...

The primer melting-temperature calculator. Paste a DNA or RNA sequence and it reports the length, the GC content, and the melting temperature using a nearest-neighbor model (the SantaLucia parameters that IDT OligoAnalyzer, Primer3, NEB, Benchling, and SnapGene use). Only the ...

Two nucleic-acid conversions in one tab. The mass-to-moles section turns a mass and a length into an amount, choosing the right average molecular weight for double-stranded DNA, single-stranded DNA, or RNA. The A260 section turns a spectrophotometer reading and a dilution fact...
