Paste an amino-acid sequence and it reports the same physico-chemical properties the ExPASy ProtParam tool reports, including the average molecular weight, the isoelectric point (pI), the molar extinction coefficient at 280 nm and the corresponding absorbance of a 0.1 percent ...

Build a multi-component buffer. Set the total volume, then add a row per component with its final concentration and its stock concentration. For each component it gives the volume of stock to add, using volume = (final concentration x total volume) / stock concentration, and i...

The calculators above are fixed, and the math you do in them is scratch. Plenty of bench math is too specific to ship for everyone, though. A spore suspension from five hemocytometer counts, a master mix scaled to your reaction count, a dosing volume from an animal's weight. S...

Open the Lab calculators modal and, alongside the built-in tabs, you will find your own calculators, a Build your own button, and a template library. A calculator you build is just inputs and a formula. You name the things you measure, you write the answer as a formula using t...

The first time, a short wizard walks you through it one plain question at a time. What does this work out, what do you measure, what is the formula, any warnings to add, and what is the answer called. It assembles the calculator for you, with the answer updating live on the fo...

Once you have built one, Build your own opens a single form instead, with the same pieces laid out together. You can switch between the wizard and the form at any time, and your work carries across. The form keeps the simple case simple, the optional pieces (intermediate steps...

You rarely need to start from a blank page. The template library carries ready-made calculators grouped by field, including spore concentration, CFU per mL from plate counts, OD600 to cell density, qPCR amplification efficiency, and doubling time. Open the closest one, change ...

An input is a box you type a measurement into. You name it in plain words and the builder gives it a short name to use in formulas, so labelling one Average spore count lets you reference it as avgCount. An input can be a single number, a dropdown of choices, or a replicate li...

Guidance is the optional safety net. You write a condition and a message, and the message only appears when the condition is true. A spore calculator can warn you when the count is too low to be reliable, a qPCR calculator can flag an efficiency outside the accepted range. The...

A calculator you build is yours until you share it. Share it with your lab and everyone in your lab folder can run it as a live reference, so they always see your latest and you stay the only editor. Share it with someone outside your folder and a copy travels to them over the...

If you build a calculator others would find useful, you can submit it to the public template library. It opens a pre-filled submission on our GitHub for a maintainer to review, and accepted calculators ship in a later release for everyone to start from. It is reviewed rather t...

The calculators are a scratchpad. They run entirely in your browser, they do not touch your data folder, and they keep no history. When you close the modal, the values are gone. If you want a number on the record, copy it into a note, an experiment, or a method protocol.

The bench calculators are checked against exact algebra and cited reference constants, so the numbers are not estimates. The Primer Tm tab is a faithful port of Biopython's nearest-neighbor model (validated against Biopython directly), and the Protein properties tab is a faith...

Build a calculator on your laptop and it is in your folder, on your paired phone, and (if you share it) in your labmates' libraries, with no extra steps. Fix a formula once and the fix reaches everyone who has the lab-shared version, because they read your copy rather than hol...

Your calculators run on the same expression engine the built-in tabs use, so the math is the hardened, tested layer rather than a loose evaluator. The engine, and how our calculations reproduce reference values and published results, is shown on the Transparency page .
The Lab calculators modal. Tabs across the top switch calculators; results update live as you type. Nothing here is saved.

The Lab calculators modal open over the app, showing a row of tabs (Scientific, Molarity, Dilution, Serial dilution, Primer Tm, DNA / RNA, Protein properties, Buffer recipe) and the Molarity calculator's input fields and result card below.
First time through, a wizard asks one plain question at a time and assembles the calculator for you.
The build-your-own calculator wizard asking one plain-language question, with a live answer shown below the formula field.
The form view. Name your inputs, write the result formula (the chips above it drop a variable in), and the result computes live.
The build-your-own calculator form, with named inputs, clickable variable chips above the result formula, a live result, and an Advanced section for steps and guidance.
The template library. Start from a proven calculator and make it yours.
The calculator template library, a gallery of ready-made calculators grouped by field with a Use this action on each card.

entries.title: Browse lab experiments
headings: Editing is deliberate, not the default
bodySnippets: Lab experiments at /lab-experiments is the lab head's window into every member's experiments at once. It's a read-first surface. You open any experiment to review it, and if you need to fix something you can edit it as the lab head, with the change still recorded against the m...

A member's own{" "} Workbench only shows their work. A PI usually needs the opposite, the whole lab in one list, so they can check on an experiment without asking the member to share it or knowing which project it lives under. Browse lab experiments is that list. It pulls ever...

The page is gated on the lab head (PI) account type. If a member or solo researcher lands on the URL directly, they're sent back home before any lab data loads, so there's no way to reach another person's experiments through this route without the PI role. For the role itself ...
The list shows a card per experiment across the whole lab. Opening one is a two-stage idea, look first, then edit only if you mean to.
For the lab head's landing page that this surface hangs off, see{" "} Lab Overview .
For the same idea applied to written notes, see Browse lab notes .
For the experiment popup itself (Details, Lab Notes, Method, Results), see the Workbench .
For the PI role and how lab-head writes are recorded, see PI .

Open it read-only. Clicking a card opens the full experiment in the same popup the Workbench uses, with Details, Lab Notes, Method, and Results, but in review mode. Nothing you do here changes the record, so you can read a member's work freely.

Edit as the lab head. If you need to correct something, the popup offers an Edit as lab head button. Clicking it opens a one-time confirmation dialog for that record. After you confirm, that specific record is freely editable for the rest of the browser session without being a...

The change stays the member's. Your edit is written back to the member who owns the experiment, not copied to you, and it's captured in the audit trail so there's a record of the lab head touching someone else's work.

Navigate directly to , or follow the link from the PI pages. It is not a tab on the member Workbench, and a member or solo researcher never sees it. The Lab Overview itself does not have a browse link-out button; those were retired when the Lab Overview was redesigned.

Opening an experiment never puts it in an editable state on its own. You review by default and have to click Edit as lab head and confirm, so a PI skimming the lab cannot change a member's record by accident. Each distinct record gets its own confirmation the first time you ed...

entries.title: PI (Principal Investigator)
headings: The six phases of the PI feature
bodySnippets: A PI is a per-user role. It is an account flagged with account_type === 'lab_head' that gains a small superset of affordances over a regular member. PI is not a separate user pool, it is a property on a normal account. One or more lab members fill this role. The role unlocks t...

The role is a single flag on a user's metadata. That flag is , with values (the default) or . A lab folder can have one or more accounts flagged as PI, and co-PI labs set the flag on every PI. A PI is otherwise a normal account, with their own projects, tasks, and notes. The r...
A lab head has access to several surfaces that are not visible to members.
The PI role shipped in six tracked phases, with a capability revamp that landed in June 2026.

On the user picker, the PI shows up with a small badge and is pinned to the top of the list regardless of which account is set as the main user. This makes it easy to find the PI account on a shared lab laptop. The badge is also visible on comment rows so members can tell when...
Set one account as the PI in these cases.
Skip the PI (every account stays ) in these cases.

Lab Overview at . The PI's landing page. Includes the What needs you hero (amber count tiles for pending approvals, flagged records, overdue tasks, and @-mentions), the lab stat strip, the announcement composer, the lab activity feed, and the People snapshot. See Lab Overview .

Approvals at . A unified queue of pending purchase approvals and flagged records. Accessible from the Lab Overview hero tiles or directly by URL. PI-only; a non-PI loading this URL is redirected home.
People at . The full lab roster with workload, IDP, and member management. The People snapshot on the Lab Overview links here.
Browse lab experiments and Browse lab notes. Cross-lab views of every member's experiments and notes. See Browse lab experiments and Browse lab notes .
Audit trail. Opened from the Audit trail button on the Lab Overview header. Shows per-field edit history across member records. See Audit log .
Account type and comment attribution. Introduced , the picker badge, and the "posted by PI" styling on comment rows.
Comment threading and @-mentions. Built out the comment surface across tasks, notes, and purchases, with one-level threading and the denormalized mentions index. See Comments .
Soft-write actions. Purchase approval, task assignment, and flag-for-review affordances. See Soft-write actions .
Cross-lab metrics. Member workload, recent activity, burn rate. The data feeds the Lab Overview page.
Audit log. Every soft-write field change appends to . The per-record confirm dialog (no password, no timer) replaced an older password gate in June 2026. See Edit as lab head and Audit log .
User archiving. Remove a former member from active views while preserving their data. See User archiving .
Your lab has more than two or three people and you want a single person triaging purchases instead of everyone approving their own.
You need a forensic trail of who approved what and who changed which field on which record.
You want the curated Lab Overview with the What needs you hero, the stat strip, and the People snapshot instead of only your own home page.
The lab is a single person, or two people who trust each other to manage their own purchases.
You do not need the audit log or the approval queue.
Soft-write actions walks the approval queue, task assignment, and flag-for-review.
Edit as lab head covers the per-record confirm-once gate.
Audit log covers and the trail viewer.
The Lab Overview dashboard is gated on the PI role.

A PI has implicit read access to every record in the lab, regardless of . The permission system still respects explicit sharing arrays for writes, but for reads the PI sees everything. That is what makes the Lab Overview dashboards possible. See Sharing and permissions for the...
The PI is pinned to the top of the user picker and carries a small role badge.
The user-picker showing two accounts, the PI pinned at the top with a small badge next to their avatar.

entries.title: Audit log
bodySnippets: Every time a lab head edits a member's record, one row per changed field is appended to that member's audit file. The audit trail is the forensic record of cross-member edits. Open it from the Audit trail button on the Lab Overview header to see who changed what, when, and wha...

Click the Audit trail button in the top-right of the Lab Overview page. The button is always visible and does not require any special state to be active. The viewer opens as a popup. If no target member is selected, the viewer shows a member picker first; select a member to lo...
Each audit entry corresponds to one field change in one record. A single save that touches three fields writes three entries. Here is what each entry contains.

Soft-write edits to a member's records are written to inside that member's folder. Announcement writes (post, edit, delete) go to a separate at the lab folder root, because announcements are lab-scoped rather than owned by any single member.
Both files use the same entry schema. The viewer reads the per-user file for member record edits. The files are append-only on the write side. The reader is free to sort and filter.
actor : the lab head username who made the edit.
target_user : the member whose record was changed.
record_type : a string identifying the kind of record, for example , , or .
record_id : the numeric or string id of the specific record that was changed.
field_path : a dot-separated path through the record shape identifying which field changed, for example , , or .
old_value : the value before the change.
new_value : the value after the change.
session_id : a synthetic grouping id stamped on related entries from the same action, for example . Not a timed session, just a label that connects rows that came from the same operation.
timestamp : ISO 8601 UTC.
A purchase shows up as approved and the member wants to confirm who signed off and when. Filter by and the field path.
A task was reassigned and the original assignment needs to be confirmed. Look for the field path on the relevant task id.
A note was edited by the lab head and the original text needs to be recovered. The on the body field carries the pre-edit text verbatim.
The audit schema does not have generic or summary fields. The record type plus the field path together describe what changed. An approval, for example, appears as a row with and / .

Like every other ResearchOS file, the audit log sits in plaintext on disk. Anyone with read access to the lab folder can open it directly. The purpose of the log is accountability, not protection against an adversary on the file system. See Security for the data-at-rest story.

The writer never modifies an existing row. A lab head cannot quietly erase or rewrite an old audit entry through the app. If a change needs to be corrected (re-approving a wrongly-declined purchase, for example), the correction writes a new row alongside the original, so both ...
The Audit trail viewer. Each row is one field change, with the before and after values shown inline.
The Audit trail viewer popup showing a list of per-field edit rows with actor, record type, field path, old value, new value, and timestamp columns.

entries.title: Edit as lab head
headings: One confirmation per record, not one unlock for all
bodySnippets: A lab head can read and edit any member's records. To prevent an accidental keystroke from changing someone else's work, the first time you edit a given record the app asks for one confirmation. After you confirm, that record stays freely editable for the rest of the browser s...

When a lab head opens a record they did not create, the record opens in read-only review mode. An Edit as lab head button appears in the header (amber, with a pencil icon). Clicking it opens a small confirmation dialog that names the member whose record you are about to change...

Once you confirm, the confirmation is remembered for that specific record for the rest of the browser session. The dialog does not appear again if you close and reopen the same record. A page reload or a user switch clears the memory, so the next session always starts fresh.

After confirming, the record becomes editable exactly as if it were your own. Your edits are written back to the member's folder, not copied to you, so ownership does not change. Each field you change appends a row to that member's file so the change leaves a complete record. ...

Before June 2026 the lab-head edit path used a separate PI password and a shared 5-minute timed session that unlocked every soft-write affordance at once. That gate was removed. A logged-in lab head is already authenticated through the normal account sign-in, so a password pro...
Soft-write actions covers the purchase approval, task assignment, and flag-for-review affordances.
Audit log covers the file and how to open the trail viewer.
Browse lab experiments and Browse lab notes are the main surfaces where a PI reads and edits member records.
The gate is keyed by record, not by a shared session. Confirming on one member's note does not unlock a different member's purchase. Each record gets its own confirmation the first time you edit it.

entries.title: Soft-write actions
headings: The confirm-once gate on member record edits
bodySnippets: A soft-write is a PI action that touches a record owned by someone else, like approving a member's purchase, assigning a member to a task, or flagging a note for review. Each action writes a row to the audit log so cross-member writes stay accountable. Posting a lab announceme...
Pending purchases appear on the Approvals page at , reachable from the What needs you hero on the Lab Overview. Each item in the queue has two inline controls.
A declined purchase can be re-approved from the same Approvals page. This is useful when the original decline reason resolves without forcing the member to re-create the purchase from scratch.

On any task popup the PI sees an Assign to member control above the description. Pick a member from the dropdown and the task's field updates. The audit row captures the actor (the PI), the record id, and the old and new assignee via the .
Assigning a task does not move ownership. The original creator still owns the file on disk. The assignment surfaces on the assignee's home page and in the member workload panel on the Lab Overview.

A PI can flag any task, note, or purchase as needing review. The affordance is a Flag button on the host record popup. Flagged items appear in the Lab Overview What needs you hero and on the Approvals page . The member also sees a flag stripe on their own copy of the record. T...

Announcements are a lab-wide PI broadcast covered on their own page. Unlike the soft-writes above, posting an announcement does not touch any member's record. A logged-in PI posts directly from the composer on the Lab Overview. See Announcements .

Soft-writes that change a member's record go through a one-time confirm dialog per record. The first time you edit a given record in a browser session the app asks whether you mean to edit that member's work. After you confirm, that record stays freely editable for the rest of...
Approve. Flips the purchase's status to approved and writes an audit row. The member sees the approval on their own Purchases page on the next refresh.
Decline. Flips the status to declined and stamps a timestamp. A appears wherever that purchase is displayed.

Soft-writes never overwrite a member's own work without their knowledge. The member always sees the result on their own page. The PI signals, rather than silently taking over. If a member's records need to be handed off entirely, the path is to archive the user (see User archi...
The Approvals page at /approvals. Pending purchases and flagged records appear here with inline approve, decline, and resolve controls.
The /approvals page showing a queue of pending purchase items, each with inline Approve and Decline buttons, plus a flagged items section.

entries.title: Lab Inbox
bodySnippets: The /lab-inbox URL is a redirect to /lab-overview. There is no separate Lab Inbox popup or tool. Comments and @-mentions surface inside the record popups where they were posted, and lab-wide announcements from the PI appear in the Announcements section of the Lab Overview page.

Comments are attached to the record they were written on, not gathered into a shared inbox view. Open a task, note, or purchase and the comments rail opens on the right side of the popup. The PI badge, the author name, and the full thread all appear in context next to the reco...

When someone @-mentions you in a comment, the mention pushes a notification to your bell. On the Lab Overview, the What needs you hero shows a count of unread @-mentions and links you to the notes surface so you can find the relevant records. The mention is always readable in ...

Lab-wide announcements from the PI live on the Lab Overview page in the Post an announcement section. PIs compose and post directly from that card. Members see the announcement stream when they visit the Lab Overview or when an announcement notification lands in their bell. Se...
Comments covers threading, mentions, and the in-record rail.
Announcements covers posting, pinning, and editing.
Lab Overview is where both surfaces live on the PI's landing page.
Any bookmark, notification deep-link, or external URL pointing to automatically redirects to . The route was renamed when the surface grew beyond a simple inbox into the full lab command center.

entries.title: Announcements
headings: Audit lives at the lab root
bodySnippets: Announcements are the PI's broadcast channel. They are short posts every member can see, pinnable to the top of the stream. Members read. PIs compose and post directly from the Lab Overview, with no password prompt and no timed session to unlock first.

Posting is PI only. The compose form does not render for member accounts, and the underlying write is rejected for any account without . Reading is open to every member. Everyone in the lab sees the same announcement stream.

The composer lives in the Post an announcement section on the Lab Overview page. A logged-in PI can type and post immediately. There is no password and no edit-session unlock. The PI's account sign-in is the only gate.
Once an announcement is published, the author has three actions on it.
Only the original author can edit or delete an announcement. A second PI cannot edit another PI's post. They can post their own follow-up.

The composer persists in-progress text to as you type. The draft is keyed by the PI's username, so each PI keeps their own draft independently of any other PI. The draft restores automatically when the composer mounts again in the same browser session. It does not survive a fr...

Announcement writes (post, edit, delete, pin toggle) can append a row to the lab-root audit file at . Because announcements are lab-scoped rather than owned by any single member, the audit entry lives at the lab root rather than in a per-user folder. See Audit log for the entr...
Announcements and comments are both async, but they serve different purposes.
Type the message in the text area. Drafts are persisted to as you type, so an accidental tab close or refresh does not lose an in-progress announcement.
Optionally check Pin to top to keep the announcement at the head of the stream regardless of post date.
Click Post announcement . The post goes out immediately and every lab member receives a notification.
Pin / Unpin. A pinned announcement floats to the top of the stream regardless of post date. Unpinning lets it slide back into chronological order.
Edit. Opens the composer pre-filled with the current text. Save to update. When the text changes, every recipient's notification preview refreshes to match the new body.
Delete. Removes the announcement after a native confirm prompt. Recipients' notification rows for that announcement are also cleaned up so the bell count stays accurate.

Post an announcement when the message is for the whole lab, should persist for at least a few days, and benefits from being on the lab's shared front page. Examples include a freezer outage notice, a lab meeting rescheduled, or an equipment ordering deadline.

Post a comment when the message is about a specific record. The comment stays tied to its host, appears in context inside the record popup, and does not need to appear on the front page. See Comments .

Unlike edits to member records, which write to each member's own , announcement audit entries go to at the lab folder root. There is no per-user announcement audit view because announcements are inherently lab-wide.
The announcement composer on the Lab Overview. Type a message, optionally pin it, and post. No unlock step required.
The Post an announcement section on the Lab Overview page, showing a textarea with a draft message, a Pin to top checkbox, and a Post announcement button.

entries.title: Comments
bodySnippets: Comments are short asynchronous messages attached to a record (a task, a note, or a purchase). They keep a conversation tied to the thing it is about, so a question about a PCR recipe lives on that PCR task, not in a separate chat channel. One level of reply nesting keeps thre...
Three record types accept comments today.

Open any of those records and comments live in a docked rail on the right side of the popup, the way Google Docs and Word park comments beside the document instead of below it. The thread sits next to the record you are reading, so you keep the recipe or the note in view while...

Each record popup has a comment button in its header. Click it to slide the rail open on the right. When a record already has comments, the button wears a small count badge so you can tell at a glance whether a thread is waiting before you open anything.

The comments rail and the version-history rail share the same docked spot, so they are mutually exclusive . Opening one closes the other. Press Escape to close whichever rail is open, which drops you back to the full-width record without closing the popup itself.

You can also start a comment without opening the record first. Right-click an experiment card or a note card and choose Add a comment (or View / add comment when a thread already exists). That opens the record with the comments rail already expanded and the cursor in the compo...

Comments support a single level of reply nesting. A top-level comment can have replies; a reply cannot have its own reply. This keeps a thread legible at a glance and prevents the rabbit-hole shape that threaded chat platforms drift into. If a reply itself needs a deeper conve...
Type inside a comment to summon the mention picker. Pick a lab member from the dropdown and the editor inserts a mention chip. The mention does two things.

Members see their @-mentions in the bell (the personal notification queue). On the Lab Overview, the What needs you hero shows a live count of @-mentions for the PI and links to the notes surface where the relevant records live.

Comments live on the record they were written on. To find a comment, open the record it belongs to and the comments rail will show the thread. When a bell notification links you to a comment, tapping it opens the host record directly with the rail expanded.

Comments inherit the read permission of their host record. If you have read access to a task that a labmate owns, you also see (and can post) comments on it. PIs have implicit read access to everything, so they see every comment in the lab. Members only see comments on the rec...
Tasks (experiments, lists, purchase orders).
Notes (free-form lab notes outside an experiment).
Purchases (individual line items inside a purchase order).
Renders in the comment body as a styled chip (the member's name, in the member's color).
Pushes the member's id onto the comment's array, a denormalized field that the inbox uses to filter for "comments I was mentioned in."

Earlier prototypes filtered mentions by regex-matching the comment body. That fell apart with edits, mention chip styling, and special characters. The denormalized array is now the only source of truth for "was X mentioned." The body rendering is independent, so it can change ...

Comments are the asynchronous-chat layer of ResearchOS. They are low-friction and in-context, with no hard notification unless you @-mention someone. If you need someone to see something right now, mention them and the bell pings. Otherwise, drop the comment and trust that the...
Comments dock in a rail on the right of the record, the way Google Docs and Word park comments beside the document. The header comment button carries a count badge when a thread is waiting.
An experiment popup with the comments rail docked open on the right, showing a threaded conversation with one reply indented, while the experiment dependency chain stays in view on the left.

entries.title: Browse lab notes
headings: Editing is deliberate, not the default
bodySnippets: Lab notes at /lab-notes is the lab head's window into every member's notes at once. It works the same way as Browse lab experiments. You open any note to read it, and if you need to fix something you can edit it as the lab head, with the change still recorded against the membe...

Notes are where a lot of the lab's day-to-day record actually lives, recipes, measurements, plasmid names, the running log of what happened at the bench. A member's own notes sit on their Workbench. A PI often wants to read across the whole lab's notes without hunting through ...

The page is gated on the lab head (PI) account type. A member or solo researcher who lands on the URL directly is sent back home before any lab data loads, so the whole-lab notes view is the PI's alone. For the role itself, see PI .
The list shows every member's notes. Opening one follows the same look-first, edit-only-if-you-mean-to flow as experiments.
For the lab head's landing page this surface hangs off, see{" "} Lab Overview .
For the same idea applied to experiments, see Browse lab experiments .
For how notes themselves work and the editor behind them, see the Workbench and the Markdown editor .
For the PI role and how lab-head writes are recorded, see PI .
Open it read-only. Clicking a note opens it in the note popup in review mode. You can read any member's note without changing it.

Edit as the lab head. If you need to correct something, the popup offers an Edit as lab head button. Clicking it opens a one-time confirmation dialog for that record. After you confirm, that specific record is freely editable for the rest of the browser session without being a...

The change stays the member's. Your edit is written back to the member who owns the note, not copied to you, and it's captured in the audit trail so the lab head touching someone else's note leaves a record.

Navigate directly to , or follow the link from the PI pages. It is the notes counterpart to Browse lab experiments . The Lab Overview itself does not have a browse link-out button; those were retired when the Lab Overview was redesigned.

Opening a note never puts it in an editable state on its own. You read by default and have to click Edit as lab head and confirm, so skimming the lab's notes cannot change a member's record by accident. Each distinct record gets its own confirmation the first time you edit it.

entries.title: Lab Overview
headings: One fixed page, not a canvas you build
Sections that are no longer on this page
bodySnippets: The Lab Overview at /lab-overview is the PI's landing page. It is a fixed, curated, action-first view of the whole lab, laid out the same way for every PI. The things that need a decision sit at the top. The ambient lab context sits below. There is nothing to configure or rear...

Lab Overview is gated on the lab head (PI) account type. A PI lands here automatically on sign-in. Lab members and solo researchers never see it. They land on the Workbench instead. The two starting points answer two different questions: the Workbench asks "what is on my plate...
The top section of the page is titled What needs you . It shows up to four amber or rose count tiles, one per category of outstanding item.
When all counts are zero the hero collapses to a single calm green "You're all caught up" line.
Immediately below the hero is a compact stat strip showing four counts across the whole lab.

Below the stat strip is the announcement composer. A PI types a message, optionally pins it, and clicks Post. There is no password prompt and no timer. A member viewing the same page sees the published announcements in read-only mode. See Announcements for the full posting, pi...

The lower portion of the page uses a two-column grid on wide screens. The main column holds the Lab activity feed, a running cross-lab log of recent experiments, notes, and tasks. The narrower column holds a People snapshot showing workload across lab members, with a View all ...

Two header buttons sit in the top-right of the page. The Audit trail button opens the AuditTrailViewer popup, a read-only window onto the per-field edit history a lab head has produced across member records. The New Project button opens the project creation modal directly, so ...
Approvals. Pending purchase approvals. Links to .
Flagged. Records you have flagged for review that have not been resolved. Links to .
Overdue. Incomplete tasks across the lab that are past their due date. Links to .
Mentions. Comments in shared notes where you were @-mentioned. Links to .
Total lab members.
Active experiments (incomplete experiments across the lab).
Open tasks (all incomplete tasks).
Overdue tasks (incomplete tasks past their due date).
For the PI role itself, see PI .
For the pending-approval queue, see Soft-write actions and the page.
For the People page and workload view, see the PI page.
Announcements are documented at Announcements .
For the audit trail viewer, see Audit log .

Lab Overview used to be a customizable widget canvas with draggable tiles and an add-widget palette. That is gone. The page is now a single curated layout that is identical for every PI. There is nothing to add, remove, drag, or configure.

Earlier versions of Lab Overview included a Today's events calendar panel, a Trainee notes and goals section, a full announcement-history stream, and a set of Browse link-out buttons. All of these have been removed. Calendar events stay on the personal Calendar tab. Browse lab...
The What needs you hero. Each tile is a live count that links directly to where that queue lives.
The What needs you section at the top of Lab Overview, showing amber and rose tiles for pending approvals, flagged records, overdue tasks, and @-mentions, each as a clickable count tile.
The lab stat strip. Four live counts give a snapshot of the lab state without requiring any clicks.
The compact stat strip below the hero, showing four cells: member count, active experiments count, open tasks count, and overdue task count, separated by vertical dividers.
The announcement composer. PIs type and post directly. Members see announcements in this same section in read mode.
The Post an announcement section showing a textarea and a Post announcement button, with a Pin to top checkbox on the left.

entries.title: Links
headings: Q7 opt-in, Links is only visible if you enabled it
Private by default, shareable per link
bodySnippets: Your own bookmark wall, link cards with preview images, grouped into categories, stored under your user. Keep a link to yourself, or share it with the whole lab.

Each saved link is a card with a preview image (or a solid colored block if no image), the link title, a short description, and the hostname. When a link has a category, the card shows it as a small badge in the top-left corner of the preview. Cards are grouped into category s...

Hover a card. Two small icons appear in the top-right corner of the preview image, a pencil to edit (reopens the same form pre-filled), and a trash can to delete (prompts for confirmation). The hostname line at the bottom of each card is auto-derived from the URL. You only eve...

When someone sets a link to Whole lab , it shows up on your Links page too, carrying their avatar and a Shared by label so you can tell it apart from your own. The pencil and trash icons stay hidden on a shared-in card, only the person who owns it can edit or remove it, or swi...

Per the on-disk JSON, a link is a title, a URL, an optional description, an optional category, a color, and an optional preview image URL, plus who owns it and whether it's shared with the whole lab. There's no labmate ordering. Links you keep private stay in your own folder, ...
Click Add Link in the top right of the page. A form expands inline below the button.

Paste a URL into the URL field. Click the small image-icon button next to it and, if the title is still blank, it seeds the title with the site's hostname (for example addgene.org ). It does this in your browser, with no server call, so nothing is sent off and no description o...

Give the link a Title (required), pick or type a Category (the dropdown suggests Protocol, Database, Tool, Reference, Supplier, Publication, Software, Other, but any text works), and pick one of the eight color swatches.

Set Visibility . Just me (the default) keeps the link private to you. Whole lab puts it on every lab member's Links page, where they can see and open it. You stay the owner, so only you can edit or remove it.
Optionally add a Description and override the Preview Image URL with a custom image.
Click Create Link . The new card appears in its category.
The Links tab only appears if you answered "Yes" to the setup wizard question about storing bookmarks (Q7). If you don't see the tab, open Settings and re-run the setup wizard to enable it.
The color swatch only appears when no preview image is set. If you supply both a color and a preview image URL, the image takes precedence and the color is not shown.

Links sit in your own folder under , so each labmate keeps their own wall. A new link is private to you. If you want, you can flip a single link to Whole lab so everyone in the lab sees it on their own page. There's no all-or-nothing switch, you choose this link by link.
Link cards grouped by category. Each card opens its URL in a new tab when clicked.
The Links page showing link cards grouped into category sections, each card with a preview image and a title underneath.

entries.title: The Markdown Editor
headings: One surface, type markdown and watch it render
What a real ResearchOS note looks like
No metadata popup on file tiles
bodySnippets: Wherever you write prose in ResearchOS (experiment notes, task descriptions, results write-ups, methods bodies, free-form notes), you're using the same markdown editor. It's one live writing surface where you type plain markdown and watch it render as you go. Learning its tool...
The same editor opens in every place you write more than a sentence in ResearchOS.

Everything you type is plain markdown. The toolbar buttons just insert the same characters you'd type by hand (e.g., , ), so opening any of these files in a normal text editor outside ResearchOS gets you the same content.

There's nothing to configure and no block to click into. The editor is a single, continuous writing surface, like a normal document. You type plain markdown and the editor renders it live around your cursor. Headings look like headings, bold looks bold, and images show as imag...

The key is what happens at your cursor. Markdown markers (the around bold, the on a heading, a link target) stay hidden while you read, so the line looks finished. Move your caret onto that line and the markers reveal themselves, ready to edit; move away and they tuck back beh...
Every editor has a single toolbar along the top. From left to right it carries these controls.
Use Cmd on macOS and Ctrl on Windows and Linux anywhere this table says Cmd .

Images and files live in one place, a single attachment strip along the bottom of the editor, toggled with the toolbar's Attachments button. A small Images / Files tab bar above the strip switches it between image thumbnails and file tiles. There's no separate Files panel and ...
There are four ways to get a new image in.

Every image lands in an folder adjacent to the document on disk (e.g., ), and the markdown body references it with . Filenames with spaces render inline just fine. The reference looks like and the editor resolves it to the right file without any extra escaping.

The Images tab in the bottom strip lists every image in the document's folder as a row of thumbnails, whether or not the body references the image yet. Images already referenced look normal. Images that exist on disk but aren't referenced yet (e.g., a fresh arrival from the co...
Click any rendered image and a size popover appears inline with 25% , 50% , 75% , and 100% options. The choice writes a width attribute into the markdown so the size persists.

If an image reference points at a file that no longer exists (e.g., you renamed the file outside the app, or the relink hasn't synced from a teammate yet), a small red "Image Not Found" popup appears in the bottom-right corner of the editor. It lists similar-named files in the...

Anything that isn't an image (PDFs, CSVs, sequence files, protocols, archives) drops into a sibling folder and shows up as a clickable hyperlink in the prose, not as an inline preview. Switch the bottom strip to its Files tab to manage them. The add flow mirrors images, so you...
Click any link in Edit or Preview mode and a small View / Download popup appears centered on screen.

When a link points at a file that isn't in the document's folder, the same red corner popup that handles broken images opens with a File Not Found heading. There's no similar-name search for files (the recovery is usually to remove the dead link), so the popup goes straight to...
A typical note in a working lab is mostly tables, measurements, and small annotations. The four examples below are the shape to aim for.
Standard GitHub-flavored markdown all works.
The editor includes a collapsible helper panel on the left side with two tabs at the top, Shortcuts and Style Guide . Click the arrow in the panel header to collapse or expand it.

The editor doesn't autosave. You save explicitly, and every save is a checkpoint , a permanent, revertible version of the document. Click Save checkpoint in the toolbar (or press Cmd + S ) to write your edits to disk and record a version you can come back to.

Next to the save button, the Version history button opens a docked sidebar listing every checkpoint, newest first, grouped by day. Select a version and the editor body flips to a read-only diff view showing exactly what changed between that checkpoint and the one before it (ad...

If you have write access, the sidebar footer offers a Restore action. Restoring writes the chosen version back as the current content and records it as a fresh checkpoint, so the restore is itself revertible. You can always roll forward again to the pre-restore state. The time...

On save, the editor also runs a quick cleanup pass over the document's and folders: anything sitting on disk that nothing in the markdown points at gets deleted, so deleted snippets don't leave dangling files behind. The sweep matches links the way the body writes them, so URL...
See Version History for the full picture of how checkpoints, diffs, and restore work across ResearchOS.

A lone link on its own line renders as a live card instead of a plain hyperlink. Trees, sequences, molecules, and notes each have their own card style. The card pulls from the same source the full viewer does, so a tree embed is the same rendering engine as the Tree Studio, no...

You get an embed link whenever you insert a reference from the @mention picker and it resolves to a block-embed type. The link is still valid markdown outside ResearchOS, it just opens as a normal link in a text editor. Inside ResearchOS, the renderer at detects the scheme and...
The Lab Notes tab of any experiment popup (see Experiments & Notes ).
The Description field on the task detail popup, which you can open from the Gantt , the left sidebar, search results, or the calendar.
The Results tab inside the Workbench project view (the old standalone Results page was retired; now redirects to Workbench ).
The body of every method in the Methods library .
Free-form notes (running logs and standalone notes).
The Edit | Preview toggle (covered above).
Focus mode . The expand glyph drops the editor into a full-screen distraction-free writing view (also Cmd + Shift + F ).
Add Image (or Add File on surfaces that accept any file type) to pick attachments from disk.
Browse to insert an image already attached to this record without re-uploading.
Attachments to show or hide the bottom attachment strip (see Attachments ).

On surfaces that own their own save (the experiment popup's Lab Notes / Results tabs, the task popup), the Version history button and the Save checkpoint button ride at the right end of this same bar (see Saving ).
Click the toolbar's Add Image button to pick one or more files from disk.

Drag image files into the editor body . While you're dragging from Finder, a blue ring lights up around the popup or editor card so you know the drop will be caught. Release inside text and the image inserts at that position. Release outside text and it appends to the bottom o...
Paste from the clipboard . Copy a screenshot (e.g., macOS Cmd + Shift + 4 , then Cmd + V in the editor) and it lands inline.
Click Browse to pick from images already attached to this record (the gallery picker). Useful when the image is already on disk and you just want to insert a reference without re-uploading.

Click a thumbnail to open the image metadata popup. The popup shows a larger preview and lets you edit the caption, rename the file, delete it from disk, or jump to where it's used in the body. If the image isn't referenced anywhere yet, the jump button is disabled. The popup ...
Drag a thumbnail into the editor to insert that image at the cursor position. The thumbnail stays in the strip so the same image can appear twice in the document.

Drag a thumbnail to the trash zone at the bottom of the editor. ResearchOS deletes the file from disk and removes every reference in the body. The trash zone only appears while you are actively dragging a thumbnail.

Each tile shows an icon for the file type and the filename. Files already linked in the body look normal. Files that exist on disk but aren't referenced yet show a small blue dot and an "unlinked" count in the tab header.
Drag a tile into the editor to insert a link to it at the cursor.

Drag a file tile to the red trash zone at the bottom-right of the editor. ResearchOS asks for confirmation, then deletes the file from disk and strips every link to it from the markdown body, including links that stored the filename URL-encoded (so gets cleaned up when the und...

Text-like files (markdown, txt, csv, json, code, sequence files like fasta and gbk) get a popup with Cancel , Download , and View buttons. Click View and the contents render in an inline monospace viewer with its own Download button up top. Click Download from either spot and ...
PDFs get the same popup. Click View and ResearchOS opens the PDF in a new browser tab so you can use the browser's built-in PDF viewer. Click Download and a copy lands on disk.
Everything else (zips, office docs, audio, video, binaries) downloads immediately without a popup. There's nothing meaningful to render inline.
Tables with the -and- syntax.
Bulleted ( ) and numbered ( ) lists.
Task lists with and . Boxes are clickable in Edit and Preview.
Blockquotes ( ).
Horizontal rules ( ).
Inline code with single backticks and code blocks with triple backticks.
Shortcuts lists every keyboard shortcut the editor responds to, with the key combo on the right. Read-only.

Style Guide shows example syntax for every markdown feature (headings, lists, tables, code blocks, callouts). Click any example to insert it at the cursor. Handy when you've forgotten the table syntax or want to see what callout markdown looks like.

Undo / redo across the whole document . The editor maintains its own undo stack. Cmd + Z steps back through edits, including image drops and deletions. Cmd + Shift + Z (or Ctrl + Y on Windows) steps forward.
Promote/demote a heading in place with Cmd + Alt + + or Cmd + Alt + - . You don't need to retype the marks.

entries.title: Template Library
headings: What a template is, and why
Verify any number against the original
Not every template, and that is honest
bodySnippets: A blank protocol is a slow way to start. The Template Library ships a set of pre-built, ready-to-use protocols, from a Q5 PCR recipe to a 384-well viability plate to an LC-MS run, that you can drop straight into your Methods library and edit. Many of them carry the vendor PDF ...

A template is a starting point for a method . It is a complete, structured protocol someone has already written, with the right method type (PCR, plate layout, LC gradient, free-form markdown), the reagent table filled in, the cycling program set, and the steps written out. Wh...

The point is twofold. First comes speed. You should not have to retype a standard agarose-gel procedure or rebuild a colony-PCR program from memory every time. Second, and just as important, comes fidelity . A transcribed protocol from a known source is less error-prone than o...
The library ships 91 templates grouped by the kind of lab task they cover. Each one targets a specific method type, so it opens in the right editor with the right fields the moment you use it.

This is what makes these templates trustworthy and not only convenient. A growing subset of the library, 52 of the 91 templates today and rolling out further, bundles the actual vendor document the protocol was transcribed from, whether that is the kit insert, the manufacturer...

Two buttons in the template detail make that comparison one click each. View full protocol opens the complete extracted method rendered the way it will look once it is in your library, the full reagent table and cycling program rather than the compact summary the preview shows...
Templates live one step away from your own Methods library . You browse them, preview them, and pull the one you want into your library as an editable method.

Plate-layout templates are not just a picture of a grid. They are an interactive, annotated layout for the exact plate format you work in, including 384-well plates (16 rows A through P by 24 columns). The 384-well viability template, for example, ships with the blank, vehicle...

A few of the LC-MS templates are combinations , packaging a liquid-chromatography gradient and a mass spec acquisition method into one attachable unit, because in practice the two are run together and the parameters are tuned as a pair. Three of these LC-MS combination templat...

Everything about a template is plain, inspectable data. The protocol body is markdown, the structured fields are JSON, and the bundled source PDFs are ordinary PDFs recorded with their origin URL and checksum. A method you create from a template is stored the same open way as ...
Molecular biology is the largest group, with PCR setups (Q5, Taq, colony-PCR screens), gel electrophoresis, cloning and assembly steps, and similar bench protocols.
qPCR covers dye-based and probe-based master-mix protocols (Luna, QuantiNova, and others), each with the primer concentrations, cycling, and melt-curve guidance from the kit handbook.
Cell culture covers passaging schedules, media, and viability assays.
LC-MS covers liquid-chromatography gradients and mass spec acquisition, including a few that pair the two into one unit (see LC-MS combination templates ).
Plate layouts are ready-to-annotate 96- and 384-well grids for dose-response and assay work.
Protein biochemistry, cell biology, analytical chemistry, and a general blank-protocol skeleton round out the set.

From the Methods library, open the template browser. Use the segmented control to switch between your method types and the template catalog, and the category filter and search box (by name, type, or tag) to narrow the list.

Select a template to preview it in the detail pane. A compact preview shows the shape of the protocol, and two buttons open the full view, View full protocol for the complete rendered method and (for the 52 with a bundled document) View vendor PDF for the original.
Pick a destination category for where the new method should land in your library (the detail pane shows "Will be added to" so there is no surprise), then click Use template .

The template is copied into your Methods library as a brand-new method you own. Edit it freely, attach it to experiments, and share it with the lab if you like. None of that touches the original template.

Transcription can introduce errors, and a protocol you cannot check is a protocol you have to trust blindly. With the bundled PDF one click away, you can confirm an annealing temperature, a reagent volume, or a primer concentration against the manufacturer's own insert before ...

Source-PDF bundling is a rollout, not a finished state. 52 of the 91 templates carry their document today. Templates without a bundled PDF still cite their source in the protocol text (a URL and a reference), so you always know where a procedure came from, even when the docume...
The Template Library picker. Cards are grouped by lab-task category, with a method-type pill and a search box that filters by name, type, or tag.

The Template Library picker showing template cards grouped by lab-task category, each card with a title, a short description, a method-type pill, and a small badge marking templates that bundle a source PDF.
The structured template beside the vendor PDF it came from. Any value in the template can be checked against the original.
A template detail with its bundled source PDF open side by side, showing the transcribed reagent table next to the same values in the original vendor insert.
A template detail. Preview the full protocol, choose where it lands, then Use template to copy it into your library.
A template detail pane showing the structured protocol body, the method-type pill, the destination-category control reading Will be added to, and the Use template button.
A 384-well plate-layout template. Control columns, blanks, and a dose series come pre-annotated; you adjust from there.
A 384-well plate-layout template rendered as a 16-by-24 interactive grid, with control columns and a dose-response series annotated by color.

entries.title: Methods Library
headings: What you see on the page
Sharing a method with the lab
Attach a method to an experiment
Recording variations on a single experiment
Transient read access when a task is shared with you
bodySnippets: A method is a reusable protocol you write once and attach to any experiment as a tab. The library ships ten method types, from free-form Markdown to specialized structured editors for PCR, LC gradients, plate layouts, and more, so a method matches the shape of the work instead...

The Method Library page lists every protocol you and your lab have saved, grouped under category headings (e.g., "Molecular Biology", "Imaging"). Each method is a card with its name, a colored type pill (Markdown, PDF, PCR, LC Gradient, Plate Layout, and so on), and any tags y...
Two buttons sit in the top-right of the page: + New Category creates an empty category heading you can drag methods into later, and + New Method opens the create-method modal.
The create-method modal groups types into two sections. Each type gets its own viewer when the method is opened, its own icon on experiment tabs, and its own colored pill on the library card.
Standard methods are body-only, with no structured fields beyond title and tags.
Structured methods swap the markdown body for a purpose-built editor.

You do not have to start from a blank protocol. ResearchOS ships a Template Library of pre-built, ready-to-use protocols, from a Q5 PCR recipe to a 384-well viability plate to an LC-MS run. Browse the catalog, preview a template, and copy it into your own library as a fully ed...

Categories are flat (no sub-folders). To move a method into a different category, grab its card by the ⋮⋮ handle and drop it on another category heading. The drop target highlights blue while you hover. Drop a card on the "Drop here to move to Uncategorized" bar at the top to ...
Use + New Category to set up an empty bucket before you have anything to put in it. Empty categories persist in your browser until at least one method lives there or you remove it.

Every method is private to you by default. To make one visible to the rest of the lab, open the method and click the Private pill in the header. The share popup opens. In the User dropdown, select All Lab Users . A confirmation box appears below. Click Apply . The pill flips f...
Lab-shared methods show a green Public badge on their card and appear in every user's library.

Sharing is stored on the method's array. Choosing All Lab Users writes the value into the array, which the permission system expands to "every user in this lab folder, present and future." Read access is gated by the primitive: owner, anyone explicitly in , the sentinel, or a ...

Sharing a method does not lock the creator out of it. Editing is gated by , which always passes for the original owner regardless of sharing status, so you keep your inline Edit button on a lab-shared method just like a private one. This holds for every type, Markdown and the ...
All users can always attach a shared method to their own experiments and record per-run variations without touching the shared library copy.

There is one more way a method becomes readable. When a user shares a task with you and that task references a method, you get a transient read on the underlying protocol even if the method itself is not in your . The check lives in (in ) and the method owner sees a entry land...
Inside an experiment popup, methods appear as a row of browser-style tabs at the top of the Methods area. Click the + button on the tab bar to open the method picker.

The picker has a search box at the top (search by name or ), a Recently used in this project section pinned to the top, and a folder-grouped list below. Hover or arrow through entries to preview the method body in the right pane, then press Enter or click to attach.

When you open a method tab on an experiment, the amber Variation Notes bar sits at the top. Expand it and click + Add Note to add a timestamped entry where you can describe what you did differently for this run (e.g., "halved the elongation time", "substituted Q5 for Phusion")...

Most method types also let you edit the protocol itself directly inside the experiment tab, not just leave a note about it. A PCR tab edits its thermal gradient and recipe table, an LC tab its gradient, a Plate tab its layout, and the Cell Culture, qPCR Analysis, and Markdown ...
PDF methods are the exception. They render the original file and have no per-experiment copy, so the Variation Notes panel is the only place to record what you did differently for that run.

Markdown is the default. Free-form protocol text in the same markdown editor used for lab notes and results, with toolbars, image drag-drop, and live preview. See The Markdown Editor for the full shortcut set.
PDF lets you upload an existing protocol PDF (a kit insert, a published paper). PDFs render in an iframe and can't be edited inline.
PCR opens a thermal-gradient editor and reaction-recipe table. Full details on the PCR Reaction Builder page.
LC Gradient opens a solvent-gradient chart editor with flow, column, and mobile-phase fields for HPLC and LC-MS protocols.
Plate Layout renders an interactive well-plate grid with sample, control, and blank annotations.
Cell culture passaging tracks a passaging schedule, media, and cell line, with per-task passage history.
Mass spec captures ionization mode, source and scan params, and calibration. Pairs with LC for LC-MS workflows.

qPCR analysis records Cq readouts, melt-curve Tm, standard-curve efficiency, and ΔΔCq fold-change. A typical qPCR workflow pairs a PCR cycling method with a qPCR analysis method by bundling both into a kit, so the thermal program and the analysis fields travel together when at...
Coding workflow stores reusable scripts (Python, R, SQL) and Jupyter notebooks alongside protocol text.

Kit bundles two or more existing methods into one attachable unit (for example, a plate layout plus an assay PDF, or a PCR cycling method plus a qPCR analysis). Because kits are created by extending an existing method (via the Add component (extend into kit) affordance on the ...

Click + New Method . Pick a type from the Standard or Structured section and give it a name. The Folder (optional) field autocompletes against existing categories (the page-level headings) so methods drop into the right bucket.

Fill in the body. Write markdown, upload a PDF, draw an LC gradient, lay out a plate, or build a PCR program and recipe. For markdown methods you can drag images and attachment files directly into the editor.
Click Create Method . The new card lands in its category on the Method Library page and is immediately available to attach to experiments.

Each account can limit which method types appear in the New Method picker. When an account was created with a narrowed set (for example, a chemistry-focused lab that opted out of Coding workflow), only the enabled types show up as picker tiles. The Kit type is always available...
The picker's search box matches names and tags (prefix with to search tags only). Many labs end up with five slightly different copies of the same protocol. A quick search prevents that.
The Method Library. Cards are grouped under category headings and carry a colored type pill. The library supports ten method types.
The Methods Library page showing method cards grouped under category headings, with colored type pills (Markdown, PCR, LC Gradient, Plate Layout, and others) marking each card.
The type picker in the New Method modal. Nine types appear as tiles. Kit does not appear here because kits are created by extending an existing method, not by starting from scratch.

The New Method modal with two sections, Standard and Structured, showing the ten method type tiles including Markdown, PDF, PCR, LC Gradient, Plate Layout, Cell culture passaging, Mass spec, qPCR analysis, and Coding workflow. The Kit type has no tile in this picker.

entries.title: Researcher network
headings: Listed by choice, private by default of email
bodySnippets: The researcher network is the people side of ResearchOS. It is a public directory of researchers, listed by their own choice, that lets you find a colleague by name or institution, confirm who they are, and open a profile of the work they share. It needs no login to browse.

Most of ResearchOS is about your own work, your methods, sequences, data, and figures, all kept in your own folder. The researcher network is the one place that looks outward, at the other people using ResearchOS. It lives at and is reachable from the top navigation and the fo...
It is a discovery surface, not a messaging product. The network tells you who is on ResearchOS and lets you confirm that a person is who they say they are. It never exposes an email address.

The hub has a single search box. Type a name or an institution and it returns the researchers who have opted in to the directory, with their display name, affiliation, and a key fingerprint. Each result links to that person's public profile. The search runs over listed profile...

Every account has a public profile at , the shareable, login-free page for your work. It shows your name, affiliation, avatar, a short bio, and your typed links, an ORCID record, a ResearchGate page, and a personal website. You edit all of it from Settings under your profile. ...

When you sign in with an institutional account, your profile carries a verified-domain badge, for example , so a collaborator can trust that you really are at the institution you claim. Alongside the badge, every profile has a key fingerprint you can read out of band to confir...

A profile is included in the directory by default once you claim a handle, because being findable is the point of a network. You can opt out at any time, which removes you from search and from any institution member list. In every case the directory stays un-walkable for email...

The researcher network is a new surface and is being turned on gradually. Some parts, like live directory search and institution pages, light up as the directory backend is enabled. Your profile at and its privacy controls work today.

entries.title: Notifications & Inbox
headings: Right-click a single row to skip the active-experiment dance
The toast is your fastest path to filing
The phone channel needs the laptop open
bodySnippets: The bell, the photo inbox, and event reminders. All three live in the app shell, so you see new things wherever you're working.
ResearchOS surfaces new things in three places.
Click the bell to drop down a list of notifications. Rows come in several kinds, each with its own icon. The three most common are below, and the lab-specific kinds follow.

Clicking anywhere on an unread row marks it as read but doesn't go anywhere. Clicking a row that is already read is a no-op. Each row also has a blue pill with a checkmark on the right that marks just that one item as read, so you can silence a single notification without touc...

At the top of the dropdown, Mark all read clears the unread badge without removing anything. It only appears when at least one unread item exists. Clear read removes the rows you've already acknowledged, and only appears when at least one read item is present. Clear all emptie...

The bell rechecks for new notifications every 30 seconds while the app is open. When something fires inside this tab (e.g., a calendar reminder), the badge updates immediately rather than waiting for the next poll. Shift alerts work differently. Another lab member writes them ...

Click the Inbox pill to open a centered popup with two tabs. The Shared with me tab holds items another ResearchOS user sent you across folders (see Sharing & Permissions for that flow), and the Photos tab, covered below, holds images waiting to be filed. Each photo row shows ...
Each row carries these actions on the right.
Clicking a single row opens an image editor where you can rename the file, edit the caption, see when it was received, and either move it to the active experiment or delete it.
For a stack of photos that all belong to the same experiment, the modal supports multi-select so you don't have to file them one at a time.

With one or more rows selected, right-click any selected row (or click the ⋯ button) to open a context menu. The top two items read Send to task... and Send to note... for a single row, or Send N items to task... / Send N items to note... when multiple are selected. Picking ei...

If any filename in the batch collides with an existing image in the destination task, the duplicate-resolution dialog pops up for each collision so you can Rename , Replace , or Cancel per item. Non-colliding items go through without interruption. When the batch finishes, a gr...

The moment a new photo arrives in your inbox, a toast appears in the bottom-right corner with a thumbnail, the caption, and a one-click action. If an experiment popup is open, the action is File here , which moves the photo straight into that experiment. If nothing's open, the...

Toasts auto-dismiss after about 12 seconds, and any photo you file or delete elsewhere also drops out of the toast queue automatically. The small close button on a toast just dismisses the notification, the photo stays in the inbox until you act on it.
All notification routing is configured in Settings, Notifications . The matrix has five categories (rows) and four channels (columns). Each cell is an independent toggle.
The five categories map the 14 internal notification types into user-facing groups.
The four channels are as follows.

The defaults shipped in the product are: Shared gets Bell, Laptop, and Phone on; Comments gets Bell and Laptop; Lab announcements gets Bell and Email; Purchases gets Bell only; Reminders gets Bell, Laptop, and Phone.

Quiet hours silence the push channels (Laptop, Phone, Email) during a time window you set. The bell still collects while quiet. Options are a start time, an end time (the window handles overnight ranges where start is after end, such as 19:00 to 08:00), and an optional weekend...

Phone and Email are account-only channels. A solo user working without a cloud account never sees the Phone or Email columns in the matrix, and any stored preference for those channels is ignored at dispatch time. This keeps the settings surface simple for local-only setups an...

The Companion phone app has a dedicated Notifications screen (reachable from the Home tab header bell icon). It shows the notifications the laptop has routed to your phone channel as a synced list. Each row shows a category-tinted icon chip, an unread dot, the notification tit...

The list is fetched from a sealed "notifications" snapshot the laptop publishes while open. It is not a live OS push feed; it is a polled mirror of what the laptop has sent to the phone channel. Pull down to refresh. Read state is owned by the laptop bell, so the list mirrors ...
Opening the Notifications screen also refreshes the phone's push token, covering a token rotation or a notification permission granted after pairing.

The bell handles things you need to acknowledge directly, including the @-mentions and lab notifications above. The broader comment stream lives in a separate surface, the Lab Inbox . The two are kept separate on purpose.

The split is by urgency. A direct @-mention lands in the bell so you do not miss a callout, while general comments on records you can read stay in the Lab Inbox only. See Comments for the threading and mention model.
Reminders are a single setting that applies to every timed event on your calendar. Open the Calendar page and click Reminders at the top of the left sidebar. The popup holds three things.

With reminders on, every timed event in the next 24 hours is queued. When the chosen lead time hits, a row appears in the bell and (if you've granted browser permission) an OS notification pops on top of whatever you're doing. All-day events don't produce reminders, since ther...

Reminder settings, the bell history, and the photo inbox are all per-user. If you share a folder with the rest of your lab, everyone has their own bell, their own inbox, and their own reminder preferences.

The bell in the top-right of the header collects everything that needs your attention. Things someone shared with you (tasks, methods, projects), calendar reminders that have fired, shift alerts when a labmate reschedules a shared task, @-mentions in comments, and (in a lab) a...

The Inbox pill , right next to the bell, opens a centered modal with two tabs, Shared with me (items another ResearchOS user sent you across folders) and Photos (images waiting to be filed into an experiment). The pill turns amber and shows a count that sums both when anything...
An inbox toast appears at the bottom-right corner the moment a new photo arrives, so you can file it in one click without hunting for the modal.
Shared with you. When another lab member shares a task, method, or project with you, a row appears here showing who shared it, what it was, and whether you got view or edit access.
Event reminder. When a calendar reminder you set up fires (see below), a row lands here with the event title, the time it starts, and how many minutes you asked to be warned in advance.

Shift alert. When another lab member with edit access reschedules a task you also have access to, a row appears reading "Alex shifted PCR optimization by +3d" with the old and new start dates underneath. The amber delta covers start-date moves and, when start and end shifted b...
@-mentions. When someone @-mentions you by name in a comment, that mention surfaces here as its own row so a direct callout doesn't get lost in the ambient comment feed.

Lab notifications. Inside a lab, the bell also fires for announcements, task assignments your PI hands you, purchase approvals, flag-for-review notes on your records, and purchase-order updates (someone asked you to order a supply, or yours was ordered). Each lands as its own ...

Move to active. Sends the photo into whichever surface is currently open in ResearchOS, whether an experiment, a note, or (if both popups are open) a small dropdown lets you pick between them. The button is disabled when nothing is open, with a tooltip explaining why.

⋯ (more actions). A small ellipsis button that only fades in when you hover the row. Clicking it opens the same menu as right-click, anchored under the button, with Send to task... , Send to note... , Move to active , and Delete .
Delete. Removes the photo from the inbox folder (with a confirm prompt).
Shift-click a second row to select the contiguous range from your last anchor row to this one.
Cmd-click (or Ctrl-click on Windows / Linux) to toggle individual rows in and out of the selection without disturbing the rest.
Selected rows pick up a blue border and a blue ring so you can see the group at a glance. Clicking the empty area of the modal clears the selection.
Shared & assigned to me. A task, method, or project shared with you, or a task assigned or flagged for your review.
Comments & mentions. Someone comments on your work or @-mentions you.
Lab announcements. Your PI posts a lab-wide announcement.
Purchases & orders. An order you requested is placed or approved, or one is assigned to you.
Reminders & schedule changes. Calendar reminders, and when a shared task's date shifts.

Bell (in-app). The bell in the laptop header always collects every notification in its category. The bell toggle is stored for a future "mute from bell" feature but does not currently suppress the in-app store.
Laptop. Desktop pop-ups from the browser while a ResearchOS tab is open. Requires browser notification permission.
Phone. A push to your paired Companion phone. Requires a cloud account (solo users without an account never see this column). The laptop must be open and publishing to deliver the push.
Email. An email to the verified address on your account. Requires a cloud account (same gate as Phone).
The bell pings you for things you need to acknowledge, including @-mentions, shares, fired reminders, shift alerts, and lab notifications.
The Lab Inbox is ambient. Comments people left on your tasks and notes appear there, alongside the lab-wide announcement stream and the @-mention filter.
A master Enable reminders checkbox.
A Remind me dropdown for how far in advance to fire (1 minute, 5, 10, 15, 30, 1 hour, 2 hours, or 1 day before).
A button to grant your browser permission to show OS-level popups, plus a Send test link once it's granted.

Right-click any inbox row (no multi-select needed) and pick Send to task... to send that one photo to any experiment, even one you don't have open. It's the fastest way to file a photo into the right experiment without first opening that experiment's popup.
If a photo lands while you're working in an experiment, the toast lets you attach it in one click without opening the Inbox modal at all.

The laptop publishes the notifications snapshot while it is running and a ResearchOS tab is open. If the laptop is closed or the tab is shut, no new notifications appear on the phone until the laptop is open again. The phone list shows a "Last synced" timestamp so you can see ...

Because ResearchOS has no server, reminders only fire while a ResearchOS tab is open in your browser. If you close every tab, the in-app row still appears the next time you open the app, but no live OS popup will go off in the meantime. For phone-side or always-on-but-laptop-a...

entries.title: Check-ins
headings: Extra sub-tabs for mentoring pairs and groups
A check-in space is not a shared notebook
bodySnippets: A check-in is a shared advising workspace between two or more people. Both people type into the same areas (weekly goals, meeting notes, freeform notes, and a running agenda), so the record of an advising relationship lives in one place instead of scattered across emails and p...

A check-in is one shared space per pair (or group). It is not a notebook and it does not appear in the Notes rail. It is a purpose-built advising workspace that all members edit, so the weekly goals you agreed on, the notes from your last meeting, and the agenda for the next o...

Each space is shared with exactly the people in it. Everything you add inside is visible to all of them and to no one else (a PI's implicit view-all still applies, the same as everywhere else in ResearchOS). See Sharing and permissions for how that read access works.

The Workbench tab reads Check-ins for every account type. The old role-flipped "Mentoring" label is retired. The tab is always visible regardless of how many spaces you are in, so starting your first space is always reachable. The empty state in the panel explains what a check...

Within a pair space, the per-space entry in the left rail shows the other member's name. In a group space it shows the group title (if one is set) or a summary like "Alex, Morgan +1". A soft relationship hint (you mentor them, they mentor you, peer) is shown inside the open sp...

Any account can start a check-in space. The old lab-head gate is retired. Click Start a check-in in the rail header (or in the empty state), pick the other person, and optionally tick I am the mentor in this relationship . The other person sees the space appear in their Check-...
Every check-in space has four shared areas, shown as sub-tabs in the main pane. All members can add to and edit all four.
Beyond the four core tabs, additional sub-tabs appear depending on the space type.

A group space holds three or more members. It carries the same four core tabs as a pair space, plus the Task board and Rotation tabs described above. Group spaces are created the same way as pair spaces: click Start a check-in and pick multiple people.
The Check-ins tab is always present on the Workbench , alongside Projects, Experiments, Notes, and Lists. If you have no spaces yet, the tab still shows and offers the empty-state start button.
Weekly goals. A per-week checklist either person can add to and check off. A week selector moves between weeks, so the goals you set in one meeting stay attached to that week.
Meeting notes. A shared running log, one entry per meeting, with a date picker so each entry is filed under the meeting it belongs to.
Notes. Freeform shared notes scoped to this space, for anything that is not a goal or a meeting note.
Agenda. A running list of items for the next meeting. Items carry between meetings until they are checked off, so nothing falls through.
IDP (Individual Development Plan). Appears on pair spaces where a mentor direction is set. The trainee sees My IDP ; the mentor sees a review surface. Peer pairs and groups have no IDP tab.
Expectations. A mentoring compact (goals and expectations both sides agreed to). Available on all spaces, started on demand.
Onboarding. A checklist for onboarding a new member. Available on all spaces, most relevant for new-member spaces.
