Every permission decision in the app routes through one of two pure, synchronous functions in .

Both functions take exactly two arguments and are pure, so the same input always gives the same output with no I/O. They are synchronous, so every render and every save can call them without async ceremony.

A companion helper, , returns true only when the viewer is the owner or has an explicit edit-share entry. Callers use it to tell whether the PI's write right comes purely from their role or from a normal owner/edit-share basis.

When a PI tries to edit a record that passes only because of the PI role (not because they own it or have an explicit edit-share), the record popup shows a once-per-session confirmation before opening the editor. That confirm step is a UI guard in the popup, not a condition in...

in replaces the sentinel with the concrete set of current lab members. The owner is excluded (they already have access). When a user appears both as an explicit entry and via the sentinel, the highest level wins (edit beats read). This is the function share dialogs use to rend...

A user whose gets an extra rule on the read side: returns true for every record in the lab regardless of . The Lab Overview cross-lab dashboards depend on this rule. The member workload widget has to be able to read every member's active tasks even when those tasks are private...

One non-obvious rule sits next to : when a user shares a task with you, you also get transient read access on any method that task references. The pure helper in performs the depth-1 check, and the read path emits a entry into the method owner's audit log so they can see who h...
Sharing is per-record.

Sharing is not silent on the receiving end. When a labmate adds you to a record, it shows up in your Inbox under the Shared with me segment, the running list of everything people in your folder have shared to you, newest first. The Inbox badge carries a count so you can tell a...

Everything above is sharing inside one folder, where labmates already see the same files. Sharing with a researcher who is not in your folder works differently, because they have nothing of yours locally. The share dialog handles this on its Outside your lab tab, and there are...

Send a note, method, project, or file to someone as a frozen copy. You pick the recipient by the email tied to their ResearchOS profile, and ResearchOS encrypts the payload end to end before it leaves your machine. The relay only ever holds ciphertext, never the readable conte...

Grant a researcher outside your folder live, editable access to a note, the same real-time collaboration labmates get. The document stays live until you revoke it. Because the outside person holds nothing of yours locally yet, accepting the invite writes a real copy into their...

An outside recipient sees the invite in their own Shared with me list, the same place in-lab shares appear. Before anything materializes, ResearchOS checks that the sender's identity matches their published directory key, so a spoofed email cannot push a document into someone'...

Sharing a task one way is read or edit access on that task. Hosting goes a step further. A labmate can let one of their tasks also appear inside your project , so the experiment lives in your project board alongside your own tasks without ever leaving its original owner's fold...

The link is bidirectional : the task points at your project and your project points back at the task, and both sides have to agree for the host to count. If the two ever drift apart (a task gets deleted, or the reference goes stale), ResearchOS self-heals the mismatch on read ...
Pages elsewhere in the wiki that touch sharing decisions.
. A user with no lab affiliation. No special privileges beyond owning their own records.
. A lab member (not the head). Can read and write their own records plus anything explicitly shared with them.
. The PI. Gets implicit view-all on reads and write-all on writes (see below for how the write-all is gated in the UI).
canRead(record, viewer) : true if the viewer is the owner, OR the viewer's account_type is (implicit view-all), OR has an entry whose matches the viewer OR is .
canWrite(record, viewer) : true if the viewer is the owner, OR the viewer's account_type is (role-based write-all, see note below), OR has an entry whose matches (or is ) AND the entry's is .

Methods can be shared with the whole lab or with specific users. Public-sharing a method makes it readable across libraries; the structured-method editor still gates writes on , which usually keeps edits to the original creator. See Methods Library .

Projects share the project itself plus every task under it. The dialog offers view-only or edit permission. The owner's Home page is the source of truth; the receiver sees a live read of the owner's data.
Tasks can be shared one at a time when you want a single experiment visible without sharing the whole project.
Notes can be shared the same way.
Inventory items default to whole-lab edit (the sentinel at level ) so every member can add, use, and update stock. You can restrict a particular item per-record.
Methods Library covers method-level sharing UI.
Home and Projects covers project-level sharing and the receiver's view.
The Workbench covers task-level sharing.
PI covers the implicit view-all rule.
Notifications covers the Inbox, the Shared with me segment, and the badge that flags a new share.
Security covers what the gate actually protects against (and what it does not).

Pre-R1 records (and older share-API callers) used a field with values or instead of . The reader ( in ) accepts both shapes so the rest of the app only ever sees the unified field ( maps to ; unknown values fall back to , the conservative default). Records still on the legacy ...

ResearchOS used to ship with a special "Lab Mode" account that held shared records on behalf of the whole lab. That mode has been retired in favor of per-user accounts plus the sharing primitive described above; pre-retirement folders auto-migrate on first login. Any old recor...
Outside-your-lab sharing relies on the directory and relay, which are turned off in the hosted beta, so it is a laptop and self-host feature for now. The in-lab sharing above does not depend on it.
The In your lab tab of the share dialog. Each recipient has its own read or edit toggle, and a Share with the whole lab toggle covers present and future members at read-only by default.
The In your lab tab of the share dialog over a method popup, showing recipient rows each with a read/edit toggle, plus a Share with the whole lab toggle below.

entries.title: Trash & History
headings: What trash does not yet do
Deleted vs edited, two different safety nets
Bulk restore skips the parent prompt
bodySnippets: A recovery window for deletes. When you delete a note, task, project, method, purchase, goal, lab link, mass spec protocol, sequence, molecule, inventory item or stock, or storage location, it does not vanish. It moves into a per-user trash folder where it sits for 30 days. Wi...

Research data has a different relationship to time than consumer data. A note about a PCR run from eight months ago is not "old, who cares," it is the experimental record. A misclick on the delete button used to be irrecoverable. The new trash flow gives you a safety net witho...

When you delete a note, the file moves from into . A small metadata block is added to the trashed record that records when the delete happened, who issued it, and when the auto-cleanup sweep will permanently remove it.

A sidecar index at keeps a flat summary of every trashed record. The index is rebuilt automatically from the directory listing if it ever goes out of sync (manual file deletion, OneDrive merge conflict, partial crash). The on-disk files are the ground truth. The index is just ...

Open the trash from the small trash-can icon in the top-right corner of the header (just left of your avatar chip) or from the Settings -> History & Trash section. Trashed records are grouped into sections by what they are, so all your deleted notes sit together, all your dele...
Each row shows the following.

A sort dropdown in the header switches between Newest first (default), Oldest first, and Expiring soon (urgent-cleanup-first). The sort applies inside each section, so the most relevant rows float to the top of every category at once.

Cleaning up after a big experiment usually means dealing with more than one stray record at a time. Rather than restoring or deleting rows one by one, you can select several and act on them in a single pass. The checkbox on each row drives the selection, and the checkbox on ea...

Defaults to 30 days . Change it under Settings -> History & Trash . Four options, 7 days, 30 days, 90 days, or Never. The Never option means automatic cleanup never fires, but you can still delete from the trash page manually.

The cleanup pass runs once on every folder-connect. If you keep ResearchOS open for weeks at a time, the sweep waits until your next cold start to clear expired entries. Multi-device users get the same behavior on each device when they connect their folder.
Only the record owner sees the Delete button. If a labmate has shared edit access to one of your notes, they can edit it, but they cannot delete it. That's your call as the owner.

The one carve-out is the lab head. A lab head can delete records owned by other lab members, based on a real role check (your account is a lab head), not a temporary unlock. The trash entry records the lab head as the deleter and rides an audit session id along so the audit lo...
Click Restore on the row. The record is written back to its original path with the metadata block stripped off, and the trash file is removed. The record is now live again at the same id.
When restoring a record whose parent (e.g. a Project that contains the task) is also in trash, a prompt asks whether to restore both or just the child. See "Restoring with a trashed parent" below.

Use Permanent delete on the row to remove a trash entry ahead of the cleanup window. The button asks for confirmation and is final. After permanent delete, the record is gone. There is no second-level recycle bin.
Projects are the one entity type with TWO recovery states, and both coexist on purpose.

Records like Tasks (parent: Project), Notes (no parent), Purchase Items (parent: Task), High-level Goals (parent: Project), and a sub-method (parent: its parent Method) record a soft reference to their parent at delete time. When you restore a record whose parent is ALSO in tr...
If the parent is not in trash (the common case), no prompt fires and the restore proceeds straight through.

Per-record edit history (the per-save timeline plus a revert button) is now shipping on a separate track. It is live today on free-form Notes as a rolling-out pilot. See Version History for what is on and where it goes next. The settings panel also surfaces an "Orphaned files"...
A checkbox for selecting the row (more on that below).
The record's name (recovered from the original title).
Who deleted it and when.
A countdown until auto-cleanup ("Expires in 27 days").
A Restore button (returns the record to its original location) and a Permanent delete button (removes the trash file immediately, with a confirmation step).

Archive (the existing button) keeps the project at with . It is hidden from default views but visible in the archived list. Use this for projects you are done with but want to keep on disk for reference.

Delete (now routes through trash) moves the project file to and the cleanup window starts. The trash entry preserves the flag, so a project you archived first and then deleted will come back archived on restore.
Restore both (default): restores the parent first, then this record.
Just this record : leaves the parent in trash. The record will exist but its parent reference will dangle.
Cancel : nothing happens.
Pick your rows. Tick the checkbox on any row you want to act on. Selections can span more than one section, so you can grab two notes and a task in the same go.

Or grab a whole category. The checkbox in a section header selects every row in that section at once. When only some of the rows in a section are selected, the header checkbox shows a dash to tell you the section is partly selected rather than fully selected.

Act from the bar. As soon as something is selected, a bar slides in at the top of the list showing how many items are selected. From there you can Restore all of them, Permanent delete all of them, or Clear selection to start over.
Confirm a bulk delete. Restoring in bulk happens right away. A bulk permanent delete is irreversible, so it asks you to confirm in a dialog first, and only then removes the selected records for good.

Trash recovers a record you deleted . It does not help with a record you edited . A paragraph you overwrote three saves ago is not in the trash, because the note itself was never deleted. For that, ResearchOS keeps a separate per-save timeline you can scroll back through and (...

If a row leaves the trash while it is selected, whether you restored it on its own or the list reloaded, it quietly drops out of the selection. The count in the bar always reflects what is actually still sitting in the trash.

Restoring a single record asks what to do when its parent is also in the trash (see "Restoring with a trashed parent" below). A bulk restore does not stop to ask. Each selected record is restored on its own, so if you want a parent and child to come back together, select both ...
Each user's cleanup window applies to their own trash. If a lab head deletes one of your records, the trash entry lands in your folder and inherits your cleanup setting.
You can archive a project today and trash it next month. Restoring from trash strips the trash metadata only, so the archive state survives the round trip.

Every delete you make through the app is recoverable for at least seven days (the most aggressive cleanup setting). Within the default 30-day window, a misclick is a one-button fix. We do not promise the trash is tamper-proof. The files sit in your folder and you can edit them...
Selecting rows reveals a bar with Restore, Permanent delete, and Clear selection.
Trash page with two rows selected and the bulk action bar showing Restore, Permanent delete, and Clear selection

entries.title: Version History
headings: Grouped by day, then by session
Who sees it, local and private
Color is never the only signal
bodySnippets: Every time you save a note, ResearchOS quietly records what changed, when, and who changed it. Open the version history sidebar and you can scroll back through that timeline, see each edit highlighted in place, and roll the note back to any earlier state. It is the experimenta...

A version is a single saved state of a note. You do not create versions by hand and there is no "commit" button to remember. You save explicitly by clicking Save checkpoint in the editor (see Saving in the editor ), and every one of those saves appends one row to an append-onl...

Because the file is append-only and stores deltas, the history never rewrites the live note. The note you are editing is always the ground truth. The history is a side-channel that the save path writes after the note is safely on disk, and a failure to write history can never ...

This is a different kind of safety net than the Trash . Trash recovers a note you deleted , while version history recovers a note you edited . A paragraph you overwrote three saves ago is not in the trash, because the note itself was never deleted, but it is sitting in the ver...

Open any note and look for the clock-arrow history control on the note popup. Click it and a version history sidebar slides in on the right while the note body stays on the left. The sidebar is the timeline, and the body column becomes the place where each version is shown.

Each row in the sidebar is one version. It shows the editor's avatar, a one-line summary of what that save changed (for example, "edited 2 paragraphs"), and a relative timestamp like "3 hours ago." Hover the timestamp for the full date and time. The newest state sits at the to...

A working note can accumulate dozens of saves in an afternoon. A flat list of every save would be overwhelming, so the sidebar groups them the way you actually remember your work, by day first, then by editing session within the day.

Select any version and the note body column does not just show that old snapshot. It shows a diff , the note as it stands, with the selected version's changes marked inline. Added lines render in green, removed lines in red with a strike-through, and unchanged prose renders no...

On a note that more than one person edits, the diff goes a step further and tints each change by editor . Every changed run of lines carries a left border in that editor's personal color, with their avatar at the start of the run, so you can see at a glance that Mira rewrote t...

By default, a version is diffed against the one immediately before it, so you see exactly what that single save changed. A toggle at the top of the sidebar switches the comparison base to the current version instead. That answers a different question, "what is different betwee...

Version history is part of your data folder, not a cloud service. The history file sits inside the note owner's folder on disk, right beside the note, and it travels with the rest of your data through whatever sync you already use (OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, and the othe...

Visibility follows the same rules as the note itself. A note that is private to you keeps its history private to you. If you share the note with the lab, a labmate who can read the note can read its history too. The history does not widen access, and it does not leak past edit...

Selecting any earlier version reveals a Restore this version button in a sticky footer at the bottom of the sidebar (the current version has nothing to restore to, so it never shows the button). Clicking it asks for an inline confirmation rather than popping a browser dialog, ...

Restoring does not erase anything. The note's current state is kept as a version in the history before the older state becomes the live note, so a restore is just one more forward edit on the timeline. You can always see what the note looked like before you restored it.

A restore is reversible. For 24 hours after you restore a version, an Undo restore affordance lets you put the note back the way it was before the restore, in one click. After the window closes the restore simply stands as a normal edit in the history, which you can still walk...

A per-entry edit history with the ability to revert is one of the things funders and reviewers look for in a research record. It is what makes the record provenanced , a tamper-evident trail of who changed what and when. ResearchOS records that trail automatically and keeps it...
Day headers ("Today," "Yesterday," then dated) split the timeline into the days you worked on the note.

Sessions bundle a run of consecutive saves by the same editor into one collapsible group. A burst of ten small saves while you tightened up a protocol collapses to a single "Mira, 10 versions" row you can expand when you need the detail and leave folded when you do not.

Version history, including restore and the 24-hour undo, is on for everyone on free-form Notes , Tasks , and Projects . There's nothing to configure or turn on, it works automatically. The same timeline, diff, and restore also cover a task's Lab Notes and Results documents and...

Notes with a very long history are paginated (a Load older versions button at the bottom), and saves from far enough back are summarized into a single "earlier versions" note rather than kept row by row. You always keep the recent, detailed timeline, and the deep past is conde...

Added and removed lines always carry the green/red coloring, the strike-through, and a / gutter mark in addition to the editor tint, so the diff reads clearly for color-blind users. The per-editor color is supplementary, never load-bearing on its own.
The version history sidebar. Saves are grouped by day, then by editing session, with the current version pinned at the top.

A note open with the version history sidebar on the right, showing versions grouped under day headers and editing sessions, each row with an editor avatar, a one-line change summary, and a relative timestamp.
The selected version's changes, shown in place. Each changed run carries a left border tinted with the editor's color and their avatar, so who-changed-what reads at a glance.
A note body showing an in-place diff: added lines in green, removed lines in red with strike-through, with a colored left border and an editor avatar marking the run of lines a given person changed.
The compare base toggle. Previous shows what one save changed; Current shows the full delta from an old state to today.
The compare toggle at the top of the version history sidebar, a two-option segmented control reading Previous and Current, with Previous selected.
An earlier version offers a Restore button. The confirm step is inline, not a native dialog.
The version history sidebar with an earlier version selected and a green Restore this version button in a sticky footer, plus the inline confirm and cancel prompt.
For 24 hours after a restore, Undo restore reverses it in one click. The undo is itself recorded as a forward edit.
The note popup header showing an Undo restore button after a restore, with a note that the undo window is open for 24 hours.

entries.title: Getting Started
bodySnippets: Short pages that take you from a brand-new visitor to a working ResearchOS install, or skip ahead to the in-browser demo.
Work through these in order:

entries.title: Account tiers
headings: How to create a "Just me, local" account
How to create a Free account
How to join an existing lab
Joining does not affect your existing data
Priced to sustain, not to profit
Lab Head: you can invite someone who is already a Free account user
bodySnippets: ResearchOS has three tiers, each designed around how much you want to share and collaborate. You pick one when you create your account, and you can switch later.

The account-first entry is the default. When you open ResearchOS signed in but without a data folder connected, you land on the /account home rather than the folder-connect wall. From there you choose a tier before linking any folder. If you arrived via OAuth (Google, GitHub, ...
The tier chooser presents three paths. Understanding what each one is will save you from picking the wrong one and wondering why a feature is missing.

The lightest path. You connect a folder, pick a username, and ResearchOS is ready to go. No account is created, no sign-in happens, and nothing leaves your machine. All features that don't involve sharing are available, including the full Workbench, notes, methods, sequences, ...

Pick this if you work alone and have no need to share data or collaborate with people outside your folder. If you share a lab folder via OneDrive, Dropbox, or Google Drive, the existing Shared Lab Accounts setup is a local-only workflow too. "Just me, local" is the right pick ...

You sign in with Google, GitHub, ORCID, or LinkedIn. Your data still lives on your disk. Signing in creates a cloud identity with an @handle and a researcher profile (name, institution, ORCID, and other typed links) that other researchers can find. It unlocks two sharing surfa...
There is no cloud storage involved in the Free account tier. The sharing relay is a one-time encrypted handoff, not a persistent store. Your data folder doesn't leave your machine.

All the sharing features of the Free account, plus real-time co-editing inside a lab. Notes, experiments, and notebooks sync live between lab members. The PI gets a Lab Overview with cross-member views of workload, the purchase-approval queue, and a running feed of lab activity.
Each member's files still live on their own disk. The cloud layer only relays edits in real time. When you are offline, the app keeps working against the local copy and syncs when you reconnect.

When you create a Lab account you become the Lab Head for that lab. Members join by following your invite link, or by finding your lab in the lab directory and requesting to join (you approve the request). The Lab tile is only shown when the Lab tier is available (it is curren...
There are two ways to join a lab someone else created.

Your Lab Head shares a link with you (by email, Slack, or any other channel they prefer). Follow the link, sign in if you have not already, connect your data folder, and pick a username. The Lab Head sees your join request in their Lab Overview and approves it. Once approved, ...
The local-first core of ResearchOS is free. Cloud storage for real-time lab collaboration is where cost recovery applies, and the model is designed so the vast majority of labs never see a bill.

None of your data is locked to a tier. If you start Local-only and later want sharing features, go to Settings -> Account and sign in with OAuth to upgrade to a Free account. If you want to move into a lab, the Lab Head can send you an invite link at any point. Your folder, pr...

Cross-boundary sharing. Send notes, methods, and files to any ResearchOS user who has an account, even if they are not in your folder. Recipients get a copy in their own folder. The transfer is end-to-end encrypted, so the relay holds only ciphertext and neither ResearchOS nor...
Researcher directory. You become findable by name and institution so collaborators can find you without an out-of-band email exchange.
"Just me, local" and Free accounts: free, no cloud storage used, no billing ever.

Lab accounts: a free plan with a 5 GB cloud pool shared across the whole lab covers most active labs. If a lab needs more, it can move to a larger flat-price plan, so the cost is a known monthly number rather than a surprise. The free plan is the default and never charges.
On the start screen, choose Just me, local .
Connect your data folder using the browser's folder picker. See Connecting your folder if you are new to this step.
Pick a username. You land on your Workbench.
On the start screen, choose Free account .

Sign in with Google, GitHub, ORCID, or LinkedIn. This creates a cloud identity with an @handle and a researcher profile other researchers can find in the directory. The sign-in happens once, and ResearchOS remembers it so you don't have to sign in again on this device.
Connect your data folder. Your folder stays on your machine. The OAuth credential is stored only in your browser, not in your folder.
Pick a username. You land on your Workbench. The sharing features are now active.
On the start screen, choose Lab , then Create a new lab .
Sign in with Google, GitHub, ORCID, or LinkedIn.
Give your lab a name. This becomes the display name in the lab directory.
Connect your data folder. You are now the Lab Head for this lab.
Invite your team. The lab settings screen gives you an invite link to share. Anyone who follows it and signs in is added as a member pending your approval.
On the start screen, choose Lab , then Find and join a lab .
Browse or search the lab directory. Each lab entry shows the Lab Head's name, institution, and the date the lab was created.
Click Request to join on the lab you want. This sends a notification to the Lab Head.
The Lab Head approves or declines. You will see the outcome the next time you open the app.

Start with "Just me, local." You can connect a folder, build out your projects, and switch to a Free account or a Lab later without losing any data. The tier is tied to your account, not to your folder.
When you join a lab, your folder stays exactly as it is. The collaboration layer connects your local data to the shared sync channel without moving or overwriting your files.

ResearchOS is not trying to extract money from researchers. The paid plans exist to cover the actual cost of running the sync infrastructure for labs that use it heavily. The free plan is generous enough that most labs will stay within it, and you are never charged without cho...

If a researcher already has a ResearchOS Free account, sending them your invite link is all it takes. They sign in with their existing identity, connect their folder, and join your lab. There is no separate re-registration.
The account start screen. Choose a path before connecting your folder.
The account start screen with the three-path tier chooser.
The tier-chooser. The Lab tile is shown when the Lab tier is available.
The tier chooser showing the Just me local tile, Free account tile, and Lab tile.

entries.title: Browser Requirements
headings: Keep Firefox or Safari as your daily driver
bodySnippets: ResearchOS reads and writes files directly on your computer, which only some browsers can do.
Google Chrome (version 86+) on Windows, macOS, Linux, ChromeOS.
Microsoft Edge (version 86+) on Windows, macOS.
Other Chromium-based browsers (e.g., Arc, Vivaldi, Opera) should work, we just don't officially test them, so report any issues you hit.

Brave is built on Chromium but deliberately removes the File System Access API for privacy reasons, and there is no reliable way to turn it back on. ResearchOS can't open your folder in Brave. Use Chrome or Edge instead.
Firefox doesn't implement the File System Access API. Your data is still stored in inside your folder, so once Firefox ships support, your existing data will load.
Safari (macOS / iOS) has the same limitation as Firefox.

Mobile browsers (e.g., Chrome / Edge / Brave on iOS or Android) won't work. The File System Access API is desktop-only, so even the Chromium-based browsers on phones and tablets can't open your folder.
Chrome : google.com/chrome
Edge : microsoft.com/edge (preinstalled on Windows 10/11)

ResearchOS uses the File System Access API to read and write JSON files directly to a folder you pick. This is what lets the app run with no server, no account, and no upload of your data. Firefox and Safari don't support the File System Access API yet, so those browsers can't...
You only need a Chromium browser open while you're using ResearchOS . Many users keep Firefox or Safari for everyday browsing and open Chrome only for ResearchOS.

entries.title: Connecting Your Folder
headings: Why there is no separate "create folder" option
The page layout, at a glance
Starting fresh? Make an empty folder first
What gets created inside the folder
Setting up a shared lab folder
The walkthrough is optional and doesn't connect a folder
If Chrome refuses the folder you picked
Already have data? Connect the same folder.
bodySnippets: Pick a folder on your disk. ResearchOS will read and write JSON files inside it (no cloud, no upload). On a brand-new visit, BeakerBot idles in the upper-right and offers an optional 3-minute walkthrough you can take before connecting anything.

ResearchOS works against a real folder on your disk, and Chrome's File System Access API can only ever open a folder you point it at. It cannot create a folder from the picker dialog (the OS picker blocks the parent locations a new-folder flow would need, even the Documents ro...
The screen is titled Connect your folder . On a first visit, three things share it.

Want to look around before committing a folder? The seeded fake yeast lab (browse it in the app, or download it as a real starter folder you can link) is accessible from the welcome page and via the URL. See Demo Mode .

Because the picker can only open a folder that already exists, you make the folder yourself first. Do this with your normal file manager (Finder on Mac, Explorer on Windows) before you click Browse for a folder.

The Take the 3-minute walkthrough button on the connect screen opens a small 4-beat modal that introduces the app before you commit to connecting a folder. It is opt-in, not auto-fire. Brand-new users see the speech bubble's gentle nudge ("New here? It is strongly recommended ...

A small Skip link sits in the corner of every beat so you can bail back to the picker at any time. The modal does NOT write anything to disk and does NOT persist a "seen" flag; reopening it is a one-click decision you make each visit.
ResearchOS creates a simple tree the first time you connect.

That tree is trimmed for readability. Each kind of record (events, goals, dependencies, purchases, and a few others) gets its own subfolder under your username, plus small counter files ResearchOS uses to hand out IDs. You don't edit those by hand. Everything is plain JSON and...

After your first connect, ResearchOS remembers the folder handle via browser storage and reconnects to it silently whenever Chrome still holds the permission, so most return visits drop you straight back into your data with no picker and no extra click. When the start screen d...

If multiple people in your lab should share one folder, put the folder inside OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, or iCloud, and follow Shared Lab Accounts . The critical step is making sure the folder is always available offline on every member's laptop.

The drop zone in the center : a dashed-border card with the heading Drag your data folder here . Below a short divider sits the Browse for a folder button for users who prefer the OS picker. Connecting an existing ResearchOS folder and starting fresh both use this same zone.

BeakerBot in the upper-right : a small sky-blue beaker mascot in idle pose (not waving). Below the mascot is a speech bubble with a brief nudge, plus a button labeled Take the 3-minute walkthrough . Skip past it entirely if you already know what you want to do.

The credentials stamp in the bottom-right : a small badge that names the academic project this app grew out of (a UW-Madison Distinguished Research Fellowship). Pure authority signal; nothing to click.
Welcome. BeakerBot waves and gives you a two-sentence pitch for ResearchOS. There is also a small heart easter egg if you click the mascot.
Data security. The core promise is that your data NEVER leaves your computer. No upload, no central server, no telemetry on your research. See Security for the full story.
Folder choice. Local (recommended for solo) or cloud-synced (for cross-device or multi-person lab). Local skips the next beat and closes the modal; cloud advances to beat 4.

Cloud provider. Picks the cloud you want to host the folder in (OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, Box, iCloud) and links you to the per-provider setup guide. Closing the modal returns you to the connect screen; the folder connection itself still happens through the drop zone.

Open your file manager and make a new folder anywhere you like (Documents/ResearchOS works well). IMPORTANT : Chrome blocks the Desktop, Documents, and Downloads folders themselves, but a folder you make inside any of them works fine.
Name it something like .
Click Browse for a folder and select the folder you just made, not its top-level parent.

Connect the folder. You have two equivalent ways. Click Browse for a folder to open your operating system's folder picker, or drag a folder directly onto the drop zone . The zone highlights with a blue dashed border and the heading changes from "Drag your data folder here" to ...

The browser asks for permission to read and write that folder. Click Allow . Chrome remembers the grant until you clear the site's data, so you won't get reprompted every time (you may see it once more after a browser restart).

ResearchOS initializes the folder structure (e.g., , ) the first time you link an empty folder, then shows the user-picker screen. After you pick or create a username, you land in the app and can start working right away.

ResearchOS uses the File System Access API, which is only available in Chrome and Edge. Safari, Firefox, and Brave are not supported (Brave disables the API on purpose). See Browser Requirements for details.

Clicking Take the 3-minute walkthrough opens a 4-beat modal (welcome, data security, folder choice, cloud provider). It runs the BeakerBot intro in slide form, then closes and returns you to this same connect screen. The modal never connects a folder for you. Returning users s...

If Chrome says a folder "contains system files" after you pick it, that is its block on sensitive locations. The top-level Desktop, Documents, Downloads, and home folders are off limits. The picker shows a recovery popup ( That folder can't be used. Pick a different spot. ) wi...
If you've used ResearchOS before, point it at the same folder you used last time. Your projects, tasks, methods, and notes will load exactly as you left them.

Skip the folder connection and visit the demo instead. The welcome page has a button to open the app at with a seeded fake yeast lab you can click around in, or you can navigate to directly. No folder, no install, edits disappear on reload. See Demo Mode .
If you clear the site's data (e.g., browser settings → clear data), the folder handle is wiped. Your data on disk is untouched. You just have to pick the folder again next time you open ResearchOS.
The Connect your folder screen. Drag a folder into the drop zone or use the Browse for a folder button. The old folder-connect.png depicts the retired single-card layout.

The Connect your folder screen with a dashed-border drop zone that reads Drag your data folder here, a Browse for a folder button below the divider, and an idle BeakerBot with a speech bubble and Take the 3-minute walkthrough button in the upper-right.

entries.title: Converting to single-user
headings: The choice you see when you connect
If you own the folder, make it your own
If you are a labmate, take your data with you
Where everything lives, and how to recover
Connecting a bundle as a folder
This only appears for a real shared folder
One person leaving does not disrupt the others
Tidy up the leftovers when you are confident
bodySnippets: ResearchOS now works best as one folder per person. If you connect a folder that several people still share, ResearchOS offers to split it so everyone ends up with their own workspace. Nothing is ever deleted, and your own data is left exactly as it is.

Early versions of ResearchOS let a whole lab work out of a single shared folder, with one subfolder per person inside it. That worked, but it carried real cost. Every read had to reason about who owned what and who could see it, the app loaded everyone's data even when you onl...

ResearchOS has moved to a simpler model. Each person keeps their own folder, the data stays local to them, and sharing happens directly between people who opt into it (see Sharing and permissions ). A solo folder is faster to load, simpler to reason about, and entirely yours. ...

When you connect a folder that still has two or more people in it, ResearchOS shows a blocking choice before you start working. It is role-aware, so what you see depends on whether you own the folder or are one of the other members in it. You can always pick Keep it shared for...
The same conversion is also reachable later from Settings under Data maintenance , as Convert this folder to single-user , so you do not have to decide the moment you connect.

As the folder's main user you are offered Convert this folder to mine . You keep this folder and everything in it that is yours. Everyone else is packaged into their own portable copy, and their originals here move to a recoverable Trash. Before anything happens you see a prev...
When you confirm, ResearchOS does three things.

When it finishes, the result screen tells you where to find each hand-off copy under and reminds you that the originals are safe in if you ever need them. Hand each person their bundle and you are done.

If you are one of the other members rather than the folder's owner, you are offered Take my data to my own folder instead. This copies just your data into your own portable folder and removes you from the shared folder. Everyone else stays exactly where they are, and their dat...
The preview shows your record count and who stays behind. When you confirm, ResearchOS does three things.

A banner on the connect screen then tells you exactly where your new folder is ( ) and how to reopen it. You can move that folder anywhere on your computer first, then click Open a folder and select it to keep working as your own workspace.
Both paths use the same two folders inside your data folder, and both are plain folders you can browse in Finder or Explorer at any time.
A bundle is a real, standalone folder. To open one, hand the folder to its owner (or keep your own), optionally move it somewhere permanent on disk, then connect it like any other folder.

The conversion is built so an interrupted run can never lose data. For each person, the portable copy is written and verified complete before anything is moved out, and the single step that removes an original is never reached until that verified copy exists. If a sync stalls,...
holds each person's portable copy. Inside it is a normal tree, so the bundle folder itself is a connectable single-user ResearchOS folder.
holds the original that was moved out. This is the recovery copy. Nothing is hard-deleted, so if something looks off you can restore from here.

Packages everyone else into a portable copy. Each other person is copied into . That bundle is a complete, connectable single-user folder for them, so you can hand it over and they open it as their own workspace without losing any work.
Moves their originals to a recoverable Trash. Once a person's bundle is copied and verified complete, their original moves to . It is a move to Trash, not a delete, so you can put anything back.

Clears the now-dangling shared links from your data. Because a single-user folder shares with no one, any sharing between you and the people who left is removed from your records. Only the sharing links change. The records themselves keep their full history, and who wrote or e...
Your data is copied into , a complete folder you open as your own single-user workspace. Nothing of yours is left behind.
Your originals here move to a recoverable Trash, and the shared folder stays intact for everyone else.
The moment your copy is complete, ResearchOS disconnects you from the shared folder so the app stops writing as you, and returns you to the connect screen.
Copy or move the folder to a permanent home on your computer (for example ). It is yours to keep, so it does not have to stay inside the old shared folder.
On the connect screen, use Browse for a folder (or drag the bundle into the drop zone) and select that bundle folder. See Connecting Your Folder for how the connect flow works.
ResearchOS opens it as a single-user folder with all of that person's notes, tasks, methods, and history intact.

Every step here is recoverable. Each person's data is copied into a complete, portable folder before anything is moved, the copy is verified, and only then do the originals move to a recoverable Trash inside the folder (not a hard delete). If the conversion is interrupted part...

The prompt fires only for a signed-in user in a folder that genuinely has more than one person. A folder that already has a single user shows nothing, and demo and wiki preview modes never trigger it.

Converting only moves the other people out and tidies up the dangling share links those departures leave behind. Your own notes, tasks, methods, files, and history are not copied, not moved, and not rewritten beyond clearing shares that no longer point at anyone.

A labmate export removes only you. It deliberately leaves everyone else's records alone, including any links they had to your data, which gracefully show as a former member on their side rather than being rewritten without their say.

The and folders stay on disk until you remove them, on purpose, so recovery is always available. Once everyone has their own folder and you have confirmed their data is intact, you can delete those two folders from the keeper's folder to reclaim the space. Do this only after y...
If you are setting up a folder to share across a lab in the first place, see Shared Lab Accounts . To hide a former member from active views without moving their data, see User Archiving .
The owner's preview screen. You see every person who will move out and their record count before you confirm. Screenshot pending a capture pass.

The Convert this folder to single-user modal as the owner. A short explanation at the top, then three points. Everyone else gets a portable copy under _migration_bundles, their data moves to a recoverable Trash under _trash/migrated_users, and shared links are cleared. Below t...
After a labmate export, a banner on the connect screen tells you exactly where your new folder is and how to open it. Screenshot pending a capture pass.

The post-export banner pinned to the top of the connect screen. A green check, the line Your data was exported and you left the folder, and a note that the new folder is at _migration_bundles/your-name with instructions to click Open a folder and select it. A recoverable copy ...

entries.title: Creating a User
headings: PI accounts always have a password
bodySnippets: Each person in the folder picks a unique username. Their data lives under users/<username>/.

Each "user" is a named folder inside in your data directory, with their own projects, tasks, methods, and notes underneath. ResearchOS has cloud accounts (Free and Lab tiers) that are separate from and independent of the data folder. An account lets you sign in with Google, Gi...

On a folder that is shared via OneDrive / Google Drive / Dropbox / iCloud, everyone with read-write access can pick or create a username on the user-picker. Cloud accounts and the Lab tier are the preferred way to collaborate. See Shared Lab Accounts for the legacy shared-fold...

A password is optional only when you're a genuinely solo folder (one user, no PI). The moment a folder is shared (two or more users) or a PI is present, every account needs a login, so the password becomes required. To set one, open Settings from your avatar in the header and ...
Click your avatar in the top-right of the header, then Switch user . Pick another user from the list. Your view and data reload to that user's namespace.

If any accounts have been archived (a way to retire a member without deleting their data), they're hidden from the picker by default. A Show archived link appears below the user grid whenever archived accounts exist, so an occasional returner can reveal their tile and sign bac...

One user can be marked as the main user . They get a star badge on their avatar in the picker and are auto-selected as the default on the next visit. To set a main user, hover any user row on the picker and click the star (outline) icon that appears on the right. The main badg...

To rename a user, hover their row on the user-picker and click the pencil (edit) icon. The row switches to an inline input with the current username pre-filled. Edit the name and press Enter or click the green checkmark to save. Press Escape or the red X to cancel. The same us...

To delete a user, hover their row and click the trash icon. A two-step confirmation dialog appears with an optional "Archive data before deletion" checkbox (checked by default). The archive step downloads a ZIP of the user's folder before the directory is removed.

On the user-picker screen, click + Create New User and type a username in the input field. The app accepts letters, numbers, and underscores only (e.g., , ). Hyphens are not accepted and will trigger a validation error.
Click Create & Login .
A directory at is created and you're signed in. The app initializes a full subdirectory tree inside it, with , , , , , , , , , , , , and .
Once your user exists, you're signed in and the app is ready to go. Explore at your own pace. A good place to start is your first project, covered in Getting Started .

When you first set a password, the app shows a one-time recovery code (twelve words). Save it somewhere safe, because it's the only way back in if you forget the password. On the sign-in screen, click your account and choose Use your recovery code instead of the password. A pa...

The optional password above is for members and solo accounts. A PI (an account flagged ) is the exception. A PI must set a password during account setup before they can finish, and it's enforced at login. So there's never a PI account with no password behind it.

The user-picker screen, shown right after you connect a folder. (Screenshot pending recapture; the Lab Mode button shown in older captures has been removed and the BeakerBot brand mark added. Use ?wikiCapture=1 when recapturing.)
The user-picker screen with the + Create New User button, the existing-user list, and the BeakerBot brand mark in the gradient pill at the top.

entries.title: Demo Mode
headings: Three ways to try the app
Every name in the demo is invented
bodySnippets: An in-browser preview of ResearchOS, seeded with a fake yeast lab. No folder picker, nothing to install, edits disappear on reload.

Open (e.g., research-os-xi.vercel.app/demo , the canonical public URL as of this writing, so check the project site for the current link if it has moved) and you land straight inside ResearchOS as a researcher named alex , with a populated lab to click around in. Nothing touch...

The Home page opens with four projects, all prefixed DEMO: so it's obvious nothing here is real research. Each project has experiments on the Gantt, methods in the library, a couple of PCR protocols, a funded purchase list, and a few results pages with lab notes. Other seeded ...

Bare stays at in the URL bar and renders the Home page. Click any nav link (Gantt, Methods, Calendar) and the URL switches to the regular app path ( , ). Demo state follows you through those navigations via a sticky session flag, so the watermarked data and the demo affordance...
Three seeded users are available from the user-picker. It helps to know who is who, since exploring the lab-head views and the shared records means signing in as the right researcher.

There's also a fourth person in the data, Dr. Sam Whitley (member, archived). He's not in the user-picker because mira archived him on 2026-03-15, and archived users drop out of the sign-in roster and the assignment pickers. He's there to show what archiving does. His historic...

Everything you do in (dragging a Gantt bar, editing a lab note, adding a task, dropping in an image) stays in this browser tab. There's no disk write, no upload, no account. Refresh the tab and the demo resets to its seed state. Good for kicking the tires, not for real research.

Every demo experiment, goal, and event gets its start and end date rebased on the fly. Every date shifts by exactly the number of days since your last visit, so the demo's "now" lines up with your real now. Visit the demo this week and upcoming experiments stay upcoming, the G...

Method history, PCR protocol creation dates, and lab note timestamps stay put, because they read as history rather than schedule. Visit the demo one day, come back a month later, and the timeline slides forward without compounding drift, since each visit shifts only the days s...

Two small pill buttons live in the bottom-right corner and follow you across every route while the demo's sticky session flag is set, including wiki pages you may bounce to. Both use the same muted neutral pill style (surface-raised background, border, foreground-muted text) s...

Feature wiki pages with a demo-able view embed an amber inline call-out (the component) that drops you into for that view. Each call-site picks its own label, so you'll see variants like Try the Gantt view , Try methods in the demo , Try the Workbench , and Open the demo and t...

The muted Leave demo pill in the bottom-right follows you across every route while the demo flag is set. It's undismissable, so you never lose the exit. Clicking it opens a dialog titled Leave the demo? with two choices.
alex (member). The default sign-in. Owns all four DEMO: projects and is the user you start as on .
morgan (member). Shares one project with alex and drops comments into alex's threads, so any view that filters by shared records shows real cross-user activity.

Dr. Mira Castellanos (account_type ). The PI of the demo lab. Pick her to explore the lab-head views, the lab inbox, the PI audit log, the announcements, and the flag-for-review notifications. She owns the orange (#f97316) user color in the comment threads.

View as lab head / View as member. The demo signs you in as alex (a lab member) by default. The View as lab head pill switches the fixture identity to Mira , the demo's PI, so you can explore the Lab Overview dashboard and other PI-only surfaces. Clicking it again (labeled Vie...
Leave demo. An always-visible escape hatch. Clicking it opens the Leave the demo dialog described below. It never disappears so you always have a one-click exit.

The Leave demo button at the top resets all demo state in this tab and drops you on the connect screen, ready to pick a real folder (or start a new one). Demo edits are not saved to disk anywhere, so they go away with the reload. If you opened from a tab that was already conne...
The smaller Keep exploring the demo link at the bottom just closes the dialog if you clicked Leave demo by mistake.
(this page). Fastest. Open the URL, click around, no setup. Edits are temporary and vanish on reload. Best for a first look or for sharing a link with a labmate.

The "download as a starter folder" link. On the welcome page there is a link to download with the same seeded yeast lab. Unzip it, point ResearchOS at the unzipped folder, and your edits persist on disk for as long as you keep the folder around.

Connect a real folder. For actual research work. See Connecting Your Folder for the connect flow, and Shared Lab Accounts for putting that folder in OneDrive / Drive / Dropbox / iCloud so your whole lab shares it.

The demo lab is full of fake-on-purpose labels: , , , and so on. If you decide to export the demo as a starter folder, rename the projects before you start filing real data in there so nothing reads as a tutorial leftover later.
The demo with both floating pills visible. Leave demo is always present; View as lab head toggles the fixture identity.
The demo app with the muted Leave demo pill and View as lab head pill visible in the bottom-right corner.
The Leave Demo dialog. Confirm to reset and return to the connect screen, or keep exploring.
The Leave the demo dialog with a primary Leave demo button and a smaller Keep exploring link below.

entries.title: Exporting from LabArchives
headings: One ZIP can be re-imported later
bodySnippets: ResearchOS imports LabArchives notebooks by reading the Offline Notebook ZIP that LabArchives exports. This page walks you through producing that ZIP.

LabArchives is your ELN of record while you're running experiments. ResearchOS doesn't talk to LabArchives in real time, and it doesn't write anything back to your LabArchives account. The hand-off in between is a single file, the Offline Notebook ZIP , that LabArchives genera...

Once you have that ZIP downloaded, the ResearchOS importer reads it directly. You don't unzip it, and you don't need ResearchOS to be signed in to LabArchives. The next page over, Import from LabArchives , covers what the importer does with the file.
You don't need to open the ZIP yourself, but it helps to know what's in there.

Some inline images live in LabArchives' cloud as URLs rather than bundled bytes. The Offline Notebook ZIP keeps the URL but not the image itself for those. ResearchOS handles that on import through a separate Fetch images step, which the import page covers.
With the ZIP downloaded, head to Import from LabArchives to walk through the ResearchOS-side wizard. You'll pick the format, drop the ZIP, preview what's inside, map folders to projects, and apply.
One HTML file per notebook page, holding the page title, every entry on that page, and the entry text.
A subdirectory of attachments for each page, where image uploads and other bundled files sit alongside the HTML.
A top-level manifest that describes the notebook tree, the export timestamp, and who triggered the export.
Sign in to LabArchives at mynotebook.labarchives.com (or your institution's LabArchives URL) in a normal browser tab.

Open the notebook you want to bring into ResearchOS from the notebook switcher in the top-left. Export is one notebook at a time, so if you want several notebooks in ResearchOS, plan to repeat this flow for each.

Open the ≡ Tools menu in the top toolbar and pick Utilities → Create Offline Notebook . (Older builds park this under More → Offline Notebook or Notebook Settings → Export . You're after the same item either way.)
Confirm the request when LabArchives prompts. The request goes into a queue, and LabArchives builds the ZIP server-side. You can close the tab while it works.
Wait for the email. LabArchives mails you a link as soon as the ZIP is ready. Small notebooks take a minute or two, large ones with many attachments can take much longer.

Click the link in the email and download the ZIP to your computer. The file is usually named something like . Save it somewhere you can find later (your Downloads folder is fine), and don't unzip it . ResearchOS reads the archive as a single file.

LabArchives occasionally renames menu items or moves them between toolbars. If a label below doesn't match what you see on screen, look for the same underlying concept. You're asking LabArchives to produce an Offline Notebook export of a single notebook. Other names you might ...

ResearchOS's importer is idempotent against the same ZIP. If you re-run the import on the same file, pages you've already imported are skipped. If you export a fresher ZIP from LabArchives later and run it through, only the new pages land. See Re-running the import for the det...

entries.title: User archiving
bodySnippets: When a lab member leaves (graduates, rotates out, switches projects), archiving their account is the right move. Archiving hides them from the surfaces where you pick someone, but keeps every byte of their data on disk and in the lab's history. They drop out of the login picke...
These two paths do different jobs.
Archive is reversible. Unarchive a member and they reappear on every surface as if they never left. Delete is permanent (modulo any archive ZIP you chose to download at delete time).

Only a lab head can archive a member. Open Settings and choose Lab Roster from the left rail. The roster lists every member. Hover any row to reveal an Archive button on the right (you can also right-click a row for the same action). Being a lab head is enough to proceed. Clic...
One archive is a single action that does a few things at once.

Archived members do not disappear from the Lab Roster. They sit at the bottom of the same table in a muted style with an Archived badge, and the row shows a Restore button where active rows show Archive. One click (after the same confirmation dialog) brings them back on every ...

The login picker is the one place archived members are hidden by default. When a lab has archived accounts, a small Show archived link appears below the account grid. Click it to reveal former members so you can sign in as one of them to check their work.
The right tool for "this account was a test that should not exist" is delete, not archive. Archive is for real members whose work you want to keep.

Archive hides the user from active surfaces while preserving their on-disk data. Tasks they own, notes they wrote, purchases they logged, all stay where they are. Anyone with read access still sees them. The archived user just stops appearing in the login picker, the @mention ...

Delete removes the user's folder from disk entirely. Their data goes with them. The delete affordance lives on the user picker (a trash icon on each row, documented in Creating a user ) and is the right tool only when you genuinely want the data gone (a test account, a duplica...
Stamps the member's onboarding sidecar as archived, with the time and the lab head who did it.
Hides the member from the login picker by default (see filter control below).

Drops the member from the active-member jump list on the Lab Overview, so the people you can quick-jump to are active first (an archived member only resurfaces when you drill into their row to restore them).
Filters them out of the @mention picker, the share dialog, and the assignee dropdown, so you do not assign new work to someone who left.

Keeps their records (tasks, notes, purchases) readable to anyone who already had access. No permissions are stripped, and the comments and announcements they already wrote stay exactly where they are.
Writes an entry to the audit log recording the actor, the target, and the timestamp. A restore writes a matching entry.
A graduate student finished and is no longer doing lab work, but their results and notes still need to be readable for the next person who picks up the project.
A rotation student wrapped up and you do not want them turning up in the assignee dropdown when you plan the next round of work.
An undergrad worked over the summer and is gone for the school year. Archive now, unarchive when they return.

Members never see an "archived" banner on their own account. If a lab head archives someone who later signs back in, that person signs in normally and sees their data as they left it. Only the shared lab surfaces stop offering them as a pick. The audit log is the source of tru...
The LabRoster in Settings. Hover a member row to surface the Archive action.
The LabRoster table in Settings with three members listed and an Archive button revealed on hover for one of the rows.

entries.title: Welcome Tour (BeakerBot)
headings: What the welcome tour actually is
Phase 2, walkthrough on your real account
How feature picks change which tabs you see
Open the experiment, meet the Methods tab
bodySnippets: BeakerBot's welcome tour was a guided walkthrough on your real account. It opened with a short setup Q&A, then dropped you into a live tour that helped you create your first project, method, and experiment right in the app. The tour has been retired (it asked for too much hand...

When you create a brand-new ResearchOS user, BeakerBot pops up to show you around. The tour runs on your real account, against your real local-first data folder. Anything BeakerBot creates (a sample project, a placeholder method, a first experiment) lands on disk just like nor...
Three things are true about every run.

Nobody, by default. The auto-fire path is off, so a fresh account lands straight on the normal empty state with no tour, no banner, and no nag. The rest of this page describes how the tour behaved while it was live, kept for reference and for the parts that still ship (the set...

The first few minutes are a short Q&A that BeakerBot uses to shape the rest of the tour and which tabs appear in your sidebar. Every question is a radio pick with a sensible default if you click Skip this step .

The first call is whether you are flying solo (one user, your own account) or running a multi-person lab (everyone points their ResearchOS at the same shared folder). The pick determines whether the PI follow-up question (Q1c, below) fires.

Conditional on Q1 = Lab. After picking Lab, BeakerBot follows up with a single binary question. Are you the PI, or are you a member? Picking PI sets to on this account. Picking Member leaves it at the default. One person in the lab fills the PI slot. The picker badge in the lo...
Four questions about ResearchOS surfaces and preferences. The numbering skips Q5, that slot was retired before launch.
These answers drive both which tabs appear in your sidebar and which conditional walkthrough clusters fire in Phase 2.

The final setup question asks whether you want a tab for saving bookmarks, things like VPN links, lab calendars, freezer inventory spreadsheets, manuscript drafts, and so on. Each card holds a URL plus a label so you can jump straight to the resource.

The tab is labeled Links for every account type. On a lab account it is a shared tab visible to everyone in the lab. Saying yes to Q7 adds the tab and fires the conditional walkthrough later in Phase 2.

This is where the tour starts touching real data. BeakerBot walks a universal sequence covering the major surfaces (Home, Project, Notifications, Workbench, the editor, Methods, Gantt, Settings, Search, and the wiki pointer), followed by four conditional clusters that fire onl...

The walkthrough was deliberately trimmed. Most clusters are pitched at awareness now. BeakerBot explains what a surface is for and why you would seek it out, lands one or two live examples so the page is not empty, then moves on. The aim is to leave you knowing the feature exi...

Projects are the top-level container that every experiment, method, and task hangs off, so the tour opens by making one. These two beats are hands-on. You click and type, because there is no better way to learn the create flow than to run it once.

The project page is where every experiment, method, and task you attach to a project comes back together in one view. The Overview box at the top is yours to fill in (hypothesis, motivation, why this project exists). The Results, Methods, and Activity tabs next to it surface a...

Two surfaces live in the top bar. The bell collects anything that needs your attention (reminders for upcoming work, updates from labmates, mentions on your writeups), and the inbox next to it collects files sent in from outside the app, like photos from a phone companion or s...

Methods are the recipe, and the Workbench is where you actually run them. Every experiment gets its own entry with space for notes, results, attached protocols, and files. This is the page you spend most of your time on, so the create flow stays hands-on across two beats.
The methods detour is set up with a single framing beat before the editor cluster, so you know where reusable protocols get pinned to a run before you go build one.

ResearchOS uses one editor everywhere, in project overviews, standalone notes, method writeups, and the experiment notes you are looking at now. It is inline-only. You just type and your markdown renders as you go, so there is no edit mode to toggle. The old markdown deep-dive...

After the editor, BeakerBot introduces the standalone Notes and Lists panels on the Workbench. The cluster was collapsed to two explanation beats. The tool is friendly enough that you only need to know what notes and lists are, so the three create demos were cut.

Methods are your reusable protocol library. Write a technique once here, then attach it to every experiment that uses it instead of rewriting the steps each time. The cluster was collapsed from five beats to three. BeakerBot asks what kind of technique you run, opens the New M...

Now that a method exists, the tour returns to the experiment to pin it. These two beats reopen the experiment popup on its Methods tab and teach the mental model. Methods are the protocol template, variation notes are the per-run delta.
Six universal beats teach core Gantt mechanics. Lab accounts see an additional six-beat share-feature cluster.
Lab accounts only (gated on Q1 = lab). Six share-feature beats follow the universal arc.
Goals overview (gated on Q4 = yes). A single step fires after the share cluster and explains the Goals overlay on the Gantt toolbar.

Settings is the last stop on the universal arc. BeakerBot opens with a narration beat that establishes scope (everything about the account, from appearance and visible tabs to integrations, the AI Helper prompt, and the re-run button), then walks two personalization beats on t...
search-demo. BeakerBot opens the Search tab and live-types a query that matches the experiment you created earlier. The highlighted result lands in the list.

The final universal cluster introduces the help icon in the top-right of the AppShell. It was collapsed from four beats to two. The two cursor demos that navigated into the wiki and back were cut for a single icon, and the click-and-return behavior folded into the icon-spotlig...
Four conditional clusters fire after the wiki-pointer cluster. Each cluster gates on the matching Q answer being yes .
The final step is . BeakerBot says "You're set! Here's to many great experiments ahead." and presents a single Let's go button.
Clicking Let's go triggers the outro.
Clicking I've got it from here at any earlier step skips the intervening steps and jumps directly to .

BeakerBot is the canonical ResearchOS mascot, a sky-blue chemistry beaker with pastel-rainbow liquid, dot eyes, and measurement-mark cheek dashes. The voice is funny and playful throughout. The tour draws from nine or more poses, chosen contextually.

A persistent link in the footer of every step. Click it, confirm in the sub-modal, and BeakerBot jumps to . The run gets recorded as a skip rather than a completion, so re-running from Settings still works.
Each step (after the intro) has a Skip this step link. If a later step depends on the artifact this step creates, BeakerBot silently creates a placeholder version with .
Closing the tab partway through writes the current step plus every artifact created so far into . The next time you open ResearchOS with this folder, a small modal gives you three options.
Clicking Re-run tour in Settings > Onboarding performs an inline that clears , , , , , , and in a single write. The controller then calls to re-mount the tour in place. No page reload.
Your Q1 through Q7 answers determine the visible-tab set through two helpers in .
The rules work like this.

Settings can manually hide a tab that the picks would otherwise show. Settings cannot unhide a tab that the picks excluded. To get a hidden tab back, re-run the tour and flip the matching Q answer, or toggle it on directly in Settings > Tabs .

In development builds, a small BeakerBot button sits in the bottom-right floating cluster alongside the data-folder and switch-user controls. Clicking it opens a dropdown with three escape hatches for driving the tour without walking it from scratch. The whole control is gated...
There are three actions.
The tour is one continuous experience inside the app, not a separate demo sandbox. There is no fake fixture you discard on close.

BeakerBot drives the pacing with a Got it, next or Next button on each step. You can also click Skip this step on any individual step, or the persistent I've got it from here link in the footer to jump straight to the goodbye screen.
Closing the tab mid-tour is safe. The next time you open the folder, BeakerBot offers Resume , Restart , or Discard .
Q2: Will you track lab purchases? (Yes / No / Maybe later)
Q3: Want calendar feeds? (Yes / No / Maybe later)
Q4: Want a goal-tracking page? (Yes / No / Maybe later)

Q6: AI Helper prompt size. ResearchOS can paste a system prompt into Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini so the assistant understands ResearchOS terminology. Full is the default. Medium and Minimal trim the prompt for smaller-context models. No or Maybe later skips the AI Helper tour c...

Purchases (Q2 = yes). An eight-step cluster in two phases. Phase 1 teaches on your empty page (intro, create-button click, form fill, autocomplete demo). Phase 2 warps into a read-only viewer over Alex's demo account to show the analytics surface, then navigates back.
Calendar (Q3 = yes). The step covers the inline calendar-feed subscribe flow. The new feed appears in the Calendar tab when you click Next.
Links (Q7 = yes). The step walks the bookmark tab, adding a card with a URL and label, and notes that on a lab account the tab is shared with everyone in the lab.
A full-screen overlay mounts. BeakerBot cheers and confetti fires (~1.8 s).
BeakerBot shifts to waving pose and translates off-screen (~1.8 s).
The overlay fades out (~0.8 s). The route lands on .
Auto-cleanup runs silently in the background, removing demo artifacts and leaving your first real project intact.
A small toast in the lower-right reads "Tour complete. Find BeakerBot again in Settings -> Onboarding." It auto-dismisses after 4 s.
Idle , the always-on baseline bob.
Waving , the welcome screen and the resume modal.
Thinking , head-tilt during setup Q1 to Q7 and the PI prompt.

entries.title: Integrations
bodySnippets: Optional add-ons that connect ResearchOS to outside services. You set each one up only if you want it, and nothing here runs until you do.

entries.title: Calendar Feeds
headings: Add a calendar by ICS URL
Where to copy the ICS URL from
What URLs the server proxy will fetch
Want to edit linked events from ResearchOS?
Secret URLs leak your whole calendar
bodySnippets: Overlay events from Google, Outlook, iCloud, or any public iCal URL on the ResearchOS calendar. Read-only, so you can plan around them without ResearchOS ever writing back to the source.

Each linked calendar shows its events on the Calendar page in the color you pick, sitting alongside ResearchOS tasks and events. Clicking an event opens a popup with the title, time, and any location, notes, or link the source calendar published.

Linked calendars are read-only. ResearchOS fetches a public iCal (ICS) URL on a timer and overlays the events. It never writes back to Google, Outlook, or Apple. To edit an event, open it in its source app, and the change syncs into ResearchOS on the next 15-minute refresh.
Go to the Calendar page. The Linked Calendars button sits top-right, next to + New Event . A small blue badge on the button counts how many subscriptions are currently turned on.
Inside the panel, the Where do I find this URL? link expands a per-provider checklist that matches whichever Provider you have selected. The summaries below are the same steps in long form.
Pick Other (any public iCal URL) in the Provider dropdown. That covers university course schedules, lab seminar series, conference timetables, sports schedules, anything that ends in or starts with .
Each subscription becomes a row in the Connected list at the top of the panel. Here's what each row gives you.

If a feed fails to fetch three times in a row (a usual sign the share URL expired or was revoked), ResearchOS stops auto-retrying it and the row shows Stopped syncing - the link may be broken or expired . Click Retry now after fixing the link, or remove the subscription and re...

ResearchOS refetches each feed at most once every 15 minutes per browser session. The fetch goes through a small server proxy (the same Vercel function that bypasses CORS for Google, Outlook, and iCloud), and the proxy caches the response on its own edge cache for another 15 m...
If you just added an event in the source calendar and want it on the ResearchOS grid right now, hard-refresh the browser tab or wait out the 15-minute window.

The proxy is the small server endpoint that fetches each ICS URL on your behalf, because browsers can't fetch directly (Google doesn't set the right CORS headers). The proxy refuses a few classes of URL outright so it can't be used to scan internal infrastructure.
If a paste fails with one of those errors, double-check that the URL is the public ICS variant of the calendar (not the regular web-app share link).

Repeating events (e.g., a weekly seminar) are expanded into one chip per occurrence inside a ±2-year window around today. That keeps a 20-year-long daily recurrence from spawning ~7,300 chips on the grid. If you scroll the calendar more than two years backward or forward, repe...

Linked calendars are read-only by design. To make changes, open the event in its source app (Google Calendar, Outlook, Apple Calendar). Your edit shows up in ResearchOS on the next 15-minute refresh. Earlier ResearchOS builds had an OAuth-backed two-way sync for Google and Out...
Open in a desktop browser.
Click the gear -> Settings -> Settings for my calendars .
Pick the calendar you want to share in the sidebar.
Copy Secret address in iCal format for a private calendar, or Public address in iCal format if the calendar is already public.
Open Outlook on the web -> Settings -> Calendar -> Shared calendars .
Under Publish a calendar , pick the calendar, choose Can view all details , and click Publish .
Copy the ICS link Outlook generates.
Open the Calendar app on macOS, or sign in to in a browser.
Right-click the calendar in the sidebar -> Share Calendar -> Public Calendar .
Copy the share link. It starts with , which is fine to paste as-is.
The label you gave it, plus a small uppercase tag with the provider type.
The full ICS URL, truncated to one line.
A Last synced timestamp once ResearchOS has fetched the feed at least once.
A 5-swatch color strip on the left edge of the row. Click any swatch to recolor that calendar's events on the grid.
An On / Off checkbox to hide a calendar's events without removing the subscription.
A red Remove link to drop the subscription. A confirmation dialog reminds you that ResearchOS events aren't touched.
Anything that isn't (so no , no , no ). Plain is rejected outright for security. The prefix is rewritten to first, so iCloud URLs still work.
and the loopback range .
The private IPv4 ranges , , and .
Link-local and the IPv6 equivalents ( , , ).
Responses that don't start with . This catches the case where a calendar share URL redirects to an HTML sign-in page instead of returning iCal text.
Click Linked Calendars , then scroll the panel down to Or paste a public iCal / ICS URL .
Pick the source from the Provider dropdown (iCloud / Apple Calendar, Google Calendar, Outlook / Office 365, or Other (any public iCal URL)).
Type a Label (this is what shows up on the Calendar page) and click one of the 10 swatches under Color .
Paste the URL into the ICS URL field and click Add Calendar . The button shows Testing... while ResearchOS fetches the feed to confirm it parses.
If the feed parses but contains no events within a ±2-year window of today, you'll get a confirmation dialog before the subscription saves. That's the normal case for stale or empty calendars.
Paste a URL exactly as iCloud (or any other client) gives it to you. The server proxy rewrites it to automatically before fetching.

Google's Secret address in iCal format grants read access to every event title, time, and note in that calendar to anyone who has the URL. Treat it like a password (e.g., don't paste it into Slack, a public repo, or a shared notes doc). ResearchOS keeps the URL in your private...

The edge cache also serves the previous response while fetching a fresh copy in the background (stale-while-revalidate, up to 1 hour). In the worst case an event you just added could take up to 75 minutes to appear (15 min client window + 60 min stale window). Hard-refreshing ...
The Linked Calendars panel with a connected ICS subscription and the paste-a-URL form.

entries.title: LabArchives
headings: Exporting an Offline Notebook ZIP from LabArchives
Path 2, a manual folder or ZIP drop
Skip image fetch on the first pass
bodySnippets: Bring notebooks out of LabArchives and into ResearchOS as native, file-on-disk tasks, and recover the inline images that LabArchives keeps in the cloud, all without any API credentials.

LabArchives and ResearchOS sit at opposite ends of a hand-off, not in a live sync. LabArchives is your ELN of record while you're running experiments, and ResearchOS is where you bring those notebooks once you want them on disk in a structured form. There's no two-way connecti...
Everything runs through one entry point under Settings -> LabArchives .
