LabArchives stores inline images in two ways internally. The legacy path, called Form-A , embeds the image as a binary file inside the notebook page entry itself. The newer path, Form-B , uploads the image to LabArchives' cloud and leaves only a URL behind in the entry body.

When you generate an Offline Notebook export ZIP, only the Form-A images come along. The Form-B URLs are still in the entry text, but the image bytes were never bundled. In a typical recent notebook that's roughly half the inline images.

If you import the ZIP without fetching Form-B images, the wizard writes a placeholder for each one. Your notes still come across, and the images at those URLs aren't there yet. You can recover them at any time using one of the three paths described below, or remove individual ...
When images are recovered, inline images land in the task's folder and the markdown is rewritten to point at the local copy. File attachments (non-image uploads) land in .
The ZIP that ResearchOS imports is LabArchives' built-in Offline Notebook export. Both the import and any image-recovery run depend on having this file first.

Three paths exist to pull Form-B images into your notes. All three are credential-free, so ResearchOS never asks for your LabArchives password. The wizard hides its Fetch images step in demo mode, but the per-task Rehydrate panel and these recovery paths still work there, sinc...

The Generate browser script tab (in the wizard's Fetch images step, or in the per-task Rehydrate panel) shows a self-contained JavaScript snippet you paste into the browser console while you're logged into LabArchives. Here's what the script does.
Drop the downloaded ZIP into the Drop your own images tab (or drag it straight into the panel) and ResearchOS matches filenames automatically.

If you already have the Form-B image files on disk (from a previous download, a screenshot batch, or an export from another tool), use the Drop your own images tab. Drop a folder or a containing the image files and ResearchOS matches them to the expected filenames automaticall...

This path also accepts the ZIP produced by the DevTools script above, which is why the two-tab flow is designed the way it is. Generate and download in the DevTools tab, then drop the result in the manual tab.
After import, any task that still has unresolved Form-B images shows broken image placeholders in the Lab Notes editor. Clicking a broken placeholder opens a small popup with three options.

Import from LabArchives is the import wizard. You download an Offline Notebook ZIP from LabArchives and feed it to the wizard. Every notebook page becomes a ResearchOS task, and every folder becomes a project you can map to your existing project list. No credentials, works off...
Generate browser script is the DevTools-script path (see below).
Drop your own images is the manual folder/ZIP drop path (see below).
Checks that it's running on . It refuses to do anything on other origins.
Fetches each missing image URL using your existing session cookies (no password, no API key, only the session the browser already has from your login).
Packages all successful fetches into a single ZIP and triggers one browser download.

Find on LabArchives opens the original image URL in a new tab pointed at . If you're logged in, the image loads and you can right-click and Save. Then use Replace from disk below, or drag the saved file onto the note.

Replace from disk opens a file picker. Pick a local image file, and ResearchOS writes it to , rewrites the markdown reference, and removes the entry from the task's sidecar so the popup doesn't re-appear on the next open.
Remove reference deletes the broken placeholder from the markdown entirely. Use this when you don't have the image and don't need it.
Open the notebook you want to export in LabArchives on the web.
Open the ≡ menu -> Utilities -> Create Offline Notebook . (The exact wording varies a little by institution build.)
Wait. Large notebooks take minutes to assemble, and LabArchives emails you when the ZIP is ready.
Download the ZIP from the email link. Don't unzip it, since ResearchOS reads the raw archive.
Go to Settings -> LabArchives and click Open import... on the Import from LabArchives card.
On the Choose format step, leave the default (Offline Notebook ZIP). The PDF and Chrome-print formats are sketched in as coming-soon and stay disabled.
Drag the Offline Notebook ZIP into the Upload ZIP step. The wizard parses the bundle and surfaces a preview of pages, folders, and inline images.
On the Preview notebook step, glance through the page list and confirm the entry counts look right.

On the Map projects step, point each LabArchives folder at one of your existing ResearchOS projects, or let the wizard create a new project named . If that name is already taken, the wizard appends a number ( , ) so you never end up with two projects sharing a name.

If the wizard detected Form-B images (and you're not in demo mode), a Fetch images step appears with two tabs. Generate browser script is the DevTools-script path (see below). Drop your own images is the manual folder/ZIP drop path (see below). Either fetch images now or click...
The Apply step writes everything to disk. When it's finished, the Done step lists what landed where.
In the Fetch images wizard step (or per-task Rehydrate panel), click the Generate browser script tab and copy the script.
Open LabArchives in your browser and navigate to any page of the notebook you imported. You just need to be logged in.
Open DevTools (F12 or right-click and Inspect), switch to the Console tab, paste the script, and press Enter.
The script logs progress in the console and triggers a single ZIP download when done. Save the ZIP somewhere easy to find.
Switch back to ResearchOS and drop the ZIP into the Drop your own images tab. The panel shows how many filenames matched and stages them for import.
Click Continue with N images . The Apply step writes them to and rewrites the markdown references.

It's fine to skip image recovery during the initial import. Form-B images become placeholders you can clean up later. The broken-image popup in any imported note lets you handle them one at a time, and the per-task "Rehydrate images" panel lets you batch them all at once.

Each notebook page becomes a directory under with a body alongside a folder that holds a subfolder for file attachments, an subfolder for inline images, and an for provenance. Tasks are marked complete, with and set to the page's newest entry timestamp.

Importing a notebook you've already brought over is safe. Pages that haven't changed are recognized and left alone. If a page was edited in LabArchives after your last import (new entries, or an existing entry updated), the Preview step flags it and lets you tick which changed...

The script runs entirely in your browser. ResearchOS never sees the image bytes until you explicitly drop the resulting ZIP back into the wizard. The URLs are hard-coded into the script (no dynamic eval, no network round-trip back to ResearchOS). The flag only attaches cookies...

If you saved an image via "Find on LabArchives" and it filename-matches an outstanding placeholder, dragging it straight onto the note triggers the same recovery pipeline as the file picker, no need to open the popup first.

entries.title: Security
headings: What briefly touches a server we operate
What we collect, and what we don't
Public hosting is opt-in, and the proxy is open
Live collaboration uploads a readable copy
The easy way is to open Settings
The worst case is your last good save
bodySnippets: Your research data stays on your computer. We never see it. This page is the plain-English version of what that means in practice, where the narrow exceptions are, and how you can verify the claim for yourself.

ResearchOS is built as a browser-only app that reads and writes a folder you pick on your own machine. Your notes, results, project and task data, images, attachments, and calendar subscriptions all stay in that folder. Nothing about your work is uploaded to a database we cont...

When you pick a data folder, ResearchOS reads and writes everything directly to that folder on your machine. The bytes never leave it. You can open the folder at any time in your operating system's file browser and see exactly what is there.
Here is what the folder holds.

A few routes on the ResearchOS server are involved. Two are CORS-bypass streams that exist because browsers refuse to talk directly to the upstream service. One is the optional AI assistant. The last two are anonymous telemetry, a page-view ping and a feature-usage beacon. Non...

The calendar CORS-bypass proxy uses the most defensive shape we know how to write, with HTTPS only, private-IP blocking, redirect re-validation, byte cap, timeout, content-type denylist, and per-IP rate limiting. The Vercel Analytics endpoint is a Vercel-owned script and beaco...

We collect anonymous page-view pings via Vercel Web Analytics. When you navigate between pages, anonymous beacons go to Vercel. No IDs, no folder contents, no typed text, no markdown bodies. We use this to know which pages get used and which sit idle. Settings -> Offline mode ...

We also collect anonymous feature-usage counts via , such as how often a share gets sent. These carry allow-listed enum and boolean flags only, never an ID, name, or anything you typed, and they ride the same Offline mode gate.

We do not collect anything else. No Sentry, no Google Analytics, no Mixpanel, no PostHog, no Hotjar, no Datadog, no Amplitude. No background phone-home. No crash reporter. No content telemetry. Running against the repo will confirm only is present, and the network tab will con...

The Report an issue button does not auto-submit anything. When you click it, your browser opens a pre-filled GitHub issue URL in a new tab. You see the body, edit it, and click Submit . Nothing happens until you do.
These claims hold, but they have implications worth understanding before you trust ResearchOS with anything sensitive.
You don't have to take any of this on faith. There are two ways to confirm what the app is doing. The first is built into Settings and takes about thirty seconds.
Your browser already shows every network request the app makes. This is the audit-grade path for anyone who wants to see the bytes themselves.

ResearchOS went through an internal security audit on 2026-05-15. It closed one Critical finding (cross-site scripting via unsanitized markdown HTML across all 8 markdown rendering sites in the app) and several Important findings around the proxy routes, the LabArchives creden...
If you find something that looks wrong, open a GitHub issue (the Report an issue button is the fastest path) or email the maintainer directly. We'd much rather hear about a problem early.

A subtler kind of data loss has nothing to do with the network and everything to do with timing. If an app is halfway through writing a file when the tab closes, the laptop sleeps, or the process crashes, a naive implementation can leave the file zero-byte or truncated, and th...

Every save writes to a temporary file first, lets that file finish landing durably on disk, and only then atomically moves it into place over the real file. The move is the kind of operation that either fully happens or does not happen at all, with no in-between state on disk....

A trust claim is only as good as the thing that keeps it true over time. The whole app is gated by automated tests that run on every commit and every pull request to . That gate runs linting, a full TypeScript typecheck, the Vitest unit and integration suite with coverage, and...
Every experiment, lab note, and result you write.
Every image, PDF, and arbitrary attachment you drop into a note.
The JSON for projects, tasks, dependencies, methods, and PCR protocols.
Your calendar subscription URLs and the events they pulled in.
The Argon2id-protected credentials in guarding your per-user profile.

Calendar feed sync. When you subscribe to an iCal URL (Google, Outlook, iCloud, a university calendar), the browser asks to fetch the iCal text from the upstream and stream it back. The subscription URL travels in the request header rather than in the URL query string, so it i...

ORCID publication lookup. When you view a researcher profile that lists an ORCID iD (during sharing or profile setup), the browser asks to fetch that person's public publication list from the ORCID API and hand it back. ORCID's API isn't CORS-open, so the call has to go server...

BeakerBot AI assistant. When you send a message to the built-in AI assistant (BeakerBot), the browser calls , which forwards the conversation to the Fireworks inference API and streams the response back. The route is only active when is on. It stores nothing server-side. No me...

Vercel Web Analytics. When you navigate between pages, your browser sends an anonymous page-view beacon to Vercel telling them which route you visited. No IDs, no folder contents, no typed text, no markdown bodies, no project names. Vercel sees your IP address (which they hash...

Feature-usage beacon. When you use a tracked feature like sending a share or publishing a directory profile, the browser fires a small anonymous beacon to so the operator dashboard can count how often features get used. The payload is allow-listed enum and boolean flags only (...
The Data inventory panel lists every file path the app has written and every IndexedDB key in use, alongside an External Calls section that names each outbound endpoint in plain English.
The Offline mode toggle disables the proxy routes plus the Vercel Analytics script tag in one click, for anyone who wants zero outbound network from the app surface.
"Where is this stored?" hints on each integration field so you always know which file holds the credential you just entered.
holds the opaque File System Access handle for your data folder. This is what gives the browser permission to read and write the folder you picked.
holds three small session-routing strings, the folder name plus its grant timestamp, the currently signed-in user, and (if you are a PI signed in) the primary account.
Open ResearchOS in Chrome or Edge, then open DevTools. F12 works on Windows and Linux. Cmd + Option + I works on macOS.
Switch to the Network tab and check Preserve log so refreshes don't clear the view.
Reload the page. Visit Calendar, the inbox, and the experiments you care about. Watch every outbound request as you go.

You should see requests to your own ResearchOS origin (for JavaScript, CSS, and static assets), occasional requests to when a subscribed feed refreshes, a one-off request to if you open a researcher profile that lists an ORCID iD, and occasional requests to and for anonymous p...

For a second pass, switch from the Network tab to Application -> IndexedDB . ResearchOS uses two IndexedDB databases. holds the opaque File System Access handle for your data folder. This is what gives the browser permission to read and write the folder you picked. holds three...

Open your data folder in Finder or Explorer. Every experiment, note, image, and attachment you have written is sitting there as a regular file you can read. ResearchOS writes to that folder and nowhere else. Quit the app and your data stays exactly where it is.

If you share your data folder with a lab-mate over OneDrive, Dropbox, Google Drive, or iCloud, every byte in it is theirs to read. The per-user password gate stops accidental account-switching inside the app, not someone opening the folder in their own file browser.

Account credentials are protected with Argon2id (t=3, m=64 MiB), stored in , so a snooping co-located user cannot trivially extract them, but the research data itself is stored as plain JSON on disk. Anyone with direct disk access to the folder can read it. Turn on OS-level fu...

If you self-host ResearchOS on a public Vercel deploy with no auth gate, anyone on the internet can hit the calendar-feed proxy route (subject to rate limiting). This is not a data-exfiltration risk because the proxy has no access to your data folder, but it does mean someone ...

One-time sends to someone outside your folder are end-to-end encrypted, the relay holds only ciphertext and we cannot read it. Live real-time collaboration is the exception. When you co-edit a note or a shared notebook live, the shared document is synced to our servers in read...

ResearchOS ships three affordances inside Settings. The Data inventory panel lists every file path the app has written and every IndexedDB key in use, alongside an External Calls section that names each outbound endpoint in plain English. The Offline mode toggle disables the p...

A torn write can only ever leave the old file contents intact, never a corrupt or empty one. The atomic move is the whole point. Either the new version replaces the old one cleanly, or the old one is still there.
The Report an Issue modal. You see the body, you edit the body, you choose whether to submit.

The Report an Issue modal, showing the Type radio group, an editable Title field, an editable description, optional auto-attached error details, and the Cancel / Copy Link / Create GitHub Issue buttons at the bottom.
Data inventory panel in Settings. Every file path and every outbound endpoint is listed here.
The Data inventory panel in Settings, listing every file path the app has written and the External Calls section naming each outbound endpoint.
Offline mode toggle in Settings. One click disables the analytics script and all proxy routes.
The Offline mode toggle in Settings, shown in the on position, with the note that the Vercel Analytics script and proxy routes are disabled.

entries.title: Shared Lab Accounts
headings: Setup steps (every member runs these once)
Want to feel out the shared model first?
The one rule, keep the folder available offline
The PI sees the lab roll-up
bodySnippets: One folder, shared across your whole lab. Everyone keeps their own data, and the PI's dashboard rolls it all up.

Put your ResearchOS folder inside a cloud-synced folder (e.g., OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, or iCloud). Every lab member points ResearchOS at that same folder on their own computer. Inside the folder, each member picks a different username, so their data lives at , , and s...
The cloud provider handles syncing files between machines. ResearchOS has no idea any of this is happening, it just reads and writes JSON files like normal.
Before you start using ResearchOS, open the shared folder in Finder (macOS) or Explorer (Windows) and confirm:
Files show as fully downloaded (i.e., no cloud icon, no "online only" badge, no "available when online" tag).
You can open a sample file with the network disconnected.
The folder's "Size on disk" in its properties dialog is non-zero and roughly matches the "Size".
Don't put the folder in two different cloud providers. Pick one. Stacking OneDrive and Dropbox on the same folder corrupts JSON files when both try to write.
Don't use the same username on two laptops. If Sarah signs in as on her laptop and also on a shared lab computer at the same time, the two ResearchOS sessions will overwrite each other's files.
Don't edit files outside ResearchOS while the app is open. The app caches data in memory, so external edits won't be picked up until you reload the page.
Install the cloud provider's desktop app (not just the website). OneDrive on Windows is pre-installed. Drive for desktop, Dropbox, and iCloud Drive each ship as a separate download.
One person creates an empty folder named e.g. inside the synced area, then shares it with the rest of the lab via the cloud provider's normal share flow.
Each member accepts the share and confirms the folder is now visible on their laptop's filesystem (Finder / Explorer).
Each member follows their provider's instructions below to make the folder always available offline .
Each member opens ResearchOS, clicks Open a folder , and picks that shared folder.
On the user-picker, each member clicks Create New User and types their own username.

Open Demo Mode for a no-setup preview. It seeds two researchers (alex and morgan) plus their PI (Mira) into one shared folder, so cross-user data and the PI rollup are already wired up for you to click around in.

If your lab uses ResearchOS cloud accounts, the PI creates a lab and invites members by email. Members accept and the lab covers their storage from the shared pool. No shared folder is required for this path. See Accounts and tiers for the invite and join flow. The shared-fold...

ResearchOS NEEDS to read and write the folder directly on disk. If your cloud provider keeps the folder "online only" (i.e., a placeholder file that downloads on demand), writes will fail or be silently dropped. EVERY member needs to flip the "always keep local" switch for the...

Once everyone is set up, the PI sees a customizable dashboard at with widgets for cross-lab activity, purchases, member workload, announcements, and more. See Lab Overview . If you only want one labmate's work surfaced on your own Home page (rather than the whole lab), they ca...

entries.title: Box
headings: Make it Available Offline (every member, every laptop)
Where the folder lives on disk
Enterprise policy can block Make Available Offline
bodySnippets: Box Drive streams files on demand. Mark the ResearchOS folder Available Offline so it stays on disk.

Download Box Drive from box.com/resources/downloads . You need the desktop client running. The web interface at is not enough. ResearchOS needs the desktop app because that's what creates the local filesystem folder you point ResearchOS at.

Box Drive streams files on demand by default, so the folder shows up as a placeholder until you open something. ResearchOS reads directly from disk, so anything still cloud-only breaks. You need to mark the lab folder as available offline on every laptop.
Windows : under (e.g. ).
macOS : under on recent macOS versions, or on older installs.

Files revert to cloud-only. If a colleague opens the shared folder's properties and chooses Free up space , the folder switches back to streaming and ResearchOS can't read it. Re-run Make Available Offline .

Conflict files. If two members edit the same file while offline, Box creates a copy named next to the original. Delete the conflicted copy in Finder / Explorer. ResearchOS only reads the canonical name.

Collaborator vs. owner storage. Shared folders on Box count against the folder owner's quota, not each collaborator's. Pick someone with enough quota (usually the lab's primary Box account) to own the folder.

Single sign-on re-auth. University Box accounts on SSO occasionally drop the Box Drive session and stop syncing without an obvious error. If files stop updating, open Box Drive's menu-bar / tray icon and confirm you're still signed in.
Installing Box Drive (Box Support)
Making files and folders available offline with Box Drive (Box Support)
Inviting collaborators to a folder (Box Support)
One member creates an empty folder inside their Box account (e.g., ). The folder can live anywhere under .
Right-click the folder in Box Drive (or open it on box.com) → Share . Invite lab members by email with Editor access. Co-owner works too if you want them to be able to re-share.
Each invited member accepts the invite. The folder appears under their Box Drive mount on disk (see "Where the folder lives on disk" below).
Open File Explorer (Windows) or Finder (macOS) and navigate to the shared lab folder under your Box mount.

Right-click the folder. In the context menu, choose Make Available Offline (Windows) or Available Offline (macOS). A green check icon replaces the cloud icon on the folder and on every file inside it.
Wait for Box Drive's menu-bar / tray icon to report "All files up to date" before opening ResearchOS. Large labs can take a while on the first sync.
Open ResearchOS, click Link Folder , and pick that shared folder.

Box Sync is the older client and has been retired. Make sure you're installing Box Drive . Box Drive shows every folder you have access to as a virtual filesystem and downloads files on demand, whereas Box Sync mirrored selected folders to disk. The instructions below assume B...
The folder picker can take up to a minute to open for Box folders. This is normal; Box Drive needs time to enumerate the folder tree. Wait for it rather than clicking again.

Some Box Enterprise admins disable Make Available Offline for compliance reasons. If you right-click the folder and don't see the option, or it's greyed out, ask your IT admin to enable offline access for your account. Without it, ResearchOS will hit empty placeholder files in...

entries.title: Dropbox
headings: Set Smart Sync to Local (every member, every laptop)
Smart Sync isn't on every plan
Or keep all of Dropbox local
bodySnippets: Dropbox's Smart Sync can store files online-only. Set the ResearchOS folder to Local so it stays on disk.
Download the desktop app from dropbox.com/install . You need the desktop client running. The web interface alone won't expose Dropbox as a folder ResearchOS can read.
Default to Online-only. Some Business plans default new shared folders to Online-only . Check the team admin settings if Smart Sync keeps reverting.
Selective Sync vs. Smart Sync. Selective Sync hides the folder entirely from your disk, while Smart Sync keeps a placeholder. You want Smart Sync set to Local , not Selective Sync excluded.

LAN sync conflicts. If two members on the same network edit the same file in quick succession, Dropbox can produce "conflicted copy" files. These appear next to the original. Delete the conflicted copy in Finder / Explorer. ResearchOS only reads the canonical name.
How to use Smart Sync (Dropbox Help)
Share files and folders (Dropbox Help)
One member creates an empty folder inside their Dropbox (e.g., ).
Right-click the folder → Share . Add lab members by email with Can edit permission.
Each invited member accepts the share. The folder appears under their local Dropbox tree on disk.
Open File Explorer (Windows) or Finder (macOS) and navigate to the shared lab folder inside Dropbox.
Right-click the folder → Smart Sync → Local . Every file gets downloaded and the cloud icon disappears.
Wait for Dropbox's menu-bar / tray icon to report "Up to date" before opening ResearchOS.
Open ResearchOS, click Link Folder , and pick that shared folder.

Smart Sync is included on Dropbox Plus, Family, Professional, and Business plans. On the free Basic plan, all files are local by default, so you can skip the Smart Sync step entirely. Just watch your storage quota.

If you're on a paid plan and want every Dropbox folder to stay on disk by default, open the Dropbox menu-bar / tray icon → Preferences -> Sync -> New folder default and set it to Local . Existing folders keep their current setting; only newly-added folders inherit the new defa...

entries.title: Google Drive
headings: Mark the lab folder Available offline (every member, every laptop)
Where the folder lives on disk
Or switch the whole Drive to Mirror
bodySnippets: Install Drive for desktop and mark the lab folder Available offline. That keeps only the lab folder on disk without pulling your whole Drive local.

Download Drive for desktop for Windows or macOS. The browser interface at is not enough. ResearchOS needs the desktop app because that's what creates the local filesystem folder you point ResearchOS at.

ResearchOS reads directly from disk, so anything still in Stream mode breaks. Most labs don't want the whole Drive switched to Mirror (your unrelated personal files, downloads, photos all get pulled local). Instead, mark just the lab folder as available offline.
Drive doesn't carry the "available offline" flag across machines, so every member has to do this on every laptop they use ResearchOS from.
Windows : usually mounted as drive or under .
macOS : under or shown as a volume on the desktop.

Stream mode by accident. If files in the lab folder show a cloud icon or take seconds to open, the folder isn't marked offline yet. Right-click the lab folder and pick Available offline (or switch your whole Drive to Mirror if you prefer that).
"Shared with me" is not synced. A shared folder doesn't appear in your local Drive folder until you Add shortcut to Drive . Without that, ResearchOS has nothing to point at.
Workspace admins sometimes lock Mirror mode off. Check with your IT admin if Drive for desktop only shows the Stream option.
Use files offline with Drive for desktop (Google Help)
Share folders in Google Drive (Google Help)
One member creates an empty folder in their Google Drive (e.g., ).
Right-click the folder → Share . Add lab members by email with Editor access.

Each invited member opens drive.google.com, finds the folder under Shared with me , right-clicks it, and selects Add shortcut to Drive → My Drive . This is what makes the folder show up on disk via Drive for desktop.
On each laptop, confirm in Finder / Explorer that the shared folder is fully downloaded (i.e., no cloud icon next to file names).
Open the lab folder in Finder (macOS) or Explorer (Windows). It shows up wherever Drive mounts (see "Where the folder lives on disk" below).
Right-click the folder. The Drive menu adds an Offline access submenu.
Click Available offline . The folder gets a small green check icon, and Drive starts pulling every file inside it down to disk.
Wait for the green check on every file inside. Cloud icons mean the file isn't local yet. ResearchOS won't be able to read those until they download.
Open ResearchOS, click Link Folder , and pick that shared folder.

If you want all of Drive locally (not just the lab folder), open Drive for desktop's Preferences → My Drive → Mirror files and confirm. Drive will download everything you have in My Drive over the next few minutes or hours depending on size. Pick this if you don't mind the ext...

entries.title: iCloud Drive
headings: Or keep all of iCloud Drive local
bodySnippets: iCloud Drive Optimize Storage offloads unused files. Mark the ResearchOS folder Keep Downloaded so it stays local.
On macOS, open System Settings → Apple ID → iCloud → iCloud Drive . Make sure it's turned on, and that the Desktop & Documents Folders sub-option is enabled if you plan to put the lab folder under .

Optimize Mac Storage (System Settings → Apple ID → iCloud) will re-offload files macOS decides you haven't used recently. Keep Downloaded overrides this per folder, so make sure it's set on the lab folder, not just on individual files.
Permissions on shared collaborations are tracked by Apple ID. If someone's share is revoked, ResearchOS will see empty or unreadable files. Reaccept the share via the email link.
Hidden placeholder files appear if the file is online-only. If you see instead of , the file isn't local. Run Keep Downloaded again.
Use iCloud Drive on your Mac (Apple Support)
Collaborate on folders with iCloud Drive (Apple Support)
One member creates an empty folder inside their iCloud Drive (e.g., ).
Right-click the folder in Finder → Share → Collaborate . Set permissions to Only invited people and Can make changes .
Send the share link to each lab member. They accept it on their Mac, and the folder appears in their iCloud Drive.
In Finder, navigate to the shared lab folder under iCloud Drive.
Right-click the folder and choose Keep Downloaded (it's near the bottom of the context menu). Every file inside downloads and the small cloud-with-arrow icon disappears.
Wait for the Finder sidebar's iCloud status to read "Up to date".
Open ResearchOS, click Link Folder , and pick that shared folder.

iCloud Drive technically runs on Windows via the iCloud for Windows app, but the experience is rough and Apple-specific permission quirks can break ResearchOS's folder access. If your lab has a mix of Mac and Windows users, we recommend OneDrive or Google Drive instead.
The folder picker can take up to a minute to open for iCloud Drive folders. This is normal; iCloud needs time to enumerate the folder tree. Wait for it rather than clicking again.

If you'd rather not have macOS offloading anything in iCloud, open System Settings -> Apple ID -> iCloud and turn off Optimize Mac Storage . Every file in your iCloud Drive stays on disk from then on. Reasonable if you have plenty of disk space and other iCloud folders that al...

entries.title: OneDrive
headings: Make it Always keep on this device (every member, every laptop)
Folder picker may be slow on Windows
Or keep all of OneDrive local
bodySnippets: OneDrive's Files On-Demand keeps files in the cloud by default. You need to mark the ResearchOS folder Always keep on this device.
Windows 10/11 : pre-installed. Sign in via Settings → Accounts.
macOS : download from microsoft.com/onedrive/download .

Storage Sense on Windows can auto-unpin files you haven't opened recently (i.e., move them back to cloud-only). Open Settings → System → Storage → Storage Sense and either disable the "content not opened in N days" option for the ResearchOS folder, or turn Storage Sense off en...
Cloud-only re-tag happens if you right-click and choose Free up space by accident. The folder reverts to cloud-only and ResearchOS can't read it. Re-run Always keep on this device .
Mac users : confirm the option says "Always keep on this device" (Files On-Demand). The older OneDrive Mac client stored files locally by default, but newer versions don't.
OneDrive Files On-Demand (Microsoft Support)
Share OneDrive files and folders (Microsoft Support)
One member creates an empty folder inside their OneDrive (e.g., ).
Right-click the folder → OneDrive → Share . Invite the other lab members by email and give them Edit permission.

Each invited member accepts the share. The folder appears under OneDrive -> Shared in the File Explorer left nav (Windows) or under your OneDrive folder in Finder (macOS). Using Add shortcut to My files is the most reliable way to get the shared folder into your normal OneDriv...
Open File Explorer (Windows) or Finder (macOS) and navigate to the shared lab folder.
Right-click the folder. In the context menu, choose Always keep on this device . A green check icon replaces the cloud icon on the folder and on every file inside it.
Wait until OneDrive finishes downloading everything (status "Up to date" in the menu-bar / tray icon).
Open ResearchOS, click Link Folder , and pick that shared folder.
The folder picker can take up to a minute to open on Windows for OneDrive folders. This is normal; OneDrive needs time to enumerate the folder tree. Wait for it rather than clicking again.

Shared folders sometimes show up as SharePoint libraries or in a separate "Shared with me" section. Microsoft's Add shortcut to My files button is the cleanest way to get the shared folder into your normal OneDrive tree, where Always keep on this device works reliably.

If you'd rather have your whole OneDrive on disk (not just the lab folder), click the OneDrive icon in the menu-bar / tray → Settings -> Sync and backup -> Advanced settings -> Files On-Demand and switch to Download all OneDrive files now . Pick this if you have other OneDrive...

entries.title: Read this first
headings: What ResearchOS is, in one paragraph
A few things worth knowing up front
How to find the rest of the wiki
Setting up a shared lab folder?
1. Your data stays on your machine
2. The “?” button jumps you into this wiki
3. There is no home dashboard, you land where you work
4. Tasks live in projects, projects live in your account
5. PIs get a Lab Overview surface
6. Calendar and the AI Helper are opt-in
bodySnippets: Yes, there are a lot of pages in here. Don't panic. Think of the wiki as a reference you dip into, not a book you read front to back. Come back here whenever you are stuck on something, find the page for it, skim, and get on with your day. This is the one page actually worth r...
It is short. It covers what ResearchOS is, the few things that will save you a headache later, and how to find everything else when you need it.

It is a research project manager that runs entirely in your browser and reads and writes a folder on your own computer. No upload required. You point it at a folder, pick a username, and everything you make (projects, tasks, lab notes, methods, protocols, purchases, attachment...
Get these and you can pick up the rest as you go.
There are a lot of pages because the app does a lot. Three ways to get around, fastest first.

Every page leads with the concept. The top explains what the feature is and why it exists, and the screenshots and steps come after. Usually you can skim to the screenshot of the thing you need and stop there.
Walk these in order. Each one is short.

Read Shared Lab Accounts first. Putting the ResearchOS folder inside OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, Box, or iCloud lets your whole lab work out of one folder. The catch is that you have to set the sync client to keep the folder downloaded locally instead of on-demand. Skip t...

This is a local-first app built by one person, and the wiki tries to be honest about what works and what does not yet. If something is broken or confusing, the Feedback page in the app files a GitHub issue with the context already filled in. It is the fastest way to get it fixed.
Search the bar at the top of the sidebar. If you know the word for the thing you want ("gradient," "calendar," "edit session"), this beats browsing every time.
Browse the sidebar categories. Pages are grouped under Getting Started, Shared Lab Accounts, Features, Integrations, and Security. Good when you know roughly what area you are in.
Hit the "?" button in the app. Drops you on the page for whatever you were just looking at. Best when you are mid-task and want context, not when you are browsing.
Browser requirements . Chrome or Edge. The folder API does not work in Safari, Firefox, or Brave.
Connecting your folder . Pick the folder ResearchOS reads and writes.
Creating a user . A username, and an optional password.
Want a sandbox first? Try the demo . It runs in your browser on a fake yeast lab, and your edits vanish on reload.

Everything you do writes to the folder you connected. Nothing is uploaded, and there is no ResearchOS server holding your research. Want a backup? Copy the folder. Want to leave? Delete it. Deleting inside the app sends records to the Trash (the trash-can icon in the top-right...

It is in the top-right of every page in the app. Click it and you land on the wiki page for whatever you were just looking at. Confused by a colored bar on the Gantt chart? Hit "?" and you are on the Gantt page.

Opening ResearchOS doesn't drop you on a dashboard to glance at. The root URL is just a router that sends you to the surface for your role . A member or solo researcher lands on the Workbench , opened to a grid of their projects, with its Projects, Experiments, Notes, Lists, a...

The shape is account, then projects, then tasks. Sharing is granular, you hand over one record at a time (most often a task, sometimes a method, note, project, or goal) and set read or edit per recipient. When you share a task with a labmate, it shows up in their Workbench und...

If your account is marked as a PI (Principal Investigator), you get Lab Overview , a curated page with cross-member views like member workload, recent activity, the purchase queue, and an audit log. Regular members never see it. Flip the PI flag on your account to get the bird...

ResearchOS works fine on its own. The calendar feeds (subscribe to a Google, Outlook, or iCloud calendar), the AI Helper, and the Companion phone pairing (the phone icon in the top-right) all live behind opt-in switches, off by default, and turn on independently. Settings sits...

entries.title: Reading your statistics
bodySnippets: When the Data Hub runs a test, it hands back numbers and a one-line plain-language verdict. These pages explain what those numbers mean in the language a working scientist actually uses, so you can read your own result, write it up honestly, and know when to trust it. You do n...

Almost every result in the Data Hub reports a p-value , a number that tells you how surprising your data would be if nothing were really going on. A small p-value is a useful flag, but it does not tell you how big an effect is or whether it matters. For that you need the effec...

These tests answer "are these groups really different, and by how much?" Reach for them when you have a measured outcome (a fluorescence reading, a growth rate, a concentration) split across two or more conditions.
These answer "do these two things move together, and can I predict one from the other?" Reach for them when both of your variables are measured numbers rather than groups.

Some biology is not a straight line. A drug saturates, a binding curve plateaus, an enzyme runs out of substrate. These pages cover fitting a shaped curve and reading the parameters that summarize it.

When your outcome is "how long until something happens," and some subjects have not had it happen yet, you need methods built for that. Reach for these for time to relapse, time to death, or time to any defined event.
When your data are counts in categories (responded or did not, mutant or wild type) rather than measured numbers, you compare proportions instead of means.
Two practical tools that sit alongside the rest, one for judging how well a measurement separates two groups, one for the honest question of whether a stray point is really an error.

Before you run a test, the power and sample-size planner lets you work out three questions in any direction: given an effect size, alpha, and a target power, how large does your sample need to be? Given the sample you already have, what power do you achieve? Given your sample ...

After you have data, the assumption Report Card runs the checks your chosen test relies on and surfaces them in plain language. For parametric tests it runs a Shapiro-Wilk normality test on each group and a Levene or Brown-Forsythe equal-variance test across groups, and tells ...
Effect sizes and confidence intervals , why the size of a difference matters more than a bare p-value, and how to read a 95% confidence interval.
ANOVA, post-hoc tests, and two-way ANOVA , comparing three or more groups at once, then finding which specific pairs differ.
Repeated measures, mixed models, and nested designs , for when the same subjects are measured more than once, or your replicates are cells within mice rather than independent samples.
Correlation and regression , measuring how tightly two variables track each other, and fitting a line you can read a slope off of.
Dose-response curves , covering EC50 and IC50, the 4PL and 5PL sigmoid fits, the Hill slope, and comparing or sharing fits across datasets.
Survival curves and hazard ratios , covering Kaplan-Meier curves, the log-rank test, and the Cox hazard ratio.
Contingency tables, odds ratios, and relative risk , covering chi-square and Fisher exact tests, plus the odds ratio that logistic regression also reports.
ROC curves and AUC , how well a continuous measurement tells two groups apart, with sensitivity, specificity, and a cut point.
Outlier tests , the Grubbs test, and the honest caution that removing a data point needs a real reason.

Each page starts with what the test is and when a lab actually reaches for it, then shows you how to read the exact fields the Data Hub reports, with a worked example that turns the numbers into a sentence. Start with effect sizes and confidence intervals if you read only one....

Every test in these pages is validated against the tools labs already trust (scipy, statsmodels, lifelines, scikit-learn, and R) on the transparency page , which reruns the same inputs through those packages and shows the numbers line up. The result you read in the Data Hub is...

entries.title: ANOVA, post-hoc tests, and two-way ANOVA
headings: When a lab reaches for it
Two-way ANOVA: two factors at once
Read the post-hoc intervals, not just the stars
bodySnippets: A t-test compares two groups. The moment you have three or more, you need ANOVA, which asks whether any of the groups differ before you go hunting for which ones. This page covers the one-way ANOVA, the post-hoc tests that find the specific pairs that differ, the two-way ANOVA...

ANOVA stands for analysis of variance. Despite the name, the question it answers is about means. Given three or more groups, it asks "is at least one of these group means different from the rest, more than chance alone would produce?" It does this by comparing how much the gro...

Why not just run a t-test on every pair? Because each test carries a chance of a false positive, and running many of them stacks those chances up until a "significant" result is likely somewhere by luck alone. ANOVA asks the single overall question once, with one controlled er...

You have a dose series (vehicle, low, medium, high) and one readout, and you want to know whether dose changes the readout at all. Or you have cells from four genotypes and a single growth measurement. Any time the design is "one measured outcome split across three or more con...

The Data Hub reports an F statistic , its degrees of freedom , and an overall p-value . The F statistic is the ratio of between-group spread to within-group spread, so a large F with a small p-value means the groups really do differ somewhere. A non-significant ANOVA is a stop...

The F statistic and its p-value tell you whether the groups differ. They do not tell you by how much in a scale you can reason about. For that, the Data Hub reports two effect sizes. Eta-squared is the fraction of total variation the grouping factor explains, computed as the b...

A significant ANOVA tells you that some group differs, not which . The post-hoc tests answer that, by comparing each pair of groups while correcting for the fact that you are making many comparisons at once. The Data Hub reports, for each pair, the difference in means , its 95...

The Data Hub offers five post-hoc families. Tukey HSD (the default) compares every group to every other using the studentized range, and is the standard choice when you have no pre-specified contrasts. Dunnett compares each treatment to a single named control, which is the rig...

A two-way ANOVA is for when two things vary together. Say you cross genotype (wild type vs mutant) with treatment (drug vs vehicle). Now there are three questions, and the two-way ANOVA answers all three.

The result reports an F statistic and p-value for each of the three rows. Read the interaction first. If it is significant, the two main effects are no longer the whole story and you interpret each combination on its own, often with post-hoc comparisons of the specific cells.

When the normality assumption is in doubt or your data are ranks or scores rather than measured quantities, the Kruskal-Wallis test is the nonparametric replacement for the one-way ANOVA. Instead of working on the raw values, it ranks all observations pooled across groups and ...

The Friedman test is the nonparametric counterpart of a one-way repeated-measures ANOVA. It is the right choice when the same subjects are measured under each condition but you cannot assume normality. Within each subject (block), observations are ranked across conditions; the...

Three media give colony counts averaging 50, 65, and 80. The one-way ANOVA returns F = 14.2, p = 0.0003, so the media differ somewhere. The Tukey post-hoc shows medium vs rich differs by 15 colonies (95% CI 6 to 24, adjusted p = 0.002) while minimal vs medium does not clear th...
ResearchOS validates one-way and two-way ANOVA, the post-hoc families, Kruskal-Wallis, and Friedman against scipy, statsmodels, and R on the transparency page .
The main effect of genotype : averaging over treatment, do the genotypes differ?
The main effect of treatment : averaging over genotype, does the drug do anything?

The interaction : does the drug's effect depend on the genotype? This is usually the interesting one. A significant interaction means you cannot describe the drug with a single number, its effect is different in the mutant than in the wild type.

A pair can clear significance with a difference that is still tiny. As on the effect sizes page, the confidence interval on each pairwise difference tells you the size and the precision, which is what you actually report.

If the same subjects are measured under every condition, or your replicates are not independent, you want a repeated-measures or nested design instead, which accounts for that structure rather than treating every point as a fresh independent sample.

The ANOVA table breaks the variation into between-group and within-group parts, and F is the ratio of the two. Eta-squared and omega-squared below it say how much of the spread the grouping explains, and the Multiple comparisons tab holds the post-hoc pairs.

A one-way ANOVA result in the Data Hub comparing four groups, with the source-of-variation table (between groups, within groups, total) reporting SS, DF, MS, F, and the p-value, plus eta-squared and omega-squared effect sizes, and a Multiple comparisons tab.

entries.title: Contingency tables, odds ratios, and relative risk
headings: Relative risk, and when to use which
Logistic regression is a first-class analysis
bodySnippets: When your data are counts in categories rather than measured numbers, responded or did not, mutant or wild type, survived or died, you compare proportions. A contingency table lays those counts out, and a handful of tests and effect sizes tell you whether the categories are li...

A contingency table is a grid of counts. It can be any size: two outcomes by two groups (2x2), three treatment arms by four response categories (3x4), or any R-by-C arrangement. The question is always the same: does the distribution of counts across columns depend on which row...

For any R×C table the Data Hub runs the Pearson chi-square test of independence . It compares the counts you observed against the counts you would expect if the rows and columns were independent. The test is reliable when the expected count in every cell is reasonably la...
For a 2x2 table , the Data Hub always reports all three of the following, regardless of cell counts.
All three report a p-value for whether the categories are associated. As elsewhere, that tells you whether there is a link, not how strong it is. For strength, you want the effect sizes below.

The odds ratio is the workhorse effect size for two-by-two data. Odds are a count ratio, the number with the outcome divided by the number without it. The odds ratio compares the odds in one group against the odds in the other.

The Data Hub reports the odds ratio with its 95% confidence interval . If the interval excludes 1, the association is statistically clear, the same call the test's p-value makes, and the interval's width tells you the precision.

Relative risk compares the actual probability of the outcome between groups, not the odds. A relative risk of 2 means the outcome was twice as likely in one group. It is often the more intuitive number, and it is the right one when you sampled groups and then watched for outco...

Of 50 treated patients, 10 relapsed; of 50 controls, 25 relapsed. Fisher's exact test gives p = 0.002. The odds ratio is 0.27 (95% CI 0.11 to 0.64) and the relative risk is 0.40. You would write "relapse was less frequent on treatment, 20% versus 50% (relative risk 0.40, odds ...

ResearchOS validates the chi-square, Yates-corrected chi-square, Fisher exact test, odds ratio, relative risk, and logistic regression (including the Firth fallback) against scipy and statsmodels on the transparency page .
Chi-square (Pearson, uncorrected), the standard large-sample statistic.
Chi-square with Yates continuity correction , which subtracts 0.5 from each absolute deviation before squaring, giving a slightly more conservative result for the 2x2 case.

Fisher's exact test , which computes the probability directly from the hypergeometric distribution rather than approximating, so it stays accurate when counts are small. Fisher exact is only computed for 2x2 tables; for larger tables the chi-square is the right test.
An odds ratio of 1 means the outcome is equally likely in both groups, no association.
Above 1 means higher odds in the first group. An odds ratio of 3 means the exposed group had three times the odds of the outcome.
Below 1 means lower odds, a protective association.

Logistic regression is its own analysis in the Data Hub, not just an extension of the contingency table. It predicts a yes/no outcome from a continuous predictor and reports the odds ratio for that predictor, read exactly as above, with its 95% confidence interval and p-value....

A 2x2 table. The Data Hub reports the chi-square (Yates-corrected and uncorrected), Fisher's exact p for small counts, the relative risk, and the odds ratio with its 95 percent confidence interval, then shows the observed counts against the counts you would expect if the two f...

A 2x2 contingency result in the Data Hub, reporting the Yates-corrected and uncorrected chi-square, Fisher's exact p-value, the relative risk, and the odds ratio with its interval, above the observed and expected count tables.

entries.title: Correlation and regression
headings: Fitting a line with simple linear regression
Multiple regression: several predictors at once
Correlation is not causation, and r is only for straight lines
Watch for predictors that move together
bodySnippets: When both of your variables are measured numbers, you usually want to know whether they move together and whether you can predict one from the other. Correlation measures how tightly two things track. Regression fits an actual line you can read a slope off of and use to predic...

Correlation measures how consistently two variables rise and fall together. The common measure is Pearson's r , which runs from -1 to +1. An r near +1 means that when one goes up the other reliably goes up; near -1 means one goes up as the other goes down; near 0 means no stra...

The Data Hub reports r , its 95% confidence interval , a p-value for whether the correlation differs from zero, and often r squared , the fraction of the variation in one variable that tracks with the other. An r of 0.7 gives an r squared of about 0.49, so roughly half the var...

When your data are ranks, scores on an ordinal scale, or visibly non-normal, Spearman's rho is the nonparametric alternative. It works exactly like Pearson's r, but on the rank-transformed data rather than the raw values. The result is a coefficient that runs from -1 to +1 wit...

Where correlation gives a single number for tightness, linear regression fits the actual line and hands you its equation. It is the right tool when one variable plausibly drives the other and you want to predict or quantify the relationship, the absorbance you expect at a give...

The result reports the slope with its confidence interval and p-value , the intercept , and r squared for how well the line fits. The slope is the headline, it is how much the outcome changes for each one-unit change in the predictor, in real units. A slope whose confidence in...

You plot fluorescence against protein concentration and fit a line. The slope is 1,250 units per microgram (95% CI 1,180 to 1,320, p 0.0001), with r squared = 0.98. You would write "fluorescence rose 1,250 units per microgram of protein (95% CI 1,180 to 1,320, p 0.0001), and t...

Multiple regression predicts one outcome from two or more predictors together. Its real value is that each predictor's effect is estimated while holding the others constant . If yield depends on both temperature and pH, multiple regression tells you the effect of temperature a...

The result reports, for each predictor, a coefficient with its confidence interval and p-value , plus an overall r squared and a model-level p-value. Read each coefficient as "the change in the outcome per unit of this predictor, with the other predictors held fixed." A predic...

The multiple-regression result includes a VIF (variance inflation factor) for each predictor. The VIF for a given predictor is computed by regressing that predictor on all the other predictors and taking 1 / (1 minus that r-squared). A VIF of 1 means the predictor is completel...
ResearchOS validates Pearson and Spearman correlation, simple regression, and multiple regression (including VIF) against scipy and statsmodels on the transparency page .

A strong r says two things track together, not that one causes the other, a lurking third variable can drive both. And Pearson's r only sees straight-line relationships. A perfect U-shaped curve can have an r near zero. Always look at the scatter plot, not just the number.

When two predictors are themselves strongly correlated (temperature and pressure that always rise together in your setup), the model struggles to credit the effect to one or the other, and their individual coefficients get wide, unstable intervals. The fix is more varied data ...

If your outcome is a yes/no category rather than a number, you want logistic regression, a first-class analysis in the Data Hub. It predicts a binary outcome from a continuous predictor and reports an odds ratio with its interval. See the contingency page for how to read that ...

The slope is the headline, the change in the outcome for each one-unit change in the predictor, in real units. It comes with its standard error and 95 percent confidence interval, and the r-squared says how much of the variation the line accounts for.
A simple linear regression result in the Data Hub, reporting the slope with its standard error and 95 percent confidence interval, the intercept with its interval, and the r-squared.

Each predictor gets its own coefficient, read as the change in the outcome per unit while the other predictors are held fixed. The standardized beta puts the slopes on a common scale, and the VIF column flags predictors that move together, where a value above about 5 to 10 mea...

A multiple regression result in the Data Hub, with a coefficient table reporting the estimate, standard error, t, p-value, 95 percent confidence interval, standardized beta, and VIF for each predictor, plus overall r-squared, adjusted r-squared, and the model F test.

entries.title: Dose-response curves
headings: EC50 and IC50, the halfway dose
The Hill slope and the models
Global fits: sharing parameters across datasets
Report the EC50 with its interval, on the log scale
bodySnippets: A lot of biology is a saturating S-curve, not a straight line. A drug has little effect at low doses, a steep response through the middle, and a plateau once the system is maxed out. A dose-response fit captures that shape and boils it down to a few numbers you can compare acr...

You plot response against dose, almost always with dose on a log scale , and fit a smooth S-shaped curve through the points. The curve summarizes the whole experiment with a handful of parameters, the bottom plateau, the top plateau, the dose at the halfway point, and how stee...

The single most-used number is the dose that produces a half-maximal response. When the response goes up with dose, that is the EC50 (half-maximal effective concentration). When the response goes down, an inhibition, it is the IC50 (half-maximal inhibitory concentration). A lo...

The Hill slope describes how steep the climb is through the middle of the curve. A slope near 1 is the standard simple case; a steeper slope means the response switches on over a narrow dose range, which can hint at cooperative binding. The Data Hub reports it as a fitted para...

The shape comes from a model. The 4PL (four-parameter logistic) fits the bottom plateau, the top plateau, the EC50, and the Hill slope, and is the default for a symmetric S-curve. The 5PL adds a fifth parameter for asymmetry, for curves that approach their two plateaus at diff...

Should you use the simpler 4PL or the more flexible 5PL? A more complex model will always fit your particular points at least slightly better, just by having an extra knob to turn, so a bare improvement in fit is not a good reason to prefer it. Model comparison asks the sharpe...
The Data Hub runs two distinct comparisons , both reported together.
The honest default is the simpler model unless the data clearly call for more. Read both verdicts, as they can sometimes point in different directions when the sample is small.

Often several curves should share something. Three compounds tested the same day plausibly share the same top and bottom plateau, they differ only in potency. A global fit fits all the curves at once with some parameters shared and others left free per curve. Any of the four 4...

The result reports the shared parameters once, the free parameters per dataset, and the confidence intervals throughout. The headline is usually the ratio of EC50s between curves, with its interval, which answers "is compound A genuinely more potent than compound B?"

You fit an inhibition curve and get an IC50 of 120 nM (95% CI 95 to 150), a Hill slope of 1.1, and r squared = 0.99. A second compound gives an IC50 of 480 nM (95% CI 410 to 560). The intervals do not overlap, so you would write "compound A was about four-fold more potent than...
ResearchOS validates the 4PL and 5PL fits, the EC50 and IC50 estimates, and the model comparisons against scipy and R on the transparency page .

The extra-sum-of-squares F test applies when the two models are nested, that is, when the simpler model is a special case of the more complex one (for example, the 4PL is the 5PL with the asymmetry parameter fixed at 1). It asks whether the reduction in residual sum of squares...

The AICc (small-sample-corrected Akaike Information Criterion) is always valid, whether or not the models are nested. It trades goodness of fit against parameter count without a null-hypothesis test. The model with the lower AICc is preferred; the Data Hub reports the delta an...

Because dose is fitted on a log scale, the EC50's uncertainty is not symmetric in plain units. The Data Hub reports its confidence interval, and that interval is what tells you whether two compounds genuinely differ in potency, as on the effect sizes page.

The 4PL fit reports every parameter with its own confidence interval, the EC50 (the half-maximal dose), the Hill slope, and the two plateaus, plus an r-squared for how closely the curve tracks the points. BeakerBot reads the EC50 back in the dose units you entered.

A four-parameter logistic dose-response fit in the Data Hub, reporting EC50/IC50 with its 95 percent confidence interval, the Hill slope, the top and bottom plateaus each with an interval, and an r-squared goodness-of-fit.

entries.title: Effect sizes and confidence intervals
headings: How to read it in the Data Hub
A quick read on the interval
bodySnippets: A p-value tells you whether a difference is likely to be real. It does not tell you how big that difference is, or whether it is big enough to care about. The effect size answers the size question, and the confidence interval tells you how precisely you have pinned it down. Th...

A p-value is the probability of seeing a difference at least as large as yours, if the groups were really identical and only chance produced the gap. A small p-value (by long convention, below 0.05) means "this would be a surprising amount of noise," so you treat the differenc...

The catch is that the p-value mixes together two things you care about separately, how big the effect is and how much data you collected. With a large enough sample, a difference far too small to matter biologically can still earn a tiny p-value. With a small sample, a real an...
An effect size is the answer to "how big is the difference?" in units you can reason about. There are two flavors you will meet.

Your effect size is an estimate from one sample. Run the experiment again and you would get a slightly different number. A 95% confidence interval is the range that captures that wobble. Read it as "the true effect is plausibly anywhere in this range, and our single best guess...

When you run a two-group comparison (a t-test), the result reports the mean of each group , the difference between the means with its 95% confidence interval , the p-value , and Cohen's d as the standardized effect size. The plain-language verdict above the table already state...

The bootstrap interval and Hedges' g sit just below. Hedges' g is Cohen's d with a small-sample correction, so on the handful of replicates a typical experiment collects it is the more honest standardized number.

The bootstrap confidence interval is a second, assumption-light estimate of the uncertainty on the mean difference. It works by resampling your data thousands of times with replacement, computing the difference of means on each resample, and reading the 2.5th and 97.5th percen...

The two-sample t-test comes in two variants. The default is Welch's t-test , which does not assume the two groups have the same variance and adjusts the degrees of freedom using the Welch-Satterthwaite equation. The optional Student's t-test pools the variance estimate across ...

If you already have group means, standard deviations, and sample sizes from a published table rather than raw replicates, the Data Hub can run the t-test and ANOVA from those entered summaries, reproducing the omnibus F and the effect sizes without needing the original data. P...

Suppose a treated group averages 42 units and a control averages 30, a difference of 12 units with a 95% confidence interval of 7 to 17, a p-value of 0.001, and a Cohen's d of 1.3. You would write that as "the treatment raised the readout by 12 units (95% CI 7 to 17, p = 0.001...

ResearchOS validates the Welch and Student t-tests, Cohen's d, Hedges' g, the bootstrap CI, and the from-summary-stats path against scipy and statsmodels on the transparency page , which reruns the same inputs through those packages so you can see the numbers match.

A raw effect size is the difference in the units you measured. Two micromolar, fifteen percent more colonies, a 0.4 hour shorter doubling time. This is usually the most honest thing to report, because anyone in your field can judge whether it matters.

A standardized effect size rescales the difference by how spread out the data are, so it has no units. The common one for two groups is Cohen's d , which measures the gap between two means in units of standard deviation. A d of 0.2 is small, 0.5 is medium, and 0.8 or more is l...

For a difference between two groups, look at whether the 95% interval includes zero. If it does not, the difference is statistically clear, the same call the p-value makes. But the interval tells you more, it shows the smallest and largest difference still consistent with your...

Every other test reports its own version of this trio. A correlation has an r and its interval , a survival analysis has a hazard ratio and its interval , a contingency table has an odds ratio and its interval . Once you can read a confidence interval here, you can read all of...

A two-group comparison. BeakerBot states the direction and whether the gap is clear above the table, and the table reports the difference of means with its 95 percent confidence interval alongside Cohen's d and its interval, so you read the size and the precision together.

A two-group t-test result in the Data Hub, showing the mean of each group, the difference of means with its 95 percent confidence interval, the p-value, Cohen's d with its own interval, and Hedges' g.

entries.title: Outlier tests
headings: When a lab reaches for it
The honest rule for removing data
A flag is not a license to delete
bodySnippets: Now and then a data point sits far from the rest and you wonder whether it is a genuine result or a mistake, a mispipette, a bubble, a transcription slip. An outlier test gives you a principled way to flag a point as statistically extreme. But flagging is the easy part. The ho...

An outlier test asks whether the most extreme point in your sample is farther from the others than you would expect from the normal spread of the data. The common one is the Grubbs test , which takes the single most extreme value, measures how many standard deviations it sits ...

You run an assay in triplicate and one well reads wildly off from the other two. Before you average, you want a defensible way to ask whether that well is an aberration rather than real biology. The Grubbs test is built for exactly this, a single suspected outlier in an otherw...

The Data Hub reports, for each pass of the sweep, the suspected value , the Grubbs G statistic (how many standard deviations it lies from the mean of the remaining sample), the critical G for the sample size and alpha, and whether the value is flagged. The engine runs iterativ...

The defensible reason to drop a point is a documented problem with that measurement, not its value. A note that the well had a visible bubble, an instrument error logged at that timestamp, a sample you know was compromised. That reason should exist independently of how the num...

Triplicate readings come back as 4.9, 5.1, and 9.8. Grubbs flags 9.8 (G = 1.15, p = 0.03). On its own that is not permission to delete it. You check the run log, find that well was flagged for a pipetting error, and on that documented basis you exclude it, reporting "one of th...
ResearchOS validates the Grubbs test against scipy and R on the transparency page .

Statistical extremeness tells you a point is unusual. It cannot tell you why . Removing a point because it is inconvenient, with no independent reason, is how real effects get erased and how noise gets polished into a false result. A flagged point is a prompt to investigate, n...

If your spread is wide because the data are genuinely variable rather than because of an error, the better path is to report the variation honestly with a confidence interval , which already accounts for how noisy the measurement is.

Grubbs runs an iterative sweep by default. The table shows each pass: the most extreme value in that pass, its G statistic, and whether G exceeded the Bonferroni-corrected critical value. The sweep stops when a pass clears or the sample is too small to test.

A Grubbs outlier result in the Data Hub, with a table reporting the most extreme value, its Grubbs G statistic, the critical G for the sample size and alpha, and whether the value is flagged as an outlier.

entries.title: Repeated measures, mixed models, and nested designs
headings: Nested designs and the replicate trap
Technical replicates are not your sample size
bodySnippets: Ordinary ANOVA assumes every data point is an independent sample. Real experiments often break that assumption. You measure the same animal before and after, or you read three wells from the same dish, or you count cells within mice within litters. These designs share structur...

Independence is the quiet assumption behind most simple tests. Two measurements are independent when knowing one tells you nothing about the other. The moment they share a source, a subject, a dish, an animal, they are correlated, and treating them as independent makes your sa...

Use a repeated-measures ANOVA when the same subjects are measured under every condition. A classic case is a crossover, the same ten patients receive drug and placebo in turn, or the same cell line is read at four timepoints. Because each subject acts as its own control, the a...

The result reports an F statistic and p-value for the within-subject factor, plus a partial eta-squared effect size. Partial eta-squared is the fraction of the within-subject variance that the condition factor explains, computed as the condition sum of squares divided by the s...

Repeated-measures ANOVA relies on an assumption called sphericity : the variances of the differences between every pair of conditions should be equal. When you have only two conditions the assumption is automatically met. With three or more, it can fail, and when it does the s...

The Data Hub reports two corrected p-values alongside the standard one. The Greenhouse-Geisser correction adjusts the degrees of freedom by a factor epsilon (between 1/(k minus 1) and 1) estimated from the covariance matrix of the conditions. The smaller epsilon is, the worse ...

A mixed model is the flexible generalization. The name comes from mixing two kinds of effect. A fixed effect is the thing you care about and chose deliberately, the drug, the genotype, the dose. A random effect is a source of variation you are sampling from rather than studyin...

Mixed models earn their keep when the design is unbalanced or has gaps, a patient missed a visit, a well failed. Repeated-measures ANOVA gets awkward with missing cells; a mixed model handles them gracefully because it works from the data you have rather than requiring a perfe...

This is the one that quietly invalidates a lot of published work, so it is worth being plain about. A nested design is when your observations sit inside a hierarchy. You image 30 cells, but those cells come from 3 mice, 10 cells each.

The honest analysis respects the nesting. Either you summarize each animal to one number and compare animals, or you use a nested t-test or nested ANOVA (a mixed model with animal as a random effect) that pools the cell-level data while still counting animals as the unit of re...

The nested result reports the effect estimated at the group level (the difference between conditions across animals), its confidence interval , and a p-value based on the number of animals. The within-animal spread is used to weight the estimate, not to inflate the sample size.

You treat 4 mice with a drug and 4 with vehicle, imaging 25 cells per mouse. A naive t-test on all 200 cells gives p 0.0001, which looks spectacular and is wrong. The nested analysis, counting mice as the unit, returns a mean difference of 8% (95% CI -2 to 18, p = 0.10). You w...
ResearchOS validates repeated-measures ANOVA (including sphericity corrections), the mixed-model fits, and the nested tests against statsmodels, pingouin, and R on the transparency page .

If normality is in doubt, the Friedman test is the nonparametric counterpart of the repeated-measures ANOVA. It ranks observations within each subject rather than working on raw values, and is covered on the ANOVA page .

Counting 30 cells as n = 30 is the mistake. The cells within one mouse are technical replicates , they tell you about that one mouse measured precisely, not about mice in general. Your real independent unit, the biological replicate , is the mouse. Your n is 3, not 30. Treatin...

If every measurement really is independent and you just have three or more groups, the plain one-way ANOVA is the right tool. If you have two groups, see effect sizes for the t-test. These structured designs are for when independence does not hold.

Three timepoints measured on the same subjects. The table splits the variation into the condition effect, the subject-to-subject differences, and the leftover error, and F is tested against that within-subject error. Partial eta-squared below it captures how much of the within...

A repeated-measures ANOVA result in the Data Hub across three within-subject timepoints, with an ANOVA table splitting variation into conditions, subjects, and error, plus partial eta-squared and the Greenhouse-Geisser and Huynh-Feldt epsilon corrections.

entries.title: ROC curves and AUC
headings: The problem a ROC curve solves
The best cut point depends on the cost of each mistake
bodySnippets: Suppose you have a continuous measurement, a biomarker level, an assay readout, and you want to use it to tell two groups apart, diseased from healthy, responder from non-responder. A ROC curve shows how well that single measurement separates the two groups across every possib...

A continuous marker rarely separates two groups perfectly, the distributions overlap. Wherever you set a threshold to call a sample positive, you make two kinds of mistake, and they trade off. Set the threshold low and you catch almost every true positive but also flag many ne...
The two axes are the two things you are trading.

Each point on the ROC curve is one possible threshold, plotting the sensitivity and false-positive rate you would get if you drew the line there. A curve that bows up toward the top-left corner is a good separator, it is achieving high sensitivity without paying much in false ...

The AUC is the area under the ROC curve, a single number from 0.5 to 1. An AUC of 0.5 means the marker has no discriminating power, the diagonal. An AUC of 1.0 is perfect separation. A useful plain reading, the AUC is the probability that a randomly chosen positive scores high...

The Data Hub reports the AUC with its standard error (Hanley and McNeil 1982 closed form, the same formula GraphPad Prism and pROC use) and its 95% confidence interval . It also reports the number of positives and negatives in the dataset, which tells you whether the confidenc...

The ROC curve shows every threshold, but eventually you have to pick one to actually use. A common, balanced choice is the Youden cut point , the threshold that maximizes sensitivity plus specificity minus one, which is the point on the curve sitting farthest above the diagona...

A blood marker separating responders from non-responders gives an AUC of 0.82 (95% CI 0.74 to 0.90). The Youden cut point sits at 3.1 ng/mL, where sensitivity is 0.78 and specificity is 0.80. You would write "the marker discriminated responders from non-responders with an AUC ...
ResearchOS validates the ROC curve, the AUC and its interval, and the cut point against scikit-learn and R's pROC package on the transparency page .
Sensitivity is the fraction of true positives the test catches. High sensitivity means few missed cases. It is on the vertical axis.
Specificity is the fraction of true negatives the test correctly clears. High specificity means few false alarms. The horizontal axis is one minus specificity, the false-positive rate.

The Youden point treats a missed case and a false alarm as equally bad. Often they are not. A cheap confirmatory test downstream argues for high sensitivity even at the cost of false positives; an invasive or costly follow-up argues for high specificity. Use the Youden point a...

The AUC comes with an accuracy band and its 95 percent confidence interval, and the Youden cut point reports the sensitivity and specificity you would get at that threshold. The table below is the curve itself, the true-positive rate against the false-positive rate at every cut.

A ROC and AUC result in the Data Hub, reporting the AUC with an accuracy band and 95 percent confidence interval, the Youden optimal threshold with its sensitivity and specificity, and a table of the false-positive and true-positive rate at each cut.

entries.title: Survival curves and hazard ratios
headings: Hazard ratios and the Cox model
The log-rank says whether, not how much
bodySnippets: When your outcome is how long until something happens, time to relapse, time to death, time to a tumor reaching a size, you need methods built for time-to-event data. The reason ordinary tests fall short is that some subjects have not had the event yet when the study ends, and...

The defining feature is censoring . A patient who is still alive at the last follow-up has not had the event, but you do not know when, or if, they will. That is real information, they survived at least this long, and survival methods use it rather than throwing it away. Avera...
